Core Reading References Used in These Answers
Pedrotti, Pedrotti & Pedrotti - Introduction to Optics (3rd ed.) Feynman, Leighton & Sands - Lectures on Physics Vol. I–III Hecht - Optics (7th ed.) Feynman - QED: The Strange Theory of Light and Matter
Topic 1

Optical Spectra of Atoms, Molecules and Solids

Q1 State the four main postulates of the Bohr model of the hydrogen atom.
4 marksShow Answer ▾

(1) Quantised stationary orbits: Electrons orbit the nucleus only in certain discrete "allowed" circular orbits. While in these orbits they do not radiate, despite undergoing centripetal acceleration - a deliberate departure from classical electrodynamics, which predicts that any accelerating charge must radiate.

(2) Energy of radiation: When an electron transitions between two stationary states EiE_iEi​ and EfE_fEf​, a single photon is emitted or absorbed with energy exactly equal to the energy difference: hν=∣Ef−Ei∣h\nu = |E_f - E_i|hν=∣Ef​−Ei​∣ This embeds the photoelectric notion of a photon directly into atomic structure.

(3) Quantisation of angular momentum: The allowed orbits are those for which the electron's orbital angular momentum is an integer multiple of ℏ\hbarℏ: L=nℏ,n=1,2,3,…L = n\hbar, \quad n = 1, 2, 3, \ldotsL=nℏ,n=1,2,3,… Equivalently (de Broglie's later interpretation), the orbit circumference must fit exactly nnn de Broglie wavelengths: 2πr=nλdB2\pi r = n\lambda_{dB}2πr=nλdB​.

(4) Classical mechanical dynamics: Within each allowed orbit, the electron obeys classical mechanics - the Coulomb attraction provides the centripetal force e24πϵ0r2=mev2r\frac{e^2}{4\pi\epsilon_0 r^2} = \frac{m_e v^2}{r}4πϵ0​r2e2​=rme​v2​. This gives the orbital radius and hence the quantised energy levels En=−13.6 eVn2E_n = -\frac{13.6\,\text{eV}}{n^2}En​=−n213.6eV​.

Feynman perspective: The Bohr model is internally inconsistent - it uses classical mechanics while forbidding classical radiation. Its success (predicting hydrogen spectral lines to remarkable precision) is now understood as emerging from the true quantum mechanics of wave functions. The quantisation condition L=nℏL=n\hbarL=nℏ anticipates that electrons are standing de Broglie waves around the nucleus.
Q2 Calculate the wavelength of light emitted when a hydrogen atom transitions from n=3n=3n=3 to n=2n=2n=2 (the Hα\alphaα line). Use En=−13.6/n2E_n = -13.6/n^2En​=−13.6/n2 eV.
3 marksShow Answer ▾
1Find energy levels: E3=−13.69=−1.511 eVE_3 = -\frac{13.6}{9} = -1.511\,\text{eV}E3​=−913.6​=−1.511eV, \quad E2=−13.64=−3.400 eVE_2 = -\frac{13.6}{4} = -3.400\,\text{eV}E2​=−413.6​=−3.400eV
2Energy of emitted photon: ΔE=∣E2−E3∣=3.400−1.511=1.889 eV=1.889�-1.6�-10−19=3.02�-10−19 J\Delta E = |E_2 - E_3| = 3.400 - 1.511 = 1.889\,\text{eV} = 1.889 \times 1.6\times10^{-19} = 3.02\times10^{-19}\,\text{J}ΔE=∣E2​−E3​∣=3.400−1.511=1.889eV=1.889�-1.6�-10−19=3.02�-10−19J
3Wavelength: λ=hcΔE=6.63�-10−34�-3�-1083.02�-10−19\lambda = \frac{hc}{\Delta E} = \frac{6.63\times10^{-34} \times 3\times10^8}{3.02\times10^{-19}}λ=ΔEhc​=3.02�-10−196.63�-10−34�-3�-108​ =656 nm= \boxed{656\,\text{nm}}=656nm​

This is the Hα\alphaα line - the red line in the Balmer series, visible to the naked eye. It is one of the most significant spectral lines in astrophysics: its Doppler shift reveals the recession velocity of distant galaxies and underpins Hubble's law.

Q3 Explain why gas lasers (e.g. He-Ne) produce narrow spectral lines while semiconductor lasers produce broad emission spectra.
3 marksShow Answer ▾

Gas lasers - narrow lines: In a dilute gas, atoms are widely separated and interact negligibly. Each atom retains its discrete, well-defined electronic energy levels from atomic quantum mechanics. Transitions between these sharp levels emit photons at a single, precise frequency. The only broadening mechanisms are Doppler shifts from thermal motion (inhomogeneous) and the natural linewidth from the uncertainty principle (homogeneous) - both very narrow, typically <1 GHz.

Semiconductor lasers - broad emission: In a crystalline solid, atoms are packed so closely (~1028 m−310^{28}\,\text{m}^{-3}1028m−3) that their outer electron wavefunctions strongly overlap. By the Pauli exclusion principle and Bloch's theorem, the discrete atomic energy levels hybridise and broaden into continuous energy bands. Electrons occupy the valence band; the conduction band lies above a gap EgE_gEg​. An electron can recombine with a hole at any kkk-vector within the band, emitting a photon of energy anywhere from EgE_gEg​ to Eg+ΔEbandE_g + \Delta E_{\text{band}}Eg​+ΔEband​. The gain bandwidth is therefore ∼50\sim 50∼50–100 nm100\,\text{nm}100nm, orders of magnitude broader than a gas transition.

Additional broadening in semiconductors: Molecular vibrations (phonons) couple to electronic transitions, providing additional dephasing. Each electronic level is further broadened by phonon-induced uncertainty in energy (T2T_2T2​ dephasing), pushing the emission even broader. This broadness is actually exploited: semiconductor optical amplifiers (SOAs) can amplify many WDM channels simultaneously.
Q4 What is a phonon? Explain why phonon-mediated de-excitation pathways are important in solid-state lasers.
3 marksShow Answer ▾

Phonon defined: A phonon is the quantum of vibrational energy in a crystal lattice - the quantised normal mode of collective atomic oscillation. By analogy with the photon (quantum of the electromagnetic field), the phonon carries energy ℏωphonon\hbar\omega_{\text{phonon}}ℏωphonon​ and (crystal) momentum ℏk\hbar kℏk. At temperature TTT, the mean phonon occupation of a mode is given by the Bose–Einstein factor nˉ=(eℏω/kT−1)−1\bar{n} = (e^{\hbar\omega/kT}-1)^{-1}nˉ=(eℏω/kT−1)−1.

Role in solid-state lasers: In four-level laser systems such as Nd:YAG, an excited Nd3+^{3+}3+ ion first absorbs a pump photon to a broad pump band (level 3). Rather than radiating back down, it undergoes rapid non-radiative decay to the upper laser level (level 2) by emitting phonons - transferring energy to the crystal lattice as heat. This phonon cascade is extremely fast (∼\sim∼picoseconds), ensuring rapid population of the upper laser level.

Equally crucial: the lower laser level (level 1' in a four-level system) is rapidly depopulated by a second phonon cascade to the ground state. This prevents population accumulating in level 1', maintaining the population inversion N2>N1N_2 > N_1N2​>N1​ without needing to overcome the Boltzmann penalty of the ground state. The net result is that phonon channels effectively "funnel" pump energy into the lasing transition with high efficiency.

Why this matters for threshold: Without phonon depopulation of the lower level, the gain medium would self-absorb its own laser light (re-absorption). The phonon-assisted four-level scheme elegantly separates pumping, storage, and emission into distinct time scales - precisely the design principle that allows CW operation in Nd:YAG.
Q5 The Nd:YAG laser operates at λ=1064 nm\lambda = 1064\,\text{nm}λ=1064nm. Calculate: (a) the energy of each emitted photon in joules, (b) the photon energy in eV.
3 marksShow Answer ▾

(a) E=hcλ=6.63�-10−34�-3�-1081064�-10−9=1.87�-10−19 JE = \frac{hc}{\lambda} = \frac{6.63\times10^{-34} \times 3\times10^8}{1064\times10^{-9}} = \boxed{1.87\times10^{-19}\,\text{J}}E=λhc​=1064�-10−96.63�-10−34�-3�-108​=1.87�-10−19J​

(b) E=1.87�-10−191.6�-10−19=1.17 eVE = \frac{1.87\times10^{-19}}{1.6\times10^{-19}} = \boxed{1.17\,\text{eV}}E=1.6�-10−191.87�-10−19​=1.17eV​

At 1064 nm, Nd:YAG output lies in the near-infrared - invisible to the eye but detectable by silicon photodiodes. Frequency doubling (second harmonic generation, SHG) in a KTP or KDP crystal converts this to 532 nm green, the familiar green laser pointer wavelength.

Q6 Explain why ruby appears red, referring to the electronic structure of the Cr3+^{3+}3+ ion in Al2_22​O3_33​.
2 marksShow Answer ▾

The Cr3+^{3+}3+ ion has three 3d electrons. In the Al2_22​O3_33​ (corundum) host crystal, the surrounding O2−^{2-}2− ions create an octahedral crystal field that breaks the spherical symmetry of the free ion and Stark-splits the 3d energy levels. This splitting creates broad absorption bands centred in the blue (~410 nm) and yellow-green (~550 nm) regions of the visible spectrum.

Because blue and green wavelengths are strongly absorbed, only red wavelengths (~700 nm) are transmitted and reflected - giving ruby its characteristic deep red colour. The sharp red emission at 694.3 nm (the R1_11​ line) arises from the 2E→4A2^2\text{E} \to ^4\text{A}_22E→4A2​ transition - a spin-forbidden transition that is nonetheless the laser transition in the ruby laser, with a long radiative lifetime of ~3 ms.

Q7 What is an exciton? Describe its formation when phosphorus is doped into silicon.
3 marksShow Answer ▾

Exciton: An exciton is a bound quasiparticle consisting of an electron and a hole held together by their mutual Coulomb attraction. It is electrically neutral and can transport energy without transporting charge. The binding energy is analogous to the hydrogen atom but screened by the dielectric constant of the host - typically 10–50 meV in semiconductors, far smaller than 13.6 eV.

P-doped silicon: Phosphorus has five valence electrons. When a P atom substitutes for a Si atom (four valence electrons), four of P's electrons form covalent bonds with neighbouring Si atoms. The fifth electron is loosely bound to the P+^++ ion core by a screened Coulomb interaction: V(r)=−e24πϵ0ϵrrV(r) = -\frac{e^2}{4\pi\epsilon_0 \epsilon_r r}V(r)=−4πϵ0​ϵr​re2​, where ϵr≈11.7\epsilon_r \approx 11.7ϵr​≈11.7 for Si. This creates a hydrogen-like bound state (a donor exciton) with energy levels En≈−13.6 eVϵr2n2≈−0.045 eV(n=1)E_n \approx -\frac{13.6\,\text{eV}}{\epsilon_r^2 n^2} \approx -0.045\,\text{eV} \quad (n=1)En​≈−ϵr2​n213.6eV​≈−0.045eV(n=1) lying just 45 meV below the conduction band. At room temperature this is easily ionised, contributing a free electron - the basis of n-type doping.

Q8 Distinguish between homogeneous and inhomogeneous spectral broadening. Give one physical cause of each.
3 marksShow Answer ▾

Homogeneous broadening affects all atoms in the ensemble equally and identically - every atom has the same broadened lineshape. The result is a Lorentzian spectral profile g(ν)∝11+(2(ν−ν0)/ΔνH)2g(\nu) \propto \frac{1}{1+(2(\nu-\nu_0)/\Delta\nu_H)^2}g(ν)∝1+(2(ν−ν0​)/ΔνH​)21​. Physical cause: the natural (lifetime) linewidth. The excited state has a finite radiative lifetime τ\tauτ, and by the energy-time uncertainty relation ΔE⋅Δt≈ℏ\Delta E\cdot\Delta t \approx \hbarΔE⋅Δt≈ℏ, the energy level has an intrinsic width ΔνH=A21/2π=1/2πτ\Delta\nu_H = A_{21}/2\pi = 1/2\pi\tauΔνH​=A21​/2π=1/2πτ. Every atom has the same τ\tauτ, so every atom contributes at the same frequencies.

Inhomogeneous broadening arises because different atoms (or ions) in the ensemble have different resonance frequencies, so each atom contributes a narrow peak at a slightly different frequency. The ensemble line is the sum of many slightly shifted lines - a Gaussian profile g(ν)∝e−(ν−ν0)2/2σ2g(\nu) \propto e^{-(\nu-\nu_0)^2/2\sigma^2}g(ν)∝e−(ν−ν0​)2/2σ2. Physical cause: Doppler broadening in gas lasers. Atoms move with a Maxwell-Boltzmann velocity distribution; an atom moving at velocity vvv along the beam axis sees the laser frequency shifted to ν0(1±v/c)\nu_0(1\pm v/c)ν0​(1±v/c). Each velocity class emits at a slightly different frequency, producing an inhomogeneous Gaussian profile with width ΔνD∝T\Delta\nu_D \propto \sqrt{T}ΔνD​∝T​.

Why it matters for lasers: In a homogeneously broadened laser, saturation depletes gain for all atoms simultaneously → tends toward single-mode operation. In an inhomogeneously broadened laser, each frequency class saturates independently → spectral hole burning allows multiple modes to oscillate at once (He-Ne typically runs 3–4 modes).
Q9 The Ti:sapphire laser can be tuned from 670 nm to 1100 nm. Calculate the corresponding frequency range.
2 marksShow Answer ▾

f1=cλ1=3�-108670�-10−9=4.48�-1014 Hzf_1 = \frac{c}{\lambda_1} = \frac{3\times10^8}{670\times10^{-9}} = 4.48\times10^{14}\,\text{Hz}f1​=λ1​c​=670�-10−93�-108​=4.48�-1014Hz f2=cλ2=3�-1081100�-10−9=2.73�-1014 Hzf_2 = \frac{c}{\lambda_2} = \frac{3\times10^8}{1100\times10^{-9}} = 2.73\times10^{14}\,\text{Hz}f2​=λ2​c​=1100�-10−93�-108​=2.73�-1014Hz Δf=4.48�-1014−2.73�-1014=1.75�-1014 Hz≈175 THz\Delta f = 4.48\times10^{14} - 2.73\times10^{14} = \boxed{1.75\times10^{14}\,\text{Hz} \approx 175\,\text{THz}}Δf=4.48�-1014−2.73�-1014=1.75�-1014Hz≈175THz​

This enormous bandwidth makes Ti:sapphire the workhorse for ultrashort pulse generation. By mode-locking all longitudinal modes simultaneously (Kerr-lens mode-locking), pulse durations of ∼\sim∼5–10 fs are achievable - approaching the fundamental transform limit set by the bandwidth-duration product Δν⋅Δt≥0.44\Delta\nu\cdot\Delta t \geq 0.44Δν⋅Δt≥0.44 for Gaussian pulses.

Q10 Using Wien's displacement law (λmax⁡T=2.90�-10−3 m⋅K\lambda_{\max}T = 2.90\times10^{-3}\,\text{m·K}λmax​T=2.90�-10−3m⋅K), calculate the peak emission wavelength of a star with surface temperature T=30,000 KT = 30{,}000\,\text{K}T=30,000K. In which part of the electromagnetic spectrum does this lie?
2 marksShow Answer ▾

λmax⁡=2.90�-10−330000=9.67�-10−8 m=96.7 nm\lambda_{\max} = \frac{2.90\times10^{-3}}{30000} = 9.67\times10^{-8}\,\text{m} = \boxed{96.7\,\text{nm}}λmax​=300002.90�-10−3​=9.67�-10−8m=96.7nm​

This lies in the extreme ultraviolet (EUV), far beyond the blue end of the visible spectrum. Hot O-type stars (T>25,000 KT > 25{,}000\,\text{K}T>25,000K) are overwhelming UV emitters; their visible light is merely the tail of the Planck distribution. Wien's law is the direct consequence of differentiating the Planck spectrum ρ(ν)=8πhν3c3⋅1ehν/kT−1\rho(\nu) = \frac{8\pi h\nu^3}{c^3}\cdot\frac{1}{e^{h\nu/kT}-1}ρ(ν)=c38πhν3​⋅ehν/kT−11​ with respect to ν\nuν and setting to zero.

Q11 Why do dye molecules produce very broad spectral emission bands compared with atoms in a gas?
2 marksShow Answer ▾

Organic dye molecules (e.g. rhodamine 6G) are large polyatomic systems with many vibrational and rotational degrees of freedom. Each electronic energy level - ground state and excited state alike - is not a single sharp level but rather a densely spaced ladder of vibrational sub-levels (and on top of these, rotational sub-levels). When the molecule is electronically excited, it very rapidly thermalises to the lowest vibrational level of the excited electronic state (in ∼10−12 s\sim10^{-12}\,\text{s}∼10−12s). It then fluoresces down to the vibrational manifold of the ground state.

Since the transition can land on any vibrational level of the ground state, each transition emits a photon of a slightly different energy, giving a broad, quasi-continuous emission band spanning ∼\sim∼50–100 nm. This is exploited in tunable dye lasers: a diffraction grating selects the lasing wavelength from across the broad gain bandwidth, enabling continuous tuning across the visible spectrum.

Q12 A doped-insulator laser uses Nd3+^{3+}3+ in YAG at 1% concentration. Explain how the crystalline host modifies the optical properties of Nd3+^{3+}3+ ions compared with free Nd ions.
3 marksShow Answer ▾

Crystal-field Stark splitting: In free Nd3+^{3+}3+, the 4f4f4f electrons are shielded from the environment by the outer 5s25p65s^2 5p^65s25p6 electron shells, so the optical transitions remain relatively sharp. However, the electric field of the surrounding O2−^{2-}2− and Al3+^{3+}3+ ions in the YAG crystal lattice (Y3Al5O12Y_3Al_5O_{12}Y3​Al5​O12​) breaks the spherical symmetry of the free ion. This Stark splitting lifts the degeneracy of the 4f4f4f sublevels, splitting each J-multiplet into a manifold of closely spaced levels. The practical effect is moderate broadening of absorption and emission lines (from ∼\sim∼MHz for the free ion to ∼\sim∼THz in the crystal), which actually benefits pumping efficiency - the broader absorption bands accept a wider range of pump wavelengths from a diode laser or flashlamp.

Isolation of active ions: YAG spatially separates neighbouring Nd3+^{3+}3+ ions. At low doping concentrations (≤\leq≤1 at%), the interionic distance is large enough that ion-ion energy transfer (concentration quenching) is suppressed. This preserves the long upper-laser-level lifetime (τ≈230 μs\tau \approx 230\,\mu\text{s}τ≈230μs) needed for efficient population inversion. The host also provides the phonon bath responsible for rapid non-radiative decay of pump and lower-laser levels (see Q4).

Thermal properties: YAG has excellent thermal conductivity (10 W m−1K−110\,\text{W\,m}^{-1}\text{K}^{-1}10Wm−1K−1), dissipating the quantum defect heat (∼\sim∼25% of pump energy) without catastrophic thermal gradients. Free Nd atoms carry no such advantage.

Topic 2

Absorption, Emission and Gain

Q13 Define the Einstein A21A_{21}A21​ and B12B_{12}B12​, B21B_{21}B21​ coefficients. State the units of each.
3 marksShow Answer ▾

A21A_{21}A21​ - Spontaneous emission coefficient: The probability per unit time that an atom in excited state 2 spontaneously decays to state 1, emitting a photon. It is an intrinsic property of the transition, independent of the radiation field. Units: s−1^{-1}−1. The spontaneous lifetime τ=1/A21\tau = 1/A_{21}τ=1/A21​.

B12B_{12}B12​ - Stimulated absorption coefficient: The probability per unit time per unit spectral energy density ρ(ν)\rho(\nu)ρ(ν) that an atom in ground state 1 absorbs a photon and transitions to state 2. The stimulated absorption rate is W12=B12ρ(ν)W_{12} = B_{12}\rho(\nu)W12​=B12​ρ(ν). Units: m3^33 J−1^{-1}−1 s−2^{-2}−2.

B21B_{21}B21​ - Stimulated emission coefficient: The probability per unit time per unit spectral energy density that an atom in excited state 2 is triggered to emit a photon identical to the stimulating photon, decaying to state 1. Rate: W21=B21ρ(ν)W_{21}=B_{21}\rho(\nu)W21​=B21​ρ(ν). Units: m3^33 J−1^{-1}−1 s−2^{-2}−2. For non-degenerate levels: B12=B21=BB_{12} = B_{21} = BB12​=B21​=B.

Physical interpretation (Feynman): Stimulated emission is the process by which a passing photon "induces" the atom to emit a second photon that is a perfect clone: same frequency, direction, phase, and polarisation. It is the fundamental quantum optical mechanism underlying laser amplification. Einstein introduced all three coefficients in 1917 - seventeen years before lasers were built - as a logical necessity for the blackbody spectrum to be self-consistent.
Q14 Write down the rate equations for populations N1N_1N1​ and N2N_2N2​ in a two-level system with energy density ρ(ν)\rho(\nu)ρ(ν).
3 marksShow Answer ▾

The rate equations track how the population of each level changes due to all three Einstein processes:

dN2dt=N1Bρ(ν)−N2A21−N2Bρ(ν)\frac{dN_2}{dt} = N_1 B\rho(\nu) - N_2 A_{21} - N_2 B\rho(\nu)dtdN2​​=N1​Bρ(ν)−N2​A21​−N2​Bρ(ν) dN1dt=−N1Bρ(ν)+N2A21+N2Bρ(ν)\frac{dN_1}{dt} = -N_1 B\rho(\nu) + N_2 A_{21} + N_2 B\rho(\nu)dtdN1​​=−N1​Bρ(ν)+N2​A21​+N2​Bρ(ν)

The three terms represent: stimulated absorption (pumping atoms into level 2), spontaneous emission (decaying to level 1), and stimulated emission (also decaying to level 1). Population is conserved: dN1dt=−dN2dt\frac{dN_1}{dt} = -\frac{dN_2}{dt}dtdN1​​=−dtdN2​​, so N1+N2=N=constN_1 + N_2 = N = \text{const}N1​+N2​=N=const.

At steady state (d/dt=0d/dt = 0d/dt=0), solving these equations under thermal equilibrium (and using the Boltzmann ratio N2/N1=e−hν/kTN_2/N_1 = e^{-h\nu/kT}N2​/N1​=e−hν/kT) forces B12=B21B_{12}=B_{21}B12​=B21​ and yields the Einstein relation A/B=8πhν3/c3A/B = 8\pi h\nu^3/c^3A/B=8πhν3/c3 - the famous result that ties all three coefficients together.
Q15 An atom has spontaneous emission lifetime τ=10 ns\tau = 10\,\text{ns}τ=10ns. (a) Calculate A21A_{21}A21​. (b) If Bρ(ν)=105 s−1B\rho(\nu) = 10^5\,\text{s}^{-1}Bρ(ν)=105s−1, which process dominates and by what factor?
3 marksShow Answer ▾

(a) A21=1τ=110�-10−9=108 s−1A_{21} = \frac{1}{\tau} = \frac{1}{10\times10^{-9}} = \boxed{10^8\,\text{s}^{-1}}A21​=τ1​=10�-10−91​=108s−1​

(b) A21Bρ(ν)=108105=1000\frac{A_{21}}{B\rho(\nu)} = \frac{10^8}{10^5} = \boxed{1000}Bρ(ν)A21​​=105108​=1000​

Spontaneous emission dominates by a factor of 1000. This is entirely typical for an atom illuminated by ordinary (non-laser) light: spontaneous emission is the overwhelmingly dominant process in conventional light sources. To reach the regime where stimulated emission is comparable to spontaneous emission requires the energy density ρ(ν)\rho(\nu)ρ(ν) of an intense laser beam. Inside a laser cavity above threshold, Bρ(ν)B\rho(\nu)Bρ(ν) can approach or exceed A21A_{21}A21​, enabling the population inversion to be efficiently depleted by stimulated emission.

Q16 State Beer's law. A beam I0=500 W m−2I_0 = 500\,\text{W\,m}^{-2}I0​=500Wm−2 passes through an absorber with α=2 m−1\alpha = 2\,\text{m}^{-1}α=2m−1. Calculate the intensity after (a) 1 m, (b) 2 m, (c) the distance at which intensity has halved.
4 marksShow Answer ▾

Beer's law: I(x)=I0 e−αxI(x) = I_0\, e^{-\alpha x}I(x)=I0​e−αx. Each infinitesimal slice dxdxdx of absorbing medium removes a fraction α dx\alpha\,dxαdx of the intensity: dI=−αI dxdI = -\alpha I\,dxdI=−αIdx. Integrating gives the exponential decay.

(a) I(1)=500�-e−2=500�-0.1353=I(1) = 500 \times e^{-2} = 500 \times 0.1353 =I(1)=500�-e−2=500�-0.1353= 67.7 W m⁻²

(b) I(2)=500�-e−4=500�-0.01832=I(2) = 500 \times e^{-4} = 500 \times 0.01832 =I(2)=500�-e−4=500�-0.01832= 9.16 W m⁻²

(c) 12=e−αx1/2⇒x1/2=ln⁡2α=0.6932=\frac{1}{2} = e^{-\alpha x_{1/2}} \Rightarrow x_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{\alpha} = \frac{0.693}{2} =21​=e−αx1/2​⇒x1/2​=αln2​=20.693​= 0.347 m

This half-power distance is the optical analogue of the nuclear half-life. Note the asymmetry: at x=2 mx = 2\,\text{m}x=2m, only 1.8% of the original power remains - the exponential decay is ruthless at large depths.

Q17 Explain qualitatively why a population inversion cannot be maintained in a simple two-level system at thermal equilibrium, regardless of pump intensity.
3 marksShow Answer ▾

At thermal equilibrium, the Boltzmann distribution requires N2N1=e−hν/kT<1\frac{N_2}{N_1} = e^{-h\nu/kT} < 1N1​N2​​=e−hν/kT<1 for any positive temperature - meaning the ground state is always more populated than the excited state. This is a statistical thermodynamics statement, not a limitation of the pumping mechanism.

Now consider the rate equations at steady state for strong pumping (Bρ≫AB\rho \gg ABρ≫A): dN2dt=N1Bρ−N2A−N2Bρ=0\frac{dN_2}{dt} = N_1 B\rho - N_2 A - N_2 B\rho = 0dtdN2​​=N1​Bρ−N2​A−N2​Bρ=0 Solving: N2=N1BρA+BρN_2 = \frac{N_1 B\rho}{A + B\rho}N2​=A+BρN1​Bρ​. As ρ→∞\rho \to \inftyρ→∞: N2→N1BρBρ=N1N_2 \to \frac{N_1 B\rho}{B\rho} = N_1N2​→BρN1​Bρ​=N1​. The populations equalise (N1=N2=N/2N_1 = N_2 = N/2N1​=N2​=N/2) but never invert. This is absorption saturation: at high intensity, stimulated emission exactly cancels stimulated absorption, bleaching the transition to transparency but never producing gain.

Physical picture: In a two-level system, pumping from level 1 to level 2 also automatically opens the return path (stimulated emission from 2 back to 1) with the same cross-section. The more you pump, the more the return path opens. A population inversion requires a third or fourth level so that the lower laser level empties by a channel other than stimulated absorption - phonon decay in Nd:YAG being the canonical example.
Q18 Derive Beer's law (I=I0e−αxI = I_0 e^{-\alpha x}I=I0​e−αx) from the absorption rate equation, showing α=BNhν/c\alpha = BNh\nu/cα=BNhν/c.
5 marksShow Answer ▾
1Consider a thin slab of absorbing medium: area AAA, thickness dxdxdx, containing NNN ground-state atoms per m3^33.
2Number of atoms in the slab: dNatoms=N⋅A⋅dxdN_{\text{atoms}} = N \cdot A \cdot dxdNatoms​=N⋅A⋅dx. Each absorbs photons at rate Bρ(ν)B\rho(\nu)Bρ(ν) per atom.
3Power absorbed from the beam: dP=N⋅A⋅dx⋅Bρ(ν)⋅hνdP = N\cdot A\cdot dx \cdot B\rho(\nu) \cdot h\nudP=N⋅A⋅dx⋅Bρ(ν)⋅hν. Since intensity I=PAI = \frac{P}{A}I=AP​ and ρ(ν)=I/c\rho(\nu) = I/cρ(ν)=I/c: dP=N⋅A⋅dx⋅B⋅Ic⋅hνdP = N\cdot A\cdot dx\cdot B\cdot\frac{I}{c}\cdot h\nudP=N⋅A⋅dx⋅B⋅cI​⋅hν
4The change in intensity dI=−dP/AdI = -dP/AdI=−dP/A: dIdx=−BNhνc I≡−αI,α=BNhνc\frac{dI}{dx} = -\frac{BNh\nu}{c}\,I \equiv -\alpha I, \quad \alpha = \frac{BNh\nu}{c}dxdI​=−cBNhν​I≡−αI,α=cBNhν​
5Integrating: ∫I0IdI′I′=−α∫0xdx′\int_{I_0}^{I}\frac{dI'}{I'} = -\alpha\int_0^x dx'∫I0​I​I′dI′​=−α∫0x​dx′ ⇒ln⁡II0=−αx⇒I=I0 e−αx■\Rightarrow \ln\frac{I}{I_0} = -\alpha x \Rightarrow \boxed{I = I_0\,e^{-\alpha x}}\quad\blacksquare⇒lnI0​I​=−αx⇒I=I0​e−αx​■

The absorption coefficient α=BNhν/c\alpha = BNh\nu/cα=BNhν/c has units m−1^{-1}−1. It is proportional to the atom density NNN and to BBB (the intrinsic transition strength). When a population inversion exists (N2>N1N_2 > N_1N2​>N1​), replacing N→N1−N2<0N \to N_1 - N_2 < 0N→N1​−N2​<0 makes α<0\alpha < 0α<0 - i.e. gain rather than loss - and the beam grows exponentially: I=I0e∣k∣xI = I_0 e^{|k|x}I=I0​e∣k∣x.

Q19 If 30% of light is transmitted through 5 cm of material, calculate the absorption coefficient α\alphaα in m−1^{-1}−1.
2 marksShow Answer ▾

0.30=e−α�-0.05⇒−α�-0.05=ln⁡(0.30)=−1.2040.30 = e^{-\alpha \times 0.05} \Rightarrow -\alpha\times0.05 = \ln(0.30) = -1.2040.30=e−α�-0.05⇒−α�-0.05=ln(0.30)=−1.204 α=1.2040.05=24.1 m−1\alpha = \frac{1.204}{0.05} = \boxed{24.1\,\text{m}^{-1}}α=0.051.204​=24.1m−1​

This corresponds to 2.41 cm−12.41\,\text{cm}^{-1}2.41cm−1 - a fairly large absorption coefficient. For comparison, silica fibre at 1550 nm has α≈4.6�-10−5 m−1\alpha \approx 4.6\times10^{-5}\,\text{m}^{-1}α≈4.6�-10−5m−1 (0.2 dB/km), illustrating the extraordinary purity required for telecommunications fibre.

Q20 At room temperature (T=300 KT=300\,\text{K}T=300K) and optical frequency ν=5�-1014 Hz\nu = 5\times10^{14}\,\text{Hz}ν=5�-1014Hz, evaluate ehν/kTe^{h\nu/kT}ehν/kT. What does this tell you about the thermal photon population at visible frequencies?
3 marksShow Answer ▾

hνkT=6.63�-10−34�-5�-10141.38�-10−23�-300=3.315�-10−194.14�-10−21≈80.1\frac{h\nu}{kT} = \frac{6.63\times10^{-34}\times5\times10^{14}}{1.38\times10^{-23}\times300} = \frac{3.315\times10^{-19}}{4.14\times10^{-21}} \approx 80.1kThν​=1.38�-10−23�-3006.63�-10−34�-5�-1014​=4.14�-10−213.315�-10−19​≈80.1

e80.1≈5.5�-1034e^{80.1} \approx 5.5\times10^{34}e80.1≈5.5�-1034

Since ehν/kT≫1e^{h\nu/kT} \gg 1ehν/kT≫1, the Planck denominator (ehν/kT−1)≈ehν/kT(e^{h\nu/kT}-1) \approx e^{h\nu/kT}(ehν/kT−1)≈ehν/kT, so ρ(ν)≈8πhν3c3e−hν/kT≈0\rho(\nu) \approx \frac{8\pi h\nu^3}{c^3}e^{-h\nu/kT} \approx 0ρ(ν)≈c38πhν3​e−hν/kT≈0. The mean number of thermal photons in a visible-frequency mode at room temperature is nˉ=(e80.1−1)−1≈10−35\bar{n} = (e^{80.1}-1)^{-1} \approx 10^{-35}nˉ=(e80.1−1)−1≈10−35 - essentially zero. Room-temperature objects simply do not emit visible photons spontaneously. You need a temperature of ∼\sim∼6000 K (the Sun's surface) to produce significant visible thermal radiation.

Q21 Using the mode density Nν=8πν2/c3N_\nu = 8\pi\nu^2/c^3Nν​=8πν2/c3 and Planck's result, write the full expression for ρ(ν) dν\rho(\nu)\,d\nuρ(ν)dν of blackbody radiation.
2 marksShow Answer ▾
ρ(ν) dν=Nν⋅Eˉ(ν) dν=8πν2c3⋅hνehν/kT−1 dν=8πhν3c3⋅1ehν/kT−1 dν\rho(\nu)\,d\nu = N_\nu \cdot \bar{E}(\nu)\,d\nu = \frac{8\pi\nu^2}{c^3} \cdot \frac{h\nu}{e^{h\nu/kT}-1}\,d\nu = \frac{8\pi h\nu^3}{c^3}\cdot\frac{1}{e^{h\nu/kT}-1}\,d\nuρ(ν)dν=Nν​⋅Eˉ(ν)dν=c38πν2​⋅ehν/kT−1hν​dν=c38πhν3​⋅ehν/kT−11​dν

The mode density Nν=8πν2/c3N_\nu = 8\pi\nu^2/c^3Nν​=8πν2/c3 counts the number of electromagnetic modes per unit volume per unit frequency interval in a cavity (factor 8π\piπ rather than 4π\piπ because each mode supports two polarisations). Planck's genius was to assign each mode a mean energy Eˉ=hν/(ehν/kT−1)\bar{E} = h\nu/(e^{h\nu/kT}-1)Eˉ=hν/(ehν/kT−1) - the average energy of a quantum harmonic oscillator - rather than the classical kTkTkT, which would give the ultraviolet catastrophe.

Q22 Show that in thermal equilibrium, N2<N1N_2 < N_1N2​<N1​ for any positive temperature TTT.
2 marksShow Answer ▾

The Boltzmann ratio for a two-level system with transition frequency ν>0\nu > 0ν>0: N2N1=e−hν/kT\frac{N_2}{N_1} = e^{-h\nu/kT}N1​N2​​=e−hν/kT

Since ν>0\nu > 0ν>0 and T>0T > 0T>0, the exponent −hν/kT<0-h\nu/kT < 0−hν/kT<0, so e−hν/kT<e0=1e^{-h\nu/kT} < e^0 = 1e−hν/kT<e0=1. Therefore N2/N1<1N_2/N_1 < 1N2​/N1​<1, i.e. N2<N1N_2 < N_1N2​<N1​. A population inversion (N2>N1N_2 > N_1N2​>N1​) corresponds to a negative temperature in the Boltzmann sense - a non-equilibrium, driven state that requires continuous pumping to sustain. ■\blacksquare■

Q23 What is absorption saturation? At what condition does it occur, and what happens to α\alphaα?
3 marksShow Answer ▾

Absorption saturation occurs when the incident radiation is intense enough that the rate of stimulated emission from level 2 becomes comparable to the absorption rate from level 1: Bρ(ν)≫A21B\rho(\nu) \gg A_{21}Bρ(ν)≫A21​. Under these conditions, the steady-state populations equalise: N1=N2=N/2N_1 = N_2 = N/2N1​=N2​=N/2.

Since the absorption coefficient α∝(N1−N2)\alpha \propto (N_1 - N_2)α∝(N1​−N2​), and at saturation N1=N2N_1 = N_2N1​=N2​, we have α→0\alpha \to 0α→0. The medium becomes transparent to the intense beam - it can absorb no more. This is a nonlinear optical effect: the absorption depends on intensity, violating the linear response assumed in Beer's law.

The saturation intensity is defined as Isat=Ac2BI_{\text{sat}} = \frac{Ac}{2B}Isat​=2BAc​ - the intensity at which the gain (or absorption) is halved. Above IsatI_{\text{sat}}Isat​: α(I)=α01+I/Isat\alpha(I) = \frac{\alpha_0}{1 + I/I_{\text{sat}}}α(I)=1+I/Isat​α0​​

Saturable absorption is not just a limitation - it is exploited. SESAMs (semiconductor saturable absorber mirrors) use this effect to initiate mode-locking in ultrafast lasers: high-intensity pulses bleach the absorber and experience lower loss, while continuous-wave background is suppressed.
Q24 Explain the physical origin of the refractive index using the classical oscillator model for atoms off resonance.
4 marksShow Answer ▾

The classical oscillator model (Lorentz model): Each atom is modelled as an electron bound to the nucleus by a restoring force, making it a harmonic oscillator with natural frequency ω0\omega_0ω0​. When a light wave of frequency ω\omegaω arrives, its oscillating electric field E(t)=E0e−iωtE(t) = E_0 e^{-i\omega t}E(t)=E0​e−iωt drives this oscillator. The steady-state displacement (from the driven harmonic oscillator equation of motion) has amplitude and phase that depend on how far ω\omegaω is from ω0\omega_0ω0​.

Re-radiation and phase lag: The oscillating electron is an accelerating charge, so it re-radiates a wave at the same frequency ω\omegaω. But crucially, this re-radiated wave has a phase lag with respect to the incident wave that depends on ω−ω0\omega - \omega_0ω−ω0​. For ω<ω0\omega < \omega_0ω<ω0​ (below resonance, as visible light in glass with UV resonances), the phase lag is between 0 and π/2\pi/2π/2.

The total field is the sum of the incident wave and all the re-radiated wavelets from the dense slab of atoms (following Feynman's beautiful phasor treatment in Lectures Vol. I, Ch. 31). When added coherently, the re-radiated contributions advance or retard the phase of the forward-propagating wave. The net effect is that the wave travels at a phase velocity v=c/nv = c/nv=c/n, where n>1n > 1n>1 for below-resonance frequencies. This apparent "slowing" is not the individual photons slowing down - it is a phase interference effect.

Quantitatively, the real part of the complex refractive index:

n(ω)≈1+Ne22mϵ0⋅ω02−ω2(ω02−ω2)2+γ2ω2n(\omega) \approx 1 + \frac{Ne^2}{2m\epsilon_0}\cdot\frac{\omega_0^2 - \omega^2}{(\omega_0^2-\omega^2)^2 + \gamma^2\omega^2}n(ω)≈1+2mϵ0​Ne2​⋅(ω02​−ω2)2+γ2ω2ω02​−ω2​

where γ\gammaγ is the damping coefficient and NNN is the atom density. Because nnn depends on ω\omegaω, light of different colours travels at different speeds - dispersion.

Feynman's insight (QED, Ch. 1): "Light does not really slow down in glass. What happens is that the light is absorbed and re-emitted by the atoms... and because of the time delay in this process, and the geometry, the net effect is as if the light had slowed down." This is one of the most vivid physical reframings in all of physics.
Q25 The Einstein relationship gives A/B=8πhν3/c3A/B = 8\pi h\nu^3/c^3A/B=8πhν3/c3. State what this implies about making lasers at X-ray frequencies (ν∼1018 Hz\nu \sim 10^{18}\,\text{Hz}ν∼1018Hz) vs visible (ν∼5�-1014 Hz\nu \sim 5\times10^{14}\,\text{Hz}ν∼5�-1014Hz).
3 marksShow Answer ▾

The ratio A/B∝ν3A/B \propto \nu^3A/B∝ν3. Comparing X-ray to visible: (A/B)X-ray(A/B)vis=(10185�-1014)3=(2000)3=8�-109\frac{(A/B)_{\text{X-ray}}}{(A/B)_{\text{vis}}} = \left(\frac{10^{18}}{5\times10^{14}}\right)^3 = (2000)^3 = 8\times10^9(A/B)vis​(A/B)X-ray​​=(5�-10141018​)3=(2000)3=8�-109

At X-ray frequencies, the spontaneous emission rate A21A_{21}A21​ is approximately 109�-\mathbf{10^9\times}109�- larger than at visible frequencies for the same transition strength BBB. This has devastating consequences for X-ray lasing:

  • Population inversion is extremely hard to build up - atoms decay spontaneously before sufficient inversion accumulates.
  • The pump must be astronomically intense to compete with A21A_{21}A21​, often requiring nuclear explosions (as in early X-ray laser experiments) or high-harmonic generation from ultrashort pulses.
  • Cavity mirrors do not exist at X-ray wavelengths with high reflectivity, so single-pass amplification of ASE is used (no resonator).
The X-ray free electron laser (XFEL) at DESY/SLAC circumvents this by using a completely different mechanism - electron undulators - rather than population inversion in atoms. It is not a "laser" in the Einstein sense at all.
Q26 A beam traverses 10 cm of glass with α=50 m−1\alpha = 50\,\text{m}^{-1}α=50m−1. Express the transmission in dB.
2 marksShow Answer ▾

IoutIin=e−αx=e−50�-0.1=e−5=6.74�-10−3\frac{I_{\text{out}}}{I_{\text{in}}} = e^{-\alpha x} = e^{-50\times0.1} = e^{-5} = 6.74\times10^{-3}Iin​Iout​​=e−αx=e−50�-0.1=e−5=6.74�-10−3 Attenuation (dB)=−10log⁡10 ⁣(IoutIin)=−10log⁡10(6.74�-10−3)=−10�-(−2.171)=21.7 dB\text{Attenuation (dB)} = -10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{I_{\text{out}}}{I_{\text{in}}}\right) = -10\log_{10}(6.74\times10^{-3}) = -10\times(-2.171) = \boxed{21.7\,\text{dB}}Attenuation (dB)=−10log10​(Iin​Iout​​)=−10log10​(6.74�-10−3)=−10�-(−2.171)=21.7dB​

Recall the dB scale: 10 dB = power ratio of 10; 3 dB ≈ factor of 2. Here, 21.7 dB ≈ 148:1 attenuation. The logarithmic scale is used throughout telecommunications because losses and gains cascade additively in dB rather than multiplicatively in linear units.

Q27 Why is B12/B21=1B_{12}/B_{21} = 1B12​/B21​=1 for non-degenerate two-level systems? What does this imply about absorption and stimulated emission cross-sections?
2 marksShow Answer ▾

Einstein derived the equality B12=B21B_{12} = B_{21}B12​=B21​ as a logical necessity for the blackbody spectrum to be recovered at thermal equilibrium. At steady state, the upward rate N1B12ρN_1 B_{12}\rhoN1​B12​ρ must equal the downward stimulated rate N2B21ρN_2 B_{21}\rhoN2​B21​ρ plus spontaneous rate N2A21N_2 A_{21}N2​A21​. Substituting Boltzmann's N2/N1=e−hν/kTN_2/N_1 = e^{-h\nu/kT}N2​/N1​=e−hν/kT and Planck's ρ(ν)\rho(\nu)ρ(ν), consistency requires B12=B21B_{12} = B_{21}B12​=B21​ (for non-degenerate levels).

Physically: absorption and stimulated emission are time-reversed processes. Quantum mechanics (specifically, the requirement that the interaction Hamiltonian is Hermitian and the principle of microscopic reversibility / detailed balance) guarantees that the matrix element for 1→21\to21→2 transitions equals that for 2→12\to12→1. Therefore the stimulated emission cross-section equals the absorption cross-section at the same wavelength. A medium with a population inversion is an amplifier with the same σ\sigmaσ as an absorber - but with N2>N1N_2 > N_1N2​>N1​ making the net effect gain rather than loss.

Q28 Describe qualitatively how refractive index varies with frequency near an absorption resonance, and how this explains why red light bends less than blue light in a prism.
3 marksShow Answer ▾

The Lorentz oscillator model predicts normal dispersion for frequencies well below resonance: nnn increases gradually with increasing ω\omegaω. Near the resonance frequency ω0\omega_0ω0​, nnn rises steeply (dn/dω>0dn/d\omega > 0dn/dω>0) just below resonance, then drops sharply above (dn/dω<0dn/d\omega < 0dn/dω<0, anomalous dispersion), before recovering to normal dispersion again at high frequencies. The imaginary part of nnn (the absorption coefficient) peaks at ω0\omega_0ω0​.

Glass prism: For optical glass, the electronic resonances ω0\omega_0ω0​ lie in the UV (λ≲200 nm\lambda \lesssim 200\,\text{nm}λ≲200nm). All visible wavelengths are well below resonance, so the entire visible spectrum lies in the normal dispersion regime: nnn increases from red to blue. Numerically, nblue≈1.530n_{\text{blue}} \approx 1.530nblue​≈1.530 vs nred≈1.514n_{\text{red}} \approx 1.514nred​≈1.514 for crown glass. By Snell's law (n1sin⁡θ1=n2sin⁡θ2n_1\sin\theta_1 = n_2\sin\theta_2n1​sinθ1​=n2​sinθ2​), the higher nnn for blue means blue light bends more at the prism surface - producing the familiar rainbow ordering of Newton's prism spectrum.

Q29 Define the absorption coefficient α\alphaα and explain how optical gain occurs. Write an expression for the gain coefficient kkk in terms of N2,N1,B,h,νN_2, N_1, B, h, \nuN2​,N1​,B,h,ν and ccc.
3 marksShow Answer ▾

The absorption coefficient α=BN1hνc\alpha = \frac{BN_1 h\nu}{c}α=cBN1​hν​ (m−1^{-1}−1) gives the fractional intensity loss per metre: 1IdIdx=−α\frac{1}{I}\frac{dI}{dx} = -\alphaI1​dxdI​=−α. It is positive when N1>0N_1 > 0N1​>0, meaning the beam is attenuated exponentially.

Optical gain occurs when stimulated emission (rate ∝N2\propto N_2∝N2​) exceeds stimulated absorption (rate ∝N1\propto N_1∝N1​). Including both processes simultaneously in the rate equation for intensity:

dIdx=Bhνc(N2−N1) I≡k I\frac{dI}{dx} = \frac{Bh\nu}{c}(N_2 - N_1)\,I \equiv k\,IdxdI​=cBhν​(N2​−N1​)I≡kI

The gain coefficient is: k=Bhνc(N2−N1)k = \frac{Bh\nu}{c}(N_2 - N_1)k=cBhν​(N2​−N1​) When N2>N1N_2 > N_1N2​>N1​ (population inversion), k>0k > 0k>0 and the intensity grows exponentially: I(x)=I0 ekxI(x) = I_0\,e^{kx}I(x)=I0​ekx. The medium is an optical amplifier - each photon triggers the creation of an identical clone photon, and the beam doubles with each 1/k1/k1/k of propagation length.

Q30 Explain, using the classical oscillator model, why light absorption can be understood as destructive interference between the incident field and the re-radiated field from atoms.
3 marksShow Answer ▾

On resonance (ω=ω0\omega = \omega_0ω=ω0​): The driven harmonic oscillator responds at its maximum amplitude, and the displacement lags the driving force by exactly π/2\pi/2π/2. Because the re-radiated field is proportional to the acceleration of the electron (the electron radiates as an accelerating charge), and acceleration lags displacement by π/2\pi/2π/2, the re-radiated field lags the driving field by π/2+π/2=π\pi/2 + \pi/2 = \piπ/2+π/2=π.

The re-radiated field is anti-phase (180°180°180° out of phase) with the incident field. When these two fields are summed, destructive interference reduces the amplitude of the forward-propagating wave. The energy that "disappears" from the electromagnetic field has been absorbed by the atoms - it has done work against the restoring force of the atomic oscillator and is stored as internal energy (which is later dissipated as heat or re-emitted as fluorescence).

Feynman's phasor treatment (Feynman Lectures Vol. I, Ch. 31): Feynman shows this with beautiful phasor diagrams. The re-radiated field from a thin sheet of atoms is imaginary (90° to the incident field) for off-resonance, and real and opposite (180°) on resonance. This makes the "slowing of light" and "absorption of light" both emerge from the same mechanism - the relative phase of the re-radiated field with respect to the incident wave. This is arguably the most elegant derivation of the refractive index in all of physics pedagogy.
Topic 3

Polarization

Q31 Linearly polarized light I0I_0I0​ passes through a polarizer at θ=35°\theta = 35°θ=35°. Calculate the transmitted intensity (Malus' Law). State the law.
3 marksShow Answer ▾

Malus' Law: When linearly polarized light of intensity I0I_0I0​ passes through a linear polarizer whose transmission axis makes an angle θ\thetaθ with the polarization direction: Itrans=I0cos⁡2θI_{\text{trans}} = I_0\cos^2\thetaItrans​=I0​cos2θ The law follows from projecting the electric field amplitude onto the transmission axis (E→Ecos⁡θE \to E\cos\thetaE→Ecosθ) and noting that intensity ∝E2\propto E^2∝E2.

Itrans=I0cos⁡2(35°)=I0�-(0.819)2=0.671 I0I_{\text{trans}} = I_0\cos^2(35°) = I_0\times(0.819)^2 = \boxed{0.671\,I_0}Itrans​=I0​cos2(35°)=I0​�-(0.819)2=0.671I0​​

At θ=90°\theta = 90°θ=90° (crossed polarizers), I=0I = 0I=0. At θ=0°\theta = 0°θ=0°, I=I0I = I_0I=I0​. Malus discovered this law empirically in 1808; it was one of the earliest quantitative confirmations of the wave nature of light.

Q32 Unpolarized light I0I_0I0​ passes through two polarizers. The second is at 60° to the first. Find the final intensity.
3 marksShow Answer ▾

Step 1 - First polarizer on unpolarized light: I1=I0/2I_1 = I_0/2I1​=I0​/2. An ideal polarizer transmits only one polarization component; the other is absorbed or reflected. For unpolarized light (equal intensity in all polarization directions), exactly half is transmitted regardless of orientation.

Step 2 - Second polarizer (Malus' Law): I2=I1cos⁡2(60°)=I02�-(12)2=I02�-14=I08I_2 = I_1\cos^2(60°) = \frac{I_0}{2}\times\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^2 = \frac{I_0}{2}\times\frac{1}{4} = \boxed{\frac{I_0}{8}}I2​=I1​cos2(60°)=2I0​​�-(21​)2=2I0​​�-41​=8I0​​​

Only 12.5% of the original intensity survives. The factor of 1/21/21/2 from the first polarizer is often forgotten - it applies to any unpolarized incident beam regardless of orientation.

Q33 Derive Brewster's angle. Show that tan⁡θB=nt/ni\tan\theta_B = n_t/n_itanθB​=nt​/ni​ from the condition that R∥=0R_\parallel = 0R∥​=0.
4 marksShow Answer ▾

The Fresnel reflection coefficient for the p-polarization (parallel to plane of incidence) is: R∥=[tan⁡(θi−θt)tan⁡(θi+θt)]2R_\parallel = \left[\frac{\tan(\theta_i - \theta_t)}{\tan(\theta_i + \theta_t)}\right]^2R∥​=[tan(θi​+θt​)tan(θi​−θt​)​]2

1R∥=0R_\parallel = 0R∥​=0 requires tan⁡(θi+θt)→∞\tan(\theta_i + \theta_t) \to \inftytan(θi​+θt​)→∞, i.e. θi+θt=90°\theta_i + \theta_t = 90°θi​+θt​=90°.
2Therefore θt=90°−θi\theta_t = 90° - \theta_iθt​=90°−θi​, and sin⁡θt=sin⁡(90°−θi)=cos⁡θi\sin\theta_t = \sin(90°-\theta_i) = \cos\theta_isinθt​=sin(90°−θi​)=cosθi​.
3Snell's law: nisin⁡θi=ntsin⁡θt=ntcos⁡θin_i\sin\theta_i = n_t\sin\theta_t = n_t\cos\theta_ini​sinθi​=nt​sinθt​=nt​cosθi​
4Dividing both sides by nicos⁡θin_i\cos\theta_ini​cosθi​: tan⁡θB=ntni■\tan\theta_B = \frac{n_t}{n_i} \quad\blacksquaretanθB​=ni​nt​​■

Physical insight: At Brewster's angle, the refracted and reflected rays are perpendicular to each other (θi+θt=90°\theta_i + \theta_t = 90°θi​+θt​=90°). The p-polarized component of the reflected wave would have to travel in the same direction as the refracted wave - which is forbidden because the oscillating dipoles in the medium cannot radiate along their own oscillation axis. Hence R∥=0R_\parallel = 0R∥​=0 exactly.

Q34 A glass surface has n=1.55n = 1.55n=1.55. Beam incident from air at Brewster's angle. (a) Calculate θB\theta_BθB​. (b) Calculate the refracted angle θt\theta_tθt​.
3 marksShow Answer ▾

(a) tan⁡θB=ntni=1.551.00⇒θB=arctan⁡(1.55)=57.2°\tan\theta_B = \frac{n_t}{n_i} = \frac{1.55}{1.00} \Rightarrow \theta_B = \arctan(1.55) = \boxed{57.2°}tanθB​=ni​nt​​=1.001.55​⇒θB​=arctan(1.55)=57.2°​

(b) At Brewster's angle, θi+θt=90°\theta_i + \theta_t = 90°θi​+θt​=90°: θt=90°−57.2°=32.8°\theta_t = 90° - 57.2° = \boxed{32.8°}θt​=90°−57.2°=32.8°​

Check via Snell's law: nisin⁡θi=1�-sin⁡(57.2°)=0.839n_i\sin\theta_i = 1\times\sin(57.2°) = 0.839ni​sinθi​=1�-sin(57.2°)=0.839; ntsin⁡θt=1.55�-sin⁡(32.8°)=1.55�-0.541=0.839n_t\sin\theta_t = 1.55\times\sin(32.8°) = 1.55\times0.541 = 0.839nt​sinθt​=1.55�-sin(32.8°)=1.55�-0.541=0.839. ✓

At Brewster's angle, reflected light is 100% s-polarized (perpendicular to the plane of incidence). This is exploited in laser Brewster windows: a glass plate at θB\theta_BθB​ transmits p-polarized light with zero reflection loss, while s-polarization is partially reflected away, creating a polarized intracavity beam with minimal loss.

Q35 Explain how polarization by Rayleigh scattering gives rise to (a) blue sky and (b) partially polarized skylight.
4 marksShow Answer ▾

(a) Blue sky: The Sun emits approximately white (broadband) light. As this light passes through the atmosphere, it encounters molecules (primarily N2_22​ and O2_22​) whose diameter (∼0.3 nm\sim0.3\,\text{nm}∼0.3nm) is far smaller than optical wavelengths (400–700 nm400\text{–}700\,\text{nm}400–700nm). This is the Rayleigh scattering regime, where the scattering cross-section scales as: σRayleigh∝1λ4\sigma_{\text{Rayleigh}} \propto \frac{1}{\lambda^4}σRayleigh​∝λ41​ Blue light (λ≈450 nm\lambda \approx 450\,\text{nm}λ≈450nm) is scattered (700/450)4≈5.9�-(700/450)^4 \approx 5.9\times(700/450)4≈5.9�- more intensely than red (λ≈700 nm\lambda \approx 700\,\text{nm}λ≈700nm). Blue scattered light reaches the eye from all directions - we see a blue sky. At sunset, light travels through a much larger atmospheric path length, scattering away virtually all blue light and leaving the red residue - giving red sunsets.

(b) Polarization of skylight: Unpolarized sunlight drives electrons in each atmospheric molecule to oscillate as electric dipoles. A dipole oscillating along, say, the vertical axis (yyy) radiates a field polarized in the yyy-direction into the horizontal plane, but radiates no field along the yyy-axis itself (a dipole has a sin⁡2ϕ\sin^2\phisin2ϕ radiation pattern with zero emission along its axis). For light scattered at exactly 90° to the Sun's direction, one polarization component (E∥E_\parallelE∥​) corresponds to the dipole direction - which emits nothing in the 90° direction - while the other polarization (E⊥E_\perpE⊥​) is transmitted fully. The result: skylight viewed at 90° to the Sun is 100% linearly polarized. At other angles, partial polarization is observed. Bees exploit this for navigation; photographers use polarizing filters to darken blue sky.

Q36 What is optical activity? Describe the helical molecule model that explains it.
3 marksShow Answer ▾

Optical activity is the rotation of the plane of polarization of linearly polarized light as it propagates through certain materials - notably quartz and sugar solutions. A material is dextrorotatory (+) if it rotates the plane clockwise (viewed toward the source) and levorotatory (−) if counterclockwise. The rotation angle ϕ=[α]⋅c⋅l\phi = [\alpha]\cdot c\cdot lϕ=[α]⋅c⋅l (specific rotation [α][\alpha][α], concentration ccc, path length lll) is the basis of polarimetry for measuring concentrations of chiral molecules.

Helical molecule model: Consider a molecule with a helical backbone. Linearly polarized light incident along the helix axis drives electrons to oscillate along the polarization direction. However, the helix structure couples the longitudinal oscillation to a transverse circular component: electrons moving "up" the helix are simultaneously moved sideways by the winding. This gives the re-radiated field a component perpendicular to the incident polarization direction. Adding this perpendicular component to the transmitted field slightly rotates the polarization direction. Left- and right-handed helices rotate in opposite directions - making optical activity a direct probe of molecular chirality.

Q37 A calcite crystal placed on text causes a double image. Explain this in terms of birefringence.
3 marksShow Answer ▾

Birefringence (double refraction) arises in anisotropic crystals where the refractive index depends on the polarization direction of the light. Calcite (CaCO3_33​) is a uniaxial crystal: the optical axis defines one special direction. Light polarized along the optical axis ("extraordinary" ray, eee-ray) experiences index ne≈1.486n_e \approx 1.486ne​≈1.486, while light polarized perpendicular to it ("ordinary" ray, ooo-ray) experiences no≈1.658n_o \approx 1.658no​≈1.658. This is a very large birefringence (Δn≈0.172\Delta n \approx 0.172Δn≈0.172).

When unpolarized light from the text enters the calcite, it is split into two orthogonally polarized beams that propagate at slightly different angles through the crystal (because they have different nnn and thus obey Snell's law differently at each surface). On exiting, the two beams are spatially separated, each forming an independent image - hence the double image. A polarizer held over one image will block it and transmit the other, confirming they are orthogonally polarized. This birefringence is what makes calcite the material of choice for Nicol and Glan-Thompson polarizing prisms in optical instruments.

Q38 What does it mean physically to say that light from a light bulb is "unpolarized"?
2 marksShow Answer ▾

At any instant, the electric field of a light wave always has a definite direction - the concept of an "unpolarized photon" is a misnomer. What "unpolarized" actually means is that the polarization state changes randomly on a timescale far shorter than any practical measurement interval.

A light bulb emits photons from millions of independent atomic emission events per second. Each atom emits a brief, randomly oriented wave train of duration ∼τ∼10 ns\sim\tau \sim 10\,\text{ns}∼τ∼10ns. The superposition of all these wave trains has a polarization direction that fluctuates randomly and continuously. Any real detector averages over many billions of emission events, measuring equal intensity in all polarization directions. No preferred direction is detectable - hence "unpolarized". This is the operational definition: an ideal polarizer oriented at any angle transmits exactly 50% of the incident power.

Q39 The Fresnel reflectance R⊥=[−sin⁡(θi−θt)/sin⁡(θi+θt)]2R_\perp = [-\sin(\theta_i-\theta_t)/\sin(\theta_i+\theta_t)]^2R⊥​=[−sin(θi​−θt​)/sin(θi​+θt​)]2. Calculate R⊥R_\perpR⊥​ at normal incidence on glass with n=1.5n = 1.5n=1.5.
3 marksShow Answer ▾

At normal incidence (θi=θt=0\theta_i = \theta_t = 0θi​=θt​=0), the general Fresnel formula is indeterminate (0/0); taking the small-angle limit using sin⁡θ≈θ\sin\theta \approx \thetasinθ≈θ and applying Snell's law niθi=ntθtn_i\theta_i = n_t\theta_tni​θi​=nt​θt​: R⊥(0)=(nt−nint+ni)2=(1.5−1.01.5+1.0)2=(0.52.5)2=(0.2)2=4%R_\perp(0) = \left(\frac{n_t - n_i}{n_t + n_i}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{1.5-1.0}{1.5+1.0}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{0.5}{2.5}\right)^2 = (0.2)^2 = \boxed{4\%}R⊥​(0)=(nt​+ni​nt​−ni​​)2=(1.5+1.01.5−1.0​)2=(2.50.5​)2=(0.2)2=4%​

This famous 4% reflection at each glass-air interface is the reason why uncoated camera lenses and eyeglasses lose 4% of incident light at each surface. An eight-element lens with 16 surfaces would lose 1−(0.96)16=49%1-(0.96)^{16} = 49\%1−(0.96)16=49% of light - motivating anti-reflection coatings (quarter-wave layers of MgF2_22​ with n≈1.38n\approx1.38n≈1.38) which reduce this to <0.5%<0.5\%<0.5% per surface.

Q40 Explain why H-sheet polaroid absorbs one polarization direction. What material and process is used?
3 marksShow Answer ▾

Material and manufacture: H-sheet polaroid is made from polyvinyl alcohol (PVA). The PVA film is first heated and mechanically stretched, which aligns the long-chain polymer molecules along the stretch direction. The sheet is then impregnated with iodine (I2_22​), which forms conducting polyiodide chains (I3−\text{I}_3^-I3−​, I5−\text{I}_5^-I5−​) that thread along the aligned polymer backbones.

Absorption mechanism: The conducting iodine chains act like microscopic parallel metallic wires. A light wave polarized along the chain direction drives free electrons up and down the chains, just as in a metallic wire antenna. These electrons absorb energy from the light field and dissipate it as heat (via resistive losses). The absorbed polarization component is completely extinguished. Light polarized perpendicular to the chains cannot drive electrons along the chains - it is not absorbed and is transmitted freely. The result is a dichroic polarizer: strongly absorbing for one polarization, transparent for the other. Sheet polarizers achieve extinction ratios of ∼103:1\sim10^3\text{:}1∼103:1 (∼\sim∼30 dB).

Q41 Linearly polarized light passes through three polarizers at 0°, 30°, 60° to the initial polarization. Calculate the final intensity as a fraction of the initial.
3 marksShow Answer ▾

Apply Malus' Law at each stage, using the angle relative to the immediately preceding polarization direction:

After P1 (0° - parallel to initial polarization): I1=I0cos⁡2(0°)=I0I_1 = I_0\cos^2(0°) = I_0I1​=I0​cos2(0°)=I0​

After P2 (30° from P1): I2=I0cos⁡2(30°)=I0�-34I_2 = I_0\cos^2(30°) = I_0\times\frac{3}{4}I2​=I0​cos2(30°)=I0​�-43​

After P3 (30° from P2, since 60°−30°=30°): I_3 = \frac{3I_0}{4}\cos^2(30°) = \frac{3I_0}{4}\times\frac{3}{4} = \boxed{\frac{9}{16}I_0 = 0.5625\,I_0}\]</p> <div class="insight">This is the intermediate-polarizer paradox in partial form: adding more polarizers between crossed axes lets more light through (cf. Q41 in the Advanced section H19). Each intermediate polarizer rotates the polarization state by \(30°, maintaining significant projection onto the next axis.

Q42 Describe circularly polarized light. How is it produced from linearly polarized light?
3 marksShow Answer ▾

Circularly polarized light: In circular polarization, the electric field vector has constant magnitude but rotates at angular frequency ω\omegaω as the wave propagates. The xxx and yyy components are equal in amplitude and exactly π/2\pi/2π/2 out of phase: E(z,t)=E0[x^cos⁡(ωt−kz)±y^sin⁡(ωt−kz)]\mathbf{E}(z,t) = E_0\left[\hat{x}\cos(\omega t - kz) \pm \hat{y}\sin(\omega t - kz)\right]E(z,t)=E0​[x^cos(ωt−kz)±y^​sin(ωt−kz)] The +++ sign gives left circular polarization (LCP), the −-− sign gives right circular polarization (RCP). In the Jones vector formalism: LCP =12(1i)= \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\begin{pmatrix}1\\i\end{pmatrix}=2​1​(1i​), RCP =12(1−i)= \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\begin{pmatrix}1\\-i\end{pmatrix}=2​1​(1−i​).

Production using a quarter-wave plate (QWP): A birefringent crystal cut so that the optical path difference between ordinary and extraordinary rays is exactly λ/4\lambda/4λ/4 (phase difference π/2\pi/2π/2). If linearly polarized light is incident with its polarization direction at 45° to the fast axis of the QWP, the two orthogonal components are equal in amplitude. The QWP then introduces exactly the required π/2\pi/2π/2 phase difference between them - producing circularly polarized output. Changing from 45° to −45° converts LCP to RCP and vice versa.

Topic 4

Light Amplification and the Laser

Q43 List the four key properties of a photon emitted by stimulated emission compared to the stimulating photon.
2 marksShow Answer ▾

A photon produced by stimulated emission is an exact clone of the stimulating photon - it is identical in all four observable properties:

  • Frequency: Same frequency (same photon energy hνh\nuhν).
  • Phase: Same phase - the emitted photon is coherent with the stimulating photon.
  • Direction: Same propagation direction (same momentum ℏk\hbar\mathbf{k}ℏk).
  • Polarization: Same polarization state.

These four properties together define coherent amplification - they explain why a laser beam is so different from a thermal light source. This cloning mechanism is what the acronym LASER captures: Light Amplification by Stimulated Emission of Radiation.

Quantum-mechanical origin: Stimulated emission is the process by which an incoming photon in a well-defined mode (k\mathbf{k}k, polarization, phase) induces the atom to emit into that same mode. The bosonic nature of photons enhances this: the rate of emission into a mode with nnn existing photons is proportional to (n+1)(n+1)(n+1) - photons "prefer" to join populated modes (stimulated) rather than create new ones (spontaneous).
Q44 Explain how an optical cavity converts a laser amplifier into an oscillator. What is the threshold condition?
4 marksShow Answer ▾

From amplifier to oscillator: An inverted gain medium alone is an amplifier: it amplifies any input signal exponentially. However, it still requires a seed signal - spontaneous emission can start the process, but without feedback the light exits after one pass. An optical cavity (two mirrors facing the gain medium) provides optical feedback: a fraction of the output light is reflected back into the gain medium for another pass of amplification. The cavity converts the open amplifier into a regenerative oscillator.

Round-trip threshold condition: For steady-state lasing, the intensity after one complete round trip (gain medium → mirror 1 → gain medium → mirror 2 → back to start) must equal the starting intensity. Letting R1,R2R_1, R_2R1​,R2​ be mirror reflectivities and LrtL_{\text{rt}}Lrt​ be the round-trip fractional loss (scatter, absorption, transmission), the threshold condition is: R1⋅R2⋅(1−Lrt)⋅e2gthlg=1R_1\cdot R_2\cdot(1-L_{\text{rt}})\cdot e^{2g_{\text{th}}l_g} = 1R1​⋅R2​⋅(1−Lrt​)⋅e2gth​lg​=1 or equivalently: round-trip gain = round-trip loss.

Operating regimes:

  • Below threshold: gain < loss; photon number decays each round trip; only incoherent spontaneous emission is observed - wide spectrum, low intensity.
  • At threshold: gain = loss; coherent oscillation just begins to build up.
  • Above threshold: gain > loss initially; photon number grows until gain saturation (see Q48) clamps the inversion back to the threshold value. Output power then increases linearly with pump power - the laser operates on its "lasing curve."

Cavity mode selectivity: The cavity also imposes a geometric constraint: only wavelengths satisfying 2nL=mλ2nL = m\lambda2nL=mλ (where mmm is an integer and nnn is the intracavity refractive index) experience constructive round-trip interference and form longitudinal resonances. All other wavelengths are suppressed by destructive interference after multiple reflections. This is why the laser spectrum narrows so dramatically at threshold.
Q45 A He-Ne laser cavity has L=25 cmL = 25\,\text{cm}L=25cm, λ=632.8 nm\lambda = 632.8\,\text{nm}λ=632.8nm. (a) Calculate the mode number mmm. (b) Calculate the mode spacing Δν\Delta\nuΔν.
3 marksShow Answer ▾

(a) Round-trip condition 2L=mλ2L = m\lambda2L=mλ: m=2Lλ=2�-0.25632.8�-10−9=0.506.328�-10−7≈7.90�-105m = \frac{2L}{\lambda} = \frac{2\times0.25}{632.8\times10^{-9}} = \frac{0.50}{6.328\times10^{-7}} \approx \boxed{7.90\times10^5}m=λ2L​=632.8�-10−92�-0.25​=6.328�-10−70.50​≈7.90�-105​ Nearly 800,000 wavelengths fit in the round trip - the mode number is enormous.

(b) Δν=c2L=3�-1082�-0.25=3�-1080.50=600 MHz\Delta\nu = \frac{c}{2L} = \frac{3\times10^8}{2\times0.25} = \frac{3\times10^8}{0.50} = \boxed{600\,\text{MHz}}Δν=2Lc​=2�-0.253�-108​=0.503�-108​=600MHz​

The He-Ne Doppler gain bandwidth is ΔνD≈1.4 GHz\Delta\nu_D \approx 1.4\,\text{GHz}ΔνD​≈1.4GHz, so approximately 1.4 GHz/600 MHz≈2–31.4\,\text{GHz}/600\,\text{MHz} \approx 2\text{–}31.4GHz/600MHz≈2–3 longitudinal modes fit under the gain curve - consistent with typical He-Ne multi-mode behaviour.

Q46 Describe the three-level optical pumping scheme used in the ruby laser. Identify each transition.
4 marksShow Answer ▾

Three-level scheme of the ruby (Cr3+^{3+}3+:Al2_22​O3_33​) laser:

  • Level 1 → Level 3 (Pump transition): Cr3+^{3+}3+ ions in the ground state 4A2^4\text{A}_24A2​ (level 1) absorb green (~550 nm) and blue (~410 nm) photons from a xenon flashlamp, exciting them to the broad 4T1^4\text{T}_14T1​ and 4T2^4\text{T}_24T2​ pump bands (level 3). The broadness of these bands (due to crystal-field Stark splitting) makes them good absorbers of the flashlamp's broadband emission.
  • Level 3 → Level 2 (Non-radiative fast decay): Extremely rapid phonon emission (∼1 ps\sim 1\,\text{ps}∼1ps) depopulates level 3, transferring Cr3+^{3+}3+ to the metastable 2E^2\text{E}2E doublet (level 2, the upper laser level). This transition is essentially irreversible due to the fast phonon rate.
  • Level 2 → Level 1 (Laser transition): The 2E^2\text{E}2E state is metastable with a very long lifetime (τ≈3 ms\tau \approx 3\,\text{ms}τ≈3ms), allowing population to accumulate. Stimulated emission from level 2 to the ground state level 1 produces the 694.3 nm (R1_11​ line) red laser output.

Key challenge: Since the lower laser level is the ground state (level 1), more than 50% of the total Cr3+^{3+}3+ population must be excited to level 2 before any population inversion is achieved (N2>N1N_2 > N_1N2​>N1​). This requires intense pulsed pumping (flashlamp), which is why ruby lasers are inherently pulsed devices. CW operation is thermally and optically impractical.

Q47 Why is a four-level laser system easier to achieve threshold than a three-level system?
3 marksShow Answer ▾

Three-level system (e.g. ruby): The lower laser level IS the ground state. At thermal equilibrium, virtually all atoms are in the ground state (N1≈NN_1 \approx NN1​≈N). To achieve population inversion (N2>N1N_2 > N_1N2​>N1​), you must excite more than half of all atoms in the medium. The threshold pump power scales with the ground-state population density - typically requiring ∼\sim∼kW of flashlamp power for even modest ruby rods.

Four-level system (e.g. Nd:YAG): The lower laser level is a real excited state (4I11/2^4\text{I}_{11/2}4I11/2​) that lies ∼\sim∼0.25 eV above the ground state - far above thermal energy at room temperature (kT≈0.025 eVkT \approx 0.025\,\text{eV}kT≈0.025eV), so its thermal population is essentially zero: N1′≈0N_{1'} \approx 0N1′​≈0. Moreover, this level has a fast phonon decay rate (τ∼30 ns\tau \sim 30\,\text{ns}τ∼30ns) that keeps it empty. Any small number of atoms N2>0N_2 > 0N2​>0 in the upper laser level (4F3/2^4\text{F}_{3/2}4F3/2​) automatically constitutes a population inversion against the empty lower laser level. Every excited atom contributes to the inversion - no minimum excitation fraction is required. This enormously reduces the pump threshold and enables CW operation at modest pump powers (∼\sim∼watts from a diode laser).

This architectural difference explains why virtually all modern solid-state and diode lasers use four-level or quasi-four-level schemes. The three-level ruby laser, for all its historical importance, is commercially obsolete for most applications.
Q48 What is gain clamping? Explain the feedback mechanism that stabilises laser output power.
3 marksShow Answer ▾

Gain clamping: Above threshold, the steady-state gain of a laser is fixed at exactly the threshold value - equal to round-trip loss - regardless of how hard the gain medium is pumped. This is called gain clamping (or gain saturation).

Negative feedback mechanism: Consider a small perturbation where gain slightly exceeds loss:

  • Photon number in cavity increases.
  • More photons → more stimulated emission → upper-level population N2N_2N2​ decreases faster (gain saturation).
  • Gain decreases back toward threshold.
Conversely, if gain drops below loss:
  • Photon number decreases.
  • Less stimulated emission → N2N_2N2​ recovers under pump excitation.
  • Gain rises back to threshold.
This is a classic negative feedback loop that clamps the inversion at the threshold value. The consequence: increasing pump power above threshold increases output power (photon emission rate), not the inversion or gain. All additional pump energy goes directly into useful laser output - this is why slope efficiency above threshold can be very high (∼\sim∼50–80% in diode lasers).

Q49 A laser cavity L=50 cmL = 50\,\text{cm}L=50cm at 1064 nm. Calculate (a) the mode spacing and (b) the approximate number of modes within a gain bandwidth of FWHM = 120 GHz.
3 marksShow Answer ▾

(a) Δν=c2L=3�-1082�-0.50=3�-1081.0=300 MHz\Delta\nu = \frac{c}{2L} = \frac{3\times10^8}{2\times0.50} = \frac{3\times10^8}{1.0} = \boxed{300\,\text{MHz}}Δν=2Lc​=2�-0.503�-108​=1.03�-108​=300MHz​

(b) Number of modes=ΔνgainΔνmode=120�-109300�-106=400 modes\text{Number of modes} = \frac{\Delta\nu_{\text{gain}}}{\Delta\nu_{\text{mode}}} = \frac{120\times10^9}{300\times10^6} = \boxed{400\,\text{modes}}Number of modes=Δνmode​Δνgain​​=300�-106120�-109​=400modes​

However, a Nd:YAG laser is homogeneously broadened (phonon broadening) - in principle, only one mode lases (the one with highest net gain). In practice, spatial hole burning in a linear cavity allows a few modes to compete. To force true single-mode operation, an intracavity etalon (Fabry-Perot with large FSR) is inserted to select a single mode from the 400 available.

Q50 Explain what amplified spontaneous emission (ASE) is and when it is useful in practice.
2 marksShow Answer ▾

ASE occurs in a gain medium (population inversion present) without a resonant optical cavity. A photon emitted spontaneously in any direction can be amplified by stimulated emission as it propagates through the gain medium, producing a cascade of coherent photons along that direction. The amplification is single-pass only, without the wavelength selectivity of a cavity, so the output spectrum spans the entire gain bandwidth.

Applications:

  • Superluminescent diodes (SLDs): Semiconductor gain chips with angled facets (to prevent cavity feedback). Low coherence length due to broad bandwidth makes them ideal for optical coherence tomography (OCT), where short coherence length gives high depth resolution.
  • Erbium-doped fibre ASE sources: Broadband (∼\sim∼40 nm) near 1550 nm, used as reference light sources for WDM component characterisation.
  • Fibre amplifiers as ASE sources: When the signal is removed from an EDFA, ASE noise can be measured to characterise the amplifier's noise figure.

Q51 In a diode laser, population inversion is achieved differently from a gas laser. Explain the mechanism briefly.
3 marksShow Answer ▾

In a semiconductor diode laser, population inversion is created by electrical injection of carriers rather than optical pumping. A forward-biased p-n junction (or more commonly, a double heterostructure) is used:

  • The forward bias lowers the p-n junction potential barrier, injecting electrons from the n-side into the conduction band of the active region, and injecting holes from the p-side into the valence band.
  • When the carrier density exceeds the transparency threshold, the quasi-Fermi level separation EFc−EFvE_{F_c} - E_{F_v}EFc​​−EFv​​ exceeds the bandgap energy EgE_gEg​. This is the condition for population inversion: there are more electrons in the conduction band than in the valence band at the same wavevector kkk.
  • Stimulated emission occurs when an electron in the conduction band recombines with a hole in the valence band, emitting a photon of energy ≈Eg\approx E_g≈Eg​.

The key advantage: electrical pumping is extraordinarily efficient. Modern double-heterostructure quantum-well diode lasers achieve wall-plug efficiencies exceeding 70% - converting electrical power directly to coherent photons with minimal loss.

Q52 The round-trip condition in a laser cavity is 2L=mλm2L = m\lambda_m2L=mλm​. Re-express as a frequency condition and derive the free spectral range Δν=c/2L\Delta\nu = c/2LΔν=c/2L.
3 marksShow Answer ▾
1Substitute λm=c/νm\lambda_m = c/\nu_mλm​=c/νm​ into 2L=mλm2L = m\lambda_m2L=mλm​: 2L=m⋅cνm⇒νm=mc2L2L = m\cdot\frac{c}{\nu_m} \Rightarrow \nu_m = \frac{mc}{2L}2L=m⋅νm​c​⇒νm​=2Lmc​
2For adjacent modes mmm and m+1m+1m+1: νm+1−νm=(m+1)c2L−mc2L=c2L\nu_{m+1} - \nu_m = \frac{(m+1)c}{2L} - \frac{mc}{2L} = \frac{c}{2L}νm+1​−νm​=2L(m+1)c​−2Lmc​=2Lc​
3ΔνFSR=c2L■\boxed{\Delta\nu_{\text{FSR}} = \frac{c}{2L}}\quad\blacksquareΔνFSR​=2Lc​​■

The FSR is independent of mode number - modes are uniformly spaced in frequency. Equivalently, modes are uniformly spaced in wavelength only approximately; the exact wavelength spacing is Δλ≈λ2/(2L)\Delta\lambda \approx \lambda^2/(2L)Δλ≈λ2/(2L), which increases for longer wavelengths. For a 30 cm He-Ne cavity: FSR = 500 MHz; for a 250 μm semiconductor laser: FSR = 200 GHz.

Q53 Why does lasing at a single frequency naturally occur in a homogeneously broadened laser but not in an inhomogeneously broadened one?
3 marksShow Answer ▾

Homogeneous broadening (e.g. Nd:YAG, phonon mechanism): All active ions are broadened by the same mechanism (phonon interaction) and all ions contribute gain across the same frequency interval. When lasing begins at the cavity mode with highest net gain, stimulated emission depletes the population inversion of all ions simultaneously - because all ions have the same resonance frequency and are coupled to the same gain pool. This global gain saturation removes available gain at all frequencies, preventing any other mode from reaching threshold. Result: single longitudinal mode (in principle; spatial hole burning can modify this in practice).

Inhomogeneous broadening (e.g. He-Ne, Doppler mechanism): Different velocity classes of Ne atoms have different Doppler-shifted resonance frequencies. Each velocity class forms an independent sub-ensemble with its own gain reservoir. Lasing at mode frequency ν1\nu_1ν1​ depletes only the velocity class resonant at ν1\nu_1ν1​ - this is spectral hole burning. Other velocity classes, resonant at ν2,ν3,…\nu_2, \nu_3, \ldotsν2​,ν3​,…, remain saturated only if a cavity mode lies nearby. If the gain bandwidth is wide enough to contain multiple mode spacings, each mode independently depletes its own velocity class and all can lase simultaneously. He-Ne typically oscillates on 2–4 modes.

Q54 Describe qualitatively how laser output power varies with pump current in a diode laser. What characterises laser threshold?
3 marksShow Answer ▾

Below threshold (I<IthI < I_{\text{th}}I<Ith​): Only spontaneous emission occurs. Output power increases slowly and nearly linearly with current, similar to an LED. The emission is spectrally broad (∼\sim∼several nm), incoherent, and diverges in all directions. Slope efficiency is low (∼\sim∼μW/mA).

At threshold (I=IthI = I_{\text{th}}I=Ith​): The gain per round trip equals the total loss. A sharp "kink" or "knee" appears in the power-current (L–I) curve - a sudden change in slope. The spectral output narrows dramatically from broad spontaneous emission to one or a few narrow lasing modes, a change easily observable with a spectrometer.

Above threshold (I>IthI > I_{\text{th}}I>Ith​): Output power rises steeply and linearly with current. The slope efficiency (differential quantum efficiency) is high: ηd=dPd(I⋅V)≈30–80%\eta_d = \frac{dP}{d(I\cdot V)} \approx 30\text{–}80\%ηd​=d(I⋅V)dP​≈30–80% for modern devices. The emission is coherent, narrow-bandwidth, and directional. The laser is now fully operating as a coherent oscillator.

The threshold current IthI_{\text{th}}Ith​ depends strongly on temperature. Empirically: Ith(T)=I0 eT/T0I_{\text{th}}(T) = I_0\,e^{T/T_0}Ith​(T)=I0​eT/T0​ where T0T_0T0​ (characteristic temperature) is 50–150 K for standard InGaAsP lasers. This exponential sensitivity to temperature motivates thermoelectric cooling of telecommunications diode lasers to within ±0.01°C.
Q55 A laser at 780 nm has cavity L=3 mmL = 3\,\text{mm}L=3mm (semiconductor laser). Calculate: (a) mode spacing Δν\Delta\nuΔν, (b) mode spacing Δλ\Delta\lambdaΔλ in nm.
3 marksShow Answer ▾

(a) Δν=c2nL≈c2L=3�-1082�-3�-10−3=3�-1086�-10−3=50 GHz\Delta\nu = \frac{c}{2nL} \approx \frac{c}{2L} = \frac{3\times10^8}{2\times3\times10^{-3}} = \frac{3\times10^8}{6\times10^{-3}} = \boxed{50\,\text{GHz}}Δν=2nLc​≈2Lc​=2�-3�-10−33�-108​=6�-10−33�-108​=50GHz​ (using n=1n=1n=1 as first approximation; in a real semiconductor n≈3.5n \approx 3.5n≈3.5, giving Δν≈14 GHz\Delta\nu \approx 14\,\text{GHz}Δν≈14GHz)

(b) Using the differential relation ∣dν∣=(c/λ2)∣dλ∣|d\nu| = (c/\lambda^2)|d\lambda|∣dν∣=(c/λ2)∣dλ∣: Δλ=λ2 Δνc=(780�-10−9)2�-50�-1093�-108\Delta\lambda = \frac{\lambda^2\,\Delta\nu}{c} = \frac{(780\times10^{-9})^2\times50\times10^9}{3\times10^8}Δλ=cλ2Δν​=3�-108(780�-10−9)2�-50�-109​ =6.08�-10−13�-5�-10103�-108=3.04�-10−23�-108≈0.10 nm= \frac{6.08\times10^{-13}\times5\times10^{10}}{3\times10^8} = \frac{3.04\times10^{-2}}{3\times10^8} \approx \boxed{0.10\,\text{nm}}=3�-1086.08�-10−13�-5�-1010​=3�-1083.04�-10−2​≈0.10nm​ (or equivalently: Δλ=λ2/(2L)=(780 nm)2/6 mm=0.101 nm\Delta\lambda = \lambda^2/(2L) = (780\text{ nm})^2/6\text{ mm} = 0.101\text{ nm}Δλ=λ2/(2L)=(780 nm)2/6 mm=0.101 nm)

Q56 The optical spectrum of a diode laser is measured below and above threshold. Describe the key differences.
2 marksShow Answer ▾

Below threshold: Broad spontaneous emission spectrum, width ∼\sim∼several nm, centred on the bandgap wavelength. The spectral shape follows the convolution of the electron and hole density-of-states in the quantum well. Low intensity. No mode structure visible - emission is incoherent.

Above threshold: Spectrum narrows dramatically to a single mode (or a few Fabry-Perot modes) of width ≪0.1 nm\ll 0.1\,\text{nm}≪0.1nm. Intensity increases by orders of magnitude. The cavity mode spacing Δλ≈λ2/(2nL)\Delta\lambda \approx \lambda^2/(2nL)Δλ≈λ2/(2nL) becomes clearly visible if multiple modes lase. The transition from broad to narrow emission is the clearest observational signature of crossing the laser threshold - the "phase transition" of photonics.

Topic 5

Characteristics of Laser Light

Q57State five key differences between laser light and light from a thermal source (e.g. a light bulb).
3 marksShow Answer ▾
  • Monochromaticity: Laser linewidth can be as narrow as kHz–MHz (e.g. 1 MHz He-Ne). A light bulb emits a thermal continuum spanning hundreds of THz. The spectral purity of a laser enables Doppler velocimetry, spectroscopy, and interferometry that are impossible with broadband sources.
  • Temporal coherence: Laser light maintains phase correlation over long distances - coherence lengths from centimetres (multimode diode) to hundreds of metres (single-mode He-Ne) to kilometres (stabilised ring laser). Thermal light has coherence lengths of micrometres to millimetres.
  • Spatial coherence: A laser emits from a single spatial mode - the wavefronts are smooth and uniform across the beam. A filament bulb emits from many incoherent points simultaneously; different parts of the source have no phase relationship.
  • Directionality: Laser divergence is diffraction-limited: θ≈λ/πw0∼0.1–1 mrad\theta \approx \lambda/\pi w_0 \sim 0.1\text{–}1\,\text{mrad}θ≈λ/πw0​∼0.1–1mrad. A bulb radiates uniformly into 4π4\pi4π steradians; the intensity falls as 1/r21/r^21/r2. A laser beam can propagate to the Moon with a spot ∼\sim∼100 m diameter.
  • Spectral energy density: A 1 mW He-Ne laser has spectral energy density ρ(ν)≈10−13 J m−3 Hz−1\rho(\nu) \approx 10^{-13}\,\text{J\,m}^{-3}\,\text{Hz}^{-1}ρ(ν)≈10−13Jm−3Hz−1 - approximately 107�-10^7\times107�- greater than the Sun at the same total intensity (Q59). This is why lasers drive nonlinear optical processes that thermal sources cannot.
Q58A 5 mW He-Ne laser has beam diameter 1.5 mm. Calculate: (a) beam intensity W m−2^{-2}−2, (b) energy density ρ\rhoρ J m−3^{-3}−3.
3 marksShow Answer ▾

(a) A=π(d/2)2=π(0.75�-10−3)2=1.767�-10−6 m2A = \pi(d/2)^2 = \pi(0.75\times10^{-3})^2 = 1.767\times10^{-6}\,\text{m}^2A=π(d/2)2=π(0.75�-10−3)2=1.767�-10−6m2. I=PA=5�-10−31.767�-10−6=2830 W m−2I = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{5\times10^{-3}}{1.767\times10^{-6}} = \boxed{2830\,\text{W\,m}^{-2}}I=AP​=1.767�-10−65�-10−3​=2830Wm−2​

(b) Using I=cρI = c\rhoI=cρ: ρ=Ic=28303�-108=9.43�-10−6 J m−3\rho = \frac{I}{c} = \frac{2830}{3\times10^8} = \boxed{9.43\times10^{-6}\,\text{J\,m}^{-3}}ρ=cI​=3�-1082830​=9.43�-10−6Jm−3​

Q59Compare spectral energy densities ρ(ν)\rho(\nu)ρ(ν) of a He-Ne laser (I=300 W m−2I=300\,\text{W\,m}^{-2}I=300Wm−2, ΔνG=107 Hz\Delta\nu_G = 10^7\,\text{Hz}ΔνG​=107Hz) and the Sun (I=700 W m−2I=700\,\text{W\,m}^{-2}I=700Wm−2, Δν≈5�-1014 Hz\Delta\nu \approx 5\times10^{14}\,\text{Hz}Δν≈5�-1014Hz).
3 marksShow Answer ▾

Since ρ(ν)=I/(c⋅Δν)\rho(\nu) = I/(c\cdot\Delta\nu)ρ(ν)=I/(c⋅Δν):

ρHe-Ne=3003�-108�-107=1.0�-10−13 J m−3 Hz−1\rho_{\text{He-Ne}} = \frac{300}{3\times10^8\times10^7} = 1.0\times10^{-13}\,\text{J\,m}^{-3}\,\text{Hz}^{-1}ρHe-Ne​=3�-108�-107300​=1.0�-10−13Jm−3Hz−1

ρSun=7003�-108�-5�-1014=4.67�-10−21 J m−3 Hz−1\rho_{\text{Sun}} = \frac{700}{3\times10^8\times5\times10^{14}} = 4.67\times10^{-21}\,\text{J\,m}^{-3}\,\text{Hz}^{-1}ρSun​=3�-108�-5�-1014700​=4.67�-10−21Jm−3Hz−1

ρHe-NeρSun=1.0�-10−134.67�-10−21≈2.1�-107\frac{\rho_{\text{He-Ne}}}{\rho_{\text{Sun}}} = \frac{1.0\times10^{-13}}{4.67\times10^{-21}} \approx \boxed{2.1\times10^7}ρSun​ρHe-Ne​​=4.67�-10−211.0�-10−13​≈2.1�-107​

The He-Ne laser has 21 million times higher spectral energy density than the Sun, despite being 2�- weaker in total intensity. This is why laser light is so uniquely effective at driving stimulated emission, nonlinear optical processes, and precision spectroscopy - it is the concentration of power into a vanishingly narrow bandwidth that matters, not merely the total power.

Q60Define temporal coherence time tct_ctc​ and coherence length lcl_clc​. Calculate both for a laser with Δν=500 kHz\Delta\nu = 500\,\text{kHz}Δν=500kHz.
3 marksShow Answer ▾

Coherence time tct_ctc​: The time interval over which the phase of the wave is predictable. Equivalently, it is the maximum time delay τ\tauτ for which ⟨E(t)E�-(t+τ)⟩≠0\langle E(t)E^*(t+\tau)\rangle \neq 0⟨E(t)E�-(t+τ)⟩=0. From the Wiener-Khinchin theorem, tc≈1/Δνt_c \approx 1/\Delta\nutc​≈1/Δν (the inverse of the spectral linewidth).

Coherence length lcl_clc​: The spatial equivalent - the maximum path length difference ΔL\Delta LΔL for which two split beams still interfere: lc=c⋅tcl_c = c\cdot t_clc​=c⋅tc​.

tc=1Δν=1500�-103=2�-10−6 s=2 μst_c = \frac{1}{\Delta\nu} = \frac{1}{500\times10^3} = \boxed{2\times10^{-6}\,\text{s} = 2\,\mu\text{s}}tc​=Δν1​=500�-1031​=2�-10−6s=2μs​ lc=c⋅tc=3�-108�-2�-10−6=600 ml_c = c\cdot t_c = 3\times10^8\times2\times10^{-6} = \boxed{600\,\text{m}}lc​=c⋅tc​=3�-108�-2�-10−6=600m​
A 600 m coherence length means interferometric fringe contrast is maintained for arm-length differences up to 600 m - sufficient for building-scale interferometry. Compare to a mercury lamp (Δν∼109 Hz\Delta\nu \sim 10^9\,\text{Hz}Δν∼109Hz, lc∼30 cml_c \sim 30\,\text{cm}lc​∼30cm) or a single-mode fibre laser (Δν∼1 kHz\Delta\nu \sim 1\,\text{kHz}Δν∼1kHz, lc∼300 kml_c \sim 300\,\text{km}lc​∼300km).
Q61Explain what laser speckle is and what it demonstrates about laser coherence.
3 marksShow Answer ▾

Laser speckle is the granular, high-contrast bright-and-dark pattern observed when coherent light scatters off a rough surface or propagates through a disordered medium (such as a white wall or frosted glass).

Origin: A rough surface has height variations much larger than λ\lambdaλ. Each microscopic surface element scatters a wavelet to the observation point with a different path length (and hence a different phase). Because laser light is spatially and temporally coherent, all these wavelets have a definite phase relationship to each other - they can interfere. At a given observation point, the sum of all contributions may be constructive (bright speckle) or destructive (dark speckle), depending on the exact path-length distribution to that point. Moving even a fraction of λ\lambdaλ changes the relative phases and shifts the speckle pattern.
