Thermal (incoherent) light produces no speckle because the random phases of different emission events wash out any interference: the intensities from different surface elements simply add, giving uniform illumination. Speckle is therefore direct visual evidence of high spatial coherence. Its statistics (speckle contrast = standard deviation/mean = 1 for fully developed speckle) quantify the coherence of the illuminating field.

Q62A laser has beam waist w0=0.3 mmw_0 = 0.3\,\text{mm}w0​=0.3mm at λ=532 nm\lambda = 532\,\text{nm}λ=532nm. (a) Calculate half-angle divergence θ\thetaθ. (b) Beam radius 10 m from the waist.
3 marksShow Answer ▾

(a) For a Gaussian beam, the far-field half-angle divergence is: θ=λπw0=532�-10−9π�-0.3�-10−3=5.32�-10−79.42�-10−4=5.65�-10−4 rad≈0.565 mrad\theta = \frac{\lambda}{\pi w_0} = \frac{532\times10^{-9}}{\pi\times0.3\times10^{-3}} = \frac{5.32\times10^{-7}}{9.42\times10^{-4}} = \boxed{5.65\times10^{-4}\,\text{rad} \approx 0.565\,\text{mrad}}θ=πw0​λ​=π�-0.3�-10−3532�-10−9​=9.42�-10−45.32�-10−7​=5.65�-10−4rad≈0.565mrad​

(b) Far from the waist, w(z)≈w0+θzw(z) \approx w_0 + \theta zw(z)≈w0​+θz: w(10)≈0.3�-10−3+5.65�-10−4�-10=0.3 mm+5.65 mm≈5.95 mmw(10) \approx 0.3\times10^{-3} + 5.65\times10^{-4}\times10 = 0.3\,\text{mm} + 5.65\,\text{mm} \approx \boxed{5.95\,\text{mm}}w(10)≈0.3�-10−3+5.65�-10−4�-10=0.3mm+5.65mm≈5.95mm​

The product w0⋅θ=λ/πw_0\cdot\theta = \lambda/\piw0​⋅θ=λ/π is invariant for a perfect Gaussian beam - the beam parameter product (BPP). It represents the fundamental diffraction limit: the more tightly focused the beam (smaller w0w_0w0​), the more rapidly it must diverge. Real beams with beam quality M2>1M^2 > 1M2>1 diverge faster by a factor M2M^2M2.

Q63A 100 W bulb (1% efficiency) is 2 m away. Calculate: (a) the intensity, (b) photons/second entering a 5 mm pupil at λ=600 nm\lambda = 600\,\text{nm}λ=600nm.
4 marksShow Answer ▾

(a) Optical power: P=0.01�-100=1 WP = 0.01\times100 = 1\,\text{W}P=0.01�-100=1W. Intensity at distance r=2 mr=2\,\text{m}r=2m: I=P4πr2=14π�-4=150.3=0.0199 W m−2I = \frac{P}{4\pi r^2} = \frac{1}{4\pi\times4} = \frac{1}{50.3} = \boxed{0.0199\,\text{W\,m}^{-2}}I=4πr2P​=4π�-41​=50.31​=0.0199Wm−2​

(b) Pupil area: A=π(2.5�-10−3)2=1.963�-10−5 m2A = \pi(2.5\times10^{-3})^2 = 1.963\times10^{-5}\,\text{m}^2A=π(2.5�-10−3)2=1.963�-10−5m2. Power entering eye: Peye=0.0199�-1.963�-10−5=3.91�-10−7 WP_{\text{eye}} = 0.0199\times1.963\times10^{-5} = 3.91\times10^{-7}\,\text{W}Peye​=0.0199�-1.963�-10−5=3.91�-10−7W. Energy per photon: E=hc/λ=3.315�-10−19 JE = hc/\lambda = 3.315\times10^{-19}\,\text{J}E=hc/λ=3.315�-10−19J. Photons/s=3.91�-10−73.315�-10−19=1.18�-1012 photons s−1\text{Photons/s} = \frac{3.91\times10^{-7}}{3.315\times10^{-19}} = \boxed{1.18\times10^{12}\,\text{photons\,s}^{-1}}Photons/s=3.315�-10−193.91�-10−7​=1.18�-1012photonss−1​

Even at this low intensity (101210^{12}1012 photons/s), the eye is far from the single-photon regime. The rod cells of the dark-adapted eye can detect as few as ∼\sim∼10 photons, but under daylight conditions the pupil receives billions per second.

Q64What is the relationship between temporal coherence length and spectral linewidth? Why does spontaneous emission have such a short coherence time?
3 marksShow Answer ▾

By the Wiener-Khinchin theorem, the temporal coherence function Γ(τ)=⟨E�-(t)E(t+τ)⟩\Gamma(\tau) = \langle E^*(t)E(t+\tau)\rangleΓ(τ)=⟨E�-(t)E(t+τ)⟩ and the power spectral density S(ν)S(\nu)S(ν) are a Fourier transform pair. The coherence time tc≈1/Δνt_c \approx 1/\Delta\nutc​≈1/Δν and coherence length lc=c/Δνl_c = c/\Delta\nulc​=c/Δν - so narrower linewidth always means longer coherence.

Spontaneous emission and short coherence: Each atom emits an independent photon wave packet of duration ∼τ\sim\tau∼τ (the excited-state lifetime, ∼\sim∼10 ns). After this time, the wave packet ends abruptly and the next atom emits with a completely random phase - there is no phase relationship between successive emissions. The autocorrelation function therefore decays in ∼τ\sim\tau∼τ, giving a coherence time tc≈τt_c \approx \tautc​≈τ and correspondingly broad linewidth Δν≈A21=1/τ\Delta\nu \approx A_{21} = 1/\tauΔν≈A21​=1/τ. This is the natural (Lorentzian) linewidth - the minimum broadening for any transition.

Q65Explain gain saturation: why does increasing pump power above threshold increase output power but not population inversion?
3 marksShow Answer ▾

Above threshold, the intracavity photon number is large and growing. More photons trigger more stimulated emission, which removes atoms from the upper laser level at a rate proportional to both photon number and inversion. This is gain saturation: the gain decreases as the photon number increases. The laser reaches a steady state where gain exactly equals loss - the threshold condition. The inversion is clamped at the threshold value ΔNth\Delta N_{\text{th}}ΔNth​.

Any additional pump power above threshold cannot further increase ΔN\Delta NΔN, because the enhanced stimulated emission immediately depletes the extra inversion. Instead, all the additional pump power goes into producing more intracavity photons (and hence more output power). This is why the L-I curve (output power vs pump current) has a sharp linear slope above threshold: every additional milliamp beyond threshold produces a fixed number of additional photons per second - the slope efficiency ηd\eta_dηd​.

Q66Why does a semiconductor diode laser beam diverge much more rapidly than a He-Ne laser beam?
3 marksShow Answer ▾

Gaussian beam divergence: θ≈λ/(πw0)\theta \approx \lambda/(\pi w_0)θ≈λ/(πw0​). The divergence is inversely proportional to the beam waist - a smaller emitting aperture produces a larger divergence angle.

He-Ne laser: Beam waist w0∼0.5–1 mmw_0 \sim 0.5\text{–}1\,\text{mm}w0​∼0.5–1mm. Divergence: θ∼λ/(πw0)≈0.2–0.4 mrad\theta \sim \lambda/(\pi w_0) \approx 0.2\text{–}0.4\,\text{mrad}θ∼λ/(πw0​)≈0.2–0.4mrad - nearly perfectly collimated.

Semiconductor diode laser: The gain region is a quantum well of thickness ∼\sim∼10 nm, within a waveguide structure of total height ∼\sim∼1 μm and width ∼\sim∼3–5 μm. The mode field diameter is ∼\sim∼1 μm (perpendicular to junction) by ∼\sim∼3 μm (parallel). Using w0≈0.5 μmw_0 \approx 0.5\,\mu\text{m}w0​≈0.5μm: θ≈λ/(π�-0.5 μm)≈200 mrad≈11°\theta \approx \lambda/(\pi\times0.5\,\mu\text{m}) \approx 200\,\text{mrad} \approx 11°θ≈λ/(π�-0.5μm)≈200mrad≈11° in the fast axis. The far-field pattern is an elliptical cone with divergences of ∼\sim∼30° (fast axis) by ∼\sim∼10° (slow axis) - so broad that a fast aspheric lens must be used within a few mm of the facet to collimate it.

Q67A Michelson interferometer uses a laser with Δν=2 GHz\Delta\nu = 2\,\text{GHz}Δν=2GHz. At what path length difference do fringes disappear?
2 marksShow Answer ▾

Fringes disappear when the path length difference exceeds the coherence length: lc=cΔν=3�-1082�-109=0.15 m=15 cml_c = \frac{c}{\Delta\nu} = \frac{3\times10^8}{2\times10^9} = \boxed{0.15\,\text{m} = 15\,\text{cm}}lc​=Δνc​=2�-1093�-108​=0.15m=15cm​

In a Michelson interferometer the path difference is twice the arm-length difference, so one arm must be extended by ∼7.5 cm\sim7.5\,\text{cm}∼7.5cm relative to the other for fringes to disappear. This sets the maximum OPD (optical path difference) for useful interference - relevant for white-light interferometry and OCT where the coherence length defines the axial resolution.

Q68Why does the frequency spectrum of a laser differ below and above threshold?
2 marksShow Answer ▾

Below threshold: Spontaneous emission dominates. Photons are emitted independently across the full gain bandwidth (∼\sim∼GHz to THz), with random phase and direction. The spectrum is broad, smooth, and Gaussian- or Lorentzian-shaped - a featureless glow spread over the entire atomic or semiconductor linewidth.

Above threshold: Stimulated emission at specific cavity resonance frequencies depletes the available inversion. For a homogeneously broadened laser, one (or a few) cavity modes capture virtually all the available gain. The spectrum narrows dramatically to one or a few extremely sharp peaks (width ≪0.1 nm\ll0.1\,\text{nm}≪0.1nm) at the lasing frequencies, with the rest of the spontaneous emission background suppressed by orders of magnitude. The Schawlow-Townes linewidth of a single lasing mode can be as narrow as mHz for stabilised ring lasers.

Q69Starting from the definition of intensity (W m−2^{-2}−2), derive I=cρI = c\rhoI=cρ.
3 marksShow Answer ▾
1Consider a beam of photon number density nnn (photons m−3^{-3}−3). All photons travel at speed ccc.
2In time dtdtdt, all photons within a cylinder of length c dtc\,dtcdt and area AAA cross the plane. Number crossing: dN=n⋅A⋅c dtdN = n\cdot A\cdot c\,dtdN=n⋅A⋅cdt.
3Energy crossing per unit time per unit area (= intensity): I=dN⋅hνA dt=n⋅c⋅hνI = \frac{dN\cdot h\nu}{A\,dt} = n\cdot c\cdot h\nuI=AdtdN⋅hν​=n⋅c⋅hν
4Since energy density ρ=n⋅hν\rho = n\cdot h\nuρ=n⋅hν: I=cρ■\boxed{I = c\rho}\quad\blacksquareI=cρ​■
Q70The Sun's total output is ∼2�-1026 W\sim2\times10^{26}\,\text{W}∼2�-1026W. Earth's orbital radius is ∼1.5�-1011 m\sim1.5\times10^{11}\,\text{m}∼1.5�-1011m. Calculate the solar constant.
2 marksShow Answer ▾
I=P4πd2=2�-10264π�-(1.5�-1011)2=2�-10264π�-2.25�-1022=2�-10262.827�-1023≈707 W m−2I = \frac{P}{4\pi d^2} = \frac{2\times10^{26}}{4\pi\times(1.5\times10^{11})^2} = \frac{2\times10^{26}}{4\pi\times2.25\times10^{22}} = \frac{2\times10^{26}}{2.827\times10^{23}} \approx \boxed{707\,\text{W\,m}^{-2}}I=4πd2P​=4π�-(1.5�-1011)22�-1026​=4π�-2.25�-10222�-1026​=2.827�-10232�-1026​≈707Wm−2​

The measured solar constant is ≈1361 W m−2\approx1361\,\text{W\,m}^{-2}≈1361Wm−2; the discrepancy here is because the accepted solar luminosity is 3.83�-1026 W3.83\times10^{26}\,\text{W}3.83�-1026W. Using the given 2�-1026 W2\times10^{26}\,\text{W}2�-1026W gives 707 W m−2^{-2}−2. The solar constant sets the maximum available power for solar cells (∼\sim∼200 W m−2^{-2}−2 practically, after atmospheric absorption and angle effects).

Topic 6

Fibre Optics and Structures

Q71Derive the critical angle θc\theta_cθc​ from Snell's law. For a glass-air interface (nglass=1.50n_{\text{glass}}=1.50nglass​=1.50), calculate θc\theta_cθc​.
3 marksShow Answer ▾

Total internal reflection (TIR) occurs when the refracted angle θt\theta_tθt​ reaches 90° - the refracted ray travels along the interface. Applying Snell's law at this limiting case: n1sin⁡θc=n2sin⁡90°=n2⇒sin⁡θc=n2n1n_1\sin\theta_c = n_2\sin 90° = n_2 \Rightarrow \sin\theta_c = \frac{n_2}{n_1}n1​sinθc​=n2​sin90°=n2​⇒sinθc​=n1​n2​​

For glass (n1=1.50n_1=1.50n1​=1.50) to air (n2=1.00n_2=1.00n2​=1.00): sin⁡θc=1.001.50=0.667⇒θc=arcsin⁡(0.667)=41.8°\sin\theta_c = \frac{1.00}{1.50} = 0.667 \Rightarrow \theta_c = \arcsin(0.667) = \boxed{41.8°}sinθc​=1.501.00​=0.667⇒θc​=arcsin(0.667)=41.8°​

Any ray striking the interface at θi>41.8°\theta_i > 41.8°θi​>41.8° undergoes TIR - 100% reflectance with no energy loss (for a perfect interface). This is the foundational principle of fibre optic waveguiding. The evanescent wave (Q73) still penetrates the second medium exponentially, but carries no time-averaged energy flux away from the glass.

Q72A step-index fibre has nf=1.52n_f=1.52nf​=1.52 (core) and nc=1.50n_c=1.50nc​=1.50 (cladding). Calculate: (a) critical angle, (b) numerical aperture NA, (c) acceptance angle in air.
4 marksShow Answer ▾

(a) sin⁡θc=nc/nf=1.50/1.52=0.9868⇒θc=arcsin⁡(0.9868)=80.6°\sin\theta_c = n_c/n_f = 1.50/1.52 = 0.9868 \Rightarrow \theta_c = \arcsin(0.9868) = \boxed{80.6°}sinθc​=nc​/nf​=1.50/1.52=0.9868⇒θc​=arcsin(0.9868)=80.6°​

(b) NA=nf2−nc2=1.522−1.502=2.3104−2.2500=0.0604=0.246\text{NA} = \sqrt{n_f^2 - n_c^2} = \sqrt{1.52^2 - 1.50^2} = \sqrt{2.3104 - 2.2500} = \sqrt{0.0604} = \boxed{0.246}NA=nf2​−nc2​​=1.522−1.502​=2.3104−2.2500​=0.0604​=0.246​

(c) sin⁡θaccept=NA/nair=0.246⇒θaccept=arcsin⁡(0.246)=14.2°\sin\theta_{\text{accept}} = \text{NA}/n_{\text{air}} = 0.246 \Rightarrow \theta_{\text{accept}} = \arcsin(0.246) = \boxed{14.2°}sinθaccept​=NA/nair​=0.246⇒θaccept​=arcsin(0.246)=14.2°​

The acceptance cone has a half-angle of 14.2°. Light entering at angles beyond this will strike the core-cladding interface at less than the critical angle and refract out - it is not guided. The NA = 0.246 is typical of multimode telecommunications fibre.

Q73What is the evanescent wave in TIR? Describe its key properties and explain why it carries no energy away from the glass.
4 marksShow Answer ▾

What it is: Although TIR results in total reflection, Maxwell's equations require that the electromagnetic field is continuous across the interface - it cannot simply vanish at the boundary. The solution in the less-dense medium (e.g. air) takes the form of an evanescent wave: a field that propagates along the interface but decays exponentially perpendicular to it:

E(x,z,t)=E0 e−x/x0 ei(kzz−ωt)E(x,z,t) = E_0\,e^{-x/x_0}\,e^{i(k_z z - \omega t)}E(x,z,t)=E0​e−x/x0​ei(kz​z−ωt)

where xxx is the distance from the interface into the second medium and the penetration depth x0=λ2πn12sin⁡2θi−n22x_0 = \frac{\lambda}{2\pi\sqrt{n_1^2\sin^2\theta_i - n_2^2}}x0​=2πn12​sin2θi​−n22​​λ​ is typically sub-wavelength (∼50–200 nm\sim50\text{–}200\,\text{nm}∼50–200nm for visible light).

Key properties:

  • Propagates along the boundary (parallel to the interface), not into the second medium.
  • Amplitude decays exponentially with distance from interface - localised within one wavelength of the surface.
  • The time-averaged Poynting vector ⟨S⟩=12Re(E�-H�-)\langle\mathbf{S}\rangle = \frac{1}{2}\text{Re}(\mathbf{E}\times\mathbf{H}^*)⟨S⟩=21​Re(E�-H�-) has zero component perpendicular to the interface: ⟨Sx⟩=0\langle S_x\rangle = 0⟨Sx​⟩=0. No net energy flows across the boundary.
  • The xxx-component of the Poynting vector oscillates between positive and negative over each optical cycle - energy flows into and out of the second medium equally, with zero net transport.

Evanescent coupling and quantum tunnelling: If a second piece of glass is brought within ∼x0\sim x_0∼x0​ of the interface (frustrated TIR), the evanescent field overlaps with the second medium's propagating modes - light "tunnels" across the gap. This is the optical analogue of quantum mechanical tunnelling through a classically forbidden region: the wavefunctions have the same e−κxe^{-\kappa x}e−κx form. Frustrated TIR is used in fibre directional couplers, optical biosensors (TIRF microscopy), and evanescent-field spectroscopy.
Q74Why does bending an optical fibre cause additional loss? At what point does TIR fail?
2 marksShow Answer ▾

A guided ray bounces at the core-cladding interface at angle θi>θc\theta_i > \theta_cθi​>θc​. When the fibre is bent, the local normal to the curved interface tilts relative to the ray direction. For a bend of radius RRR, rays on the outer edge of the curve strike the interface at a reduced effective angle of incidence. When the bend radius is small enough that this effective angle drops below θc\theta_cθc​, the ray is no longer totally internally reflected - it refracts out of the core and the guided power is lost.

Macrobending loss becomes significant when R≲Rc≈3nf2λ4π(nf2−nc2)3/2R \lesssim R_c \approx \frac{3n_f^2\lambda}{4\pi(n_f^2-n_c^2)^{3/2}}R≲Rc​≈4π(nf2​−nc2​)3/23nf2​λ​. For standard SMF-28 fibre (bend-insensitive version), Rc≈10 mmR_c \approx 10\,\text{mm}Rc​≈10mm; bending tightly (e.g. wrapping around a pencil) induces measurable loss and can permanently damage the fibre. Bend-insensitive fibres use a trench index profile to push light more tightly into the core.

Q75Define the V-number. A fibre has a=4 μma=4\,\mu\text{m}a=4μm, NA=0.12=0.12=0.12, λ=1300 nm\lambda=1300\,\text{nm}λ=1300nm. (a) Calculate VVV. (b) Single-mode? (c) Maximum aaa for single-mode at this λ\lambdaλ.
4 marksShow Answer ▾

The V-number (normalised frequency) encapsulates the waveguide's mode-supporting capacity: V=2πa⋅NAλV = \frac{2\pi a\cdot\text{NA}}{\lambda}V=λ2πa⋅NA​

(a) V=2π�-4�-10−6�-0.121300�-10−9=2π�-4.8�-10−71.3�-10−6=2.32V = \frac{2\pi\times4\times10^{-6}\times0.12}{1300\times10^{-9}} = \frac{2\pi\times4.8\times10^{-7}}{1.3\times10^{-6}} = \boxed{2.32}V=1300�-10−92π�-4�-10−6�-0.12​=1.3�-10−62π�-4.8�-10−7​=2.32​

(b) Since V=2.32<2.405V = 2.32 < 2.405V=2.32<2.405 (the first zero of J0J_0J0​, the cutoff for the LP11_{11}11​ mode), the fibre supports only the fundamental LP01_{01}01​ mode. ✓ Single-mode.

(c) For single-mode: V<2.405⇒a<2.405λ2π⋅NA=2.405�-1300�-10−92π�-0.12=4.15 μmV < 2.405 \Rightarrow a < \frac{2.405\lambda}{2\pi\cdot\text{NA}} = \frac{2.405\times1300\times10^{-9}}{2\pi\times0.12} = \boxed{4.15\,\mu\text{m}}V<2.405⇒a<2π⋅NA2.405λ​=2π�-0.122.405�-1300�-10−9​=4.15μm​

Q76Derive NA =nf2−nc2=\sqrt{n_f^2-n_c^2}=nf2​−nc2​​. Start from the geometry at the fibre end-face.
5 marksShow Answer ▾
1At the fibre end-face (air|core), Snell's law: sin⁡θi=nfsin⁡θt\sin\theta_i = n_f\sin\theta_tsinθi​=nf​sinθt​
2For a ray to be guided, it must undergo TIR at the core-cladding interface. The critical condition is exactly at θc\theta_cθc​: sin⁡θc=nc/nf\sin\theta_c = n_c/n_fsinθc​=nc​/nf​
3Geometry at the end-face: if the ray refracts to angle θt\theta_tθt​ inside the core, it hits the side wall at (90°−θt)(90°-\theta_t)(90°−θt​) to the normal. For this to equal θc\theta_cθc​: θt=90°−θc\theta_t = 90°-\theta_cθt​=90°−θc​, so sin⁡θt=cos⁡θc\sin\theta_t = \cos\theta_csinθt​=cosθc​.
4cos⁡θc=1−sin⁡2θc=1−nc2/nf2=nf2−nc2nf\cos\theta_c = \sqrt{1-\sin^2\theta_c} = \sqrt{1-n_c^2/n_f^2} = \frac{\sqrt{n_f^2-n_c^2}}{n_f}cosθc​=1−sin2θc​​=1−nc2​/nf2​​=nf​nf2​−nc2​​​
5Substituting into step 1: sin⁡θi=nf⋅sin⁡θt=nf⋅cos⁡θc=nf⋅nf2−nc2nf=nf2−nc2\sin\theta_i = n_f\cdot\sin\theta_t = n_f\cdot\cos\theta_c = n_f\cdot\frac{\sqrt{n_f^2-n_c^2}}{n_f} = \sqrt{n_f^2-n_c^2}sinθi​=nf​⋅sinθt​=nf​⋅cosθc​=nf​⋅nf​nf2​−nc2​​​=nf2​−nc2​​. Since NA ≡sin⁡θi\equiv \sin\theta_i≡sinθi​: NA=nf2−nc2■\boxed{\text{NA} = \sqrt{n_f^2-n_c^2}}\quad\blacksquareNA=nf2​−nc2​​​■
Q77A multimode fibre has V=25V=25V=25 at 850 nm. Estimate the number of supported modes.
2 marksShow Answer ▾

Number of modes≈V22=2522=6252=312 modes\text{Number of modes} \approx \frac{V^2}{2} = \frac{25^2}{2} = \frac{625}{2} = \boxed{312\,\text{modes}}Number of modes≈2V2​=2252​=2625​=312modes​

This approximation holds well for V≫2.405V \gg 2.405V≫2.405. Each mode propagates at a slightly different group velocity, causing modal dispersion (Q89). For 312 modes, the differential group delay limits bandwidth severely - modern optical fibre communications exclusively uses single-mode fibre (V<2.405V < 2.405V<2.405) to eliminate this.

Q78Explain what cladding modes are and why they do not propagate far along the fibre.
2 marksShow Answer ▾

Cladding modes are light rays that enter the cladding region directly (bypassing TIR at the core-cladding interface) and are guided by TIR at the outer cladding-coating boundary. They carry no signal information but can confuse power measurements near the fibre input. They are rapidly extinguished within a few centimetres because the outer cladding-coating interface is lossy: the plastic coating has a high absorption coefficient, the outer surface is rough (scattering), and any imperfection allows light to refract out. Mode strippers (index-matching fluid applied to stripped fibre) are used to deliberately remove cladding modes in measurement setups.

Q79If the laser beam diameter is D=3 mmD=3\,\text{mm}D=3mm and fibre NA=0.15=0.15=0.15, calculate the required coupling lens focal length.
2 marksShow Answer ▾

The lens must focus the collimated beam to a cone angle matching the fibre NA. For small angles, NA≈θ=(D/2)/f\text{NA} \approx \theta = (D/2)/fNA≈θ=(D/2)/f: f=D/2NA=1.5�-10−30.15=10 mmf = \frac{D/2}{\text{NA}} = \frac{1.5\times10^{-3}}{0.15} = \boxed{10\,\text{mm}}f=NAD/2​=0.151.5�-10−3​=10mm​

This is a short-focal-length aspheric lens - the standard coupling optic for fibre pigtailing. Matching the beam cone to NA is critical: an overfilled fibre excites higher-order modes (causing modal noise in multimode fibre); an underfilled fibre wastes coupling efficiency.

Q80Explain Fermat's principle and how it leads to the laws of geometrical optics.
4 marksShow Answer ▾

Fermat's principle: Light travels between two points along the path for which the optical path length L=∫n ds\mathcal{L} = \int n\,dsL=∫nds is stationary (typically a minimum, but may be a maximum or saddle point in degenerate cases). The optical path length n dsn\,dsnds accounts for the fact that light propagates more slowly in a dense medium, and the physically traversed path is weighted by the local refractive index.

Why stationary phase? Feynman's path-integral (sum-over-histories) picture provides the deepest explanation (Feynman Lectures Vol. I, Ch. 26; QED, Ch. 1–2): light simultaneously explores all possible paths between two points, each contributing a phasor eiϕ=ei2πL/λe^{i\phi} = e^{i2\pi\mathcal{L}/\lambda}eiϕ=ei2πL/λ. For paths far from the stationary-phase path, neighbouring paths have rapidly varying phases and cancel by destructive interference. Near the stationary path, phases vary slowly - all nearby paths have nearly the same L\mathcal{L}L and hence nearly the same phase - they add constructively. The observed ray is therefore the stationary-phase path, where neighbouring paths reinforce.

Derivation of Snell's law: Consider a ray from point A in medium n1n_1n1​ to point B in medium n2n_2n2​. The total optical path as a function of the interface crossing point xxx is: L(x)=n1a2+x2+n2b2+(d−x)2\mathcal{L}(x) = n_1\sqrt{a^2+x^2} + n_2\sqrt{b^2+(d-x)^2}L(x)=n1​a2+x2​+n2​b2+(d−x)2​ Setting dL/dx=0d\mathcal{L}/dx = 0dL/dx=0: n1xa2+x2−n2d−xb2+(d−x)2=0n_1\frac{x}{\sqrt{a^2+x^2}} - n_2\frac{d-x}{\sqrt{b^2+(d-x)^2}} = 0n1​a2+x2​x​−n2​b2+(d−x)2​d−x​=0, which gives n1sin⁡θ1=n2sin⁡θ2n_1\sin\theta_1 = n_2\sin\theta_2n1​sinθ1​=n2​sinθ2​ - Snell's law. ∎

Q81Explain the connection between the evanescent wave in TIR and quantum mechanical tunnelling.
2 marksShow Answer ▾

The evanescent field in TIR decays as E∝e−x/x0E \propto e^{-x/x_0}E∝e−x/x0​ into the forbidden region - identical in mathematical form to the quantum wavefunction ψ∝e−κx\psi \propto e^{-\kappa x}ψ∝e−κx inside a classically forbidden potential barrier (κ=2m(V−E)/ℏ\kappa = \sqrt{2m(V-E)}/\hbarκ=2m(V−E)​/ℏ). In both cases, the field is non-zero in the classically forbidden region despite carrying no net energy flux (for TIR) or probability current (for a standing-wave solution).

Frustrated TIR (light tunnelling across a thin air gap between two prisms) is the direct optical analogue of quantum tunnelling: if the gap width is ≲x0\lesssim x_0≲x0​, the evanescent field on the far side has non-negligible amplitude and drives a propagating wave in the second prism. Transmission decays exponentially with gap width - exactly as quantum tunnelling probability decays with barrier width. This analogy is exploited in frustrated-TIR couplers, optical tunnelling microscopy, and TIRF (total internal reflection fluorescence) microscopy.

Q82In wave optics, fibre modes arise from interference conditions. Explain qualitatively why only certain modes are allowed.
3 marksShow Answer ▾

Consider a ray bouncing between the two walls of a planar waveguide (a simplified fibre model) at angle θ\thetaθ to the axis. Each reflection at the core-cladding interface introduces a phase shift ϕr\phi_rϕr​ (from the Fresnel TIR phase), and the transverse round trip (wall to wall and back) traverses a path 2acos⁡θ2a\cos\theta2acosθ, accumulating phase k⊥⋅2a=2πnfλ⋅2acos⁡θk_\perp \cdot 2a = \frac{2\pi n_f}{\lambda}\cdot2a\cos\thetak⊥​⋅2a=λ2πnf​​⋅2acosθ.

Standing-wave condition: For a self-consistent mode, the total phase accumulated on one transverse round trip must equal an integer multiple of 2π2\pi2π: 4πnfacos⁡θλ−2ϕr=2πm,m=0,1,2,…\frac{4\pi n_f a\cos\theta}{\lambda} - 2\phi_r = 2\pi m, \quad m = 0, 1, 2, \ldotsλ4πnf​acosθ​−2ϕr​=2πm,m=0,1,2,… Only discrete values of θ\thetaθ satisfy this - each value of mmm corresponds to one allowed mode (LP0m_{0m}0m​, LP1m_{1m}1m​, etc.). For a given VVV-number, only a finite number of values of mmm give θ>θc\theta > \theta_cθ>θc​, so only finitely many modes are guided. As the core shrinks toward the wavelength, fewer and fewer integers mmm satisfy the condition - ultimately only m=0m=0m=0 is retained: single-mode operation.

Q83What is the LP01_{01}01​ mode? Why is single-mode operation desirable for telecommunications?
2 marksShow Answer ▾

The LP01_{01}01​ mode (linearly polarized, zero azimuthal index, first radial mode) is the fundamental guided mode of a step-index fibre. It has a Gaussian-like transverse intensity profile (maximum on axis, decaying radially), no nodal lines, and a single polarization direction. It is the only mode supported below the LP11_{11}11​ cutoff at V=2.405V = 2.405V=2.405.

Advantages for telecommunications: In a multimode fibre, different modes travel at different group velocities - their arrival time difference is the modal dispersion Δt=nf(nf−nc)l/(cnc)\Delta t = n_f(n_f-n_c)l/(cn_c)Δt=nf​(nf​−nc​)l/(cnc​), which broadens pulses and limits bandwidth to ≲\lesssim≲1 GHz·km. Single-mode fibre eliminates modal dispersion entirely, leaving only material and waveguide dispersion (∼17 ps nm−1 km−1\sim17\,\text{ps\,nm}^{-1}\,\text{km}^{-1}∼17psnm−1km−1 at 1550 nm in SMF-28). This enables bandwidths of ∼10 THz⋅km\sim10\,\text{THz·km}∼10THz⋅km - supporting terabit-per-second WDM systems over transoceanic distances.

Q84A fibre has NA=0.20=0.20=0.20. A 2 mm collimated beam must be focused to match a 10 μm mode field diameter. What focal length is needed?
2 marksShow Answer ▾

Diffraction-limited spot diameter for a lens: dspot=4λfπD≈λfD/2d_{\text{spot}} = \frac{4\lambda f}{\pi D} \approx \frac{\lambda f}{D/2}dspot​=πD4λf​≈D/2λf​. Setting dspot=10 μmd_{\text{spot}} = 10\,\mu\text{m}dspot​=10μm and using λ≈1550 nm\lambda \approx 1550\,\text{nm}λ≈1550nm: f=dspot⋅(D/2)λ=10�-10−6�-1�-10−31550�-10−9=10−81.55�-10−6=6.5 mmf = \frac{d_{\text{spot}}\cdot(D/2)}{\lambda} = \frac{10\times10^{-6}\times1\times10^{-3}}{1550\times10^{-9}} = \frac{10^{-8}}{1.55\times10^{-6}} = \boxed{6.5\,\text{mm}}f=λdspot​⋅(D/2)​=1550�-10−910�-10−6�-1�-10−3​=1.55�-10−610−8​=6.5mm​

(Alternatively from the NA condition: f=(D/2)/NA=1 mm/0.20=5 mmf = (D/2)/\text{NA} = 1\,\text{mm}/0.20 = 5\,\text{mm}f=(D/2)/NA=1mm/0.20=5mm. Both approaches give similar answers; the NA condition is the appropriate constraint for fibre coupling.)

Topic 7

Loss and Dispersion in Fibres

Q85Define attenuation in decibels. A 50 km fibre has loss 0.2 dB/km. (a) Total attenuation. (b) Fraction of power remaining.
3 marksShow Answer ▾

Attenuation in dB: Atten (dB)=10log⁡10(Pin/Pout)\text{Atten (dB)} = 10\log_{10}(P_{\text{in}}/P_{\text{out}})Atten (dB)=10log10​(Pin​/Pout​). A positive dB value means loss; 10 dB = factor-of-10 power reduction; 3 dB ≈ factor of 2.

(a) Total attenuation =0.2 dB/km�-50 km=10 dB= 0.2\,\text{dB/km}\times50\,\text{km} = \boxed{10\,\text{dB}}=0.2dB/km�-50km=10dB​

(b) 10=10log⁡10(Pin/Pout)⇒Pout/Pin=10−1=10%10 = 10\log_{10}(P_\text{in}/P_\text{out}) \Rightarrow P_\text{out}/P_\text{in} = 10^{-1} = \boxed{10\%}10=10log10​(Pin​/Pout​)⇒Pout​/Pin​=10−1=10%​

Only 10% of input power remains after 50 km - yet this is considered excellent fibre performance. 0.2 dB/km at 1550 nm is near the Rayleigh scattering limit for silica glass. In the 1970s, fibre loss exceeded 1000 dB/km; the reduction to 0.2 dB/km by Corning in 1970 (using MCVD) made long-haul fibre communications feasible.

Q86List and explain four mechanisms that cause loss in optical fibres.
4 marksShow Answer ▾
  • Rayleigh scattering: Random density and composition fluctuations frozen in during glass solidification scatter light with cross-section ∝1/λ4\propto 1/\lambda^4∝1/λ4. This is the dominant loss mechanism at wavelengths below ∼1.6 μm\sim1.6\,\mu\text{m}∼1.6μm and sets the fundamental limit. At 1550 nm: ∼0.14 dB/km\sim0.14\,\text{dB/km}∼0.14dB/km. Cannot be reduced without using different materials.
  • IR absorption (multiphonon): The Si-O bond vibrational modes absorb light at λ>2 μm\lambda > 2\,\mu\text{m}λ>2μm. The tail of this absorption extends into the near-IR, contributing an exponentially rising loss background for λ>1.6 μm\lambda > 1.6\,\mu\text{m}λ>1.6μm. This sets the long-wavelength edge of the transmission window.
  • OH (water) absorption: O-H bond stretching modes produce sharp absorption peaks at 0.95, 1.23, and 1.39 μm. Even trace concentrations (ppb\text{ppb}ppb) of water dramatically increase loss. The 1.39 μm peak falls between the 1310 nm and 1550 nm telecommunications windows. Modern "low-water-peak" fibres (ITU-T G.652.D) achieve <0.4 dB/km<0.4\,\text{dB/km}<0.4dB/km even at 1383 nm, enabling the full E-band (1360–1460 nm).
  • Macrobending and microbending: Sharp bends (R below critical bend radius) cause TIR failure (Q74). Random microbends from cabling and temperature cycling couple guided modes to radiation modes, leaking power. Both are extrinsic (packaging/installation dependent) and can be minimised with careful cable design and bend-insensitive fibre profiles.
Q87Rayleigh scattering ∝1/λ4\propto 1/\lambda^4∝1/λ4. How many times more intense is scattering of blue (450 nm) vs red (700 nm) light?
2 marksShow Answer ▾
IblueIred=(λredλblue)4=(700450)4=(1.556)4=5.85\frac{I_{\text{blue}}}{I_{\text{red}}} = \left(\frac{\lambda_{\text{red}}}{\lambda_{\text{blue}}}\right)^4 = \left(\frac{700}{450}\right)^4 = (1.556)^4 = \boxed{5.85}Ired​Iblue​​=(λblue​λred​​)4=(450700​)4=(1.556)4=5.85​

Blue light is scattered approximately 6�- more strongly than red light by Rayleigh scattering. This is why the sky appears blue (scattered sunlight) and sunsets appear red (transmitted light after the blue has been scattered away). For optical fibre, it means that shorter-wavelength (800 nm) systems suffer far higher Rayleigh losses than 1310 nm or 1550 nm systems - a major driver for the shift to longer-wavelength windows in telecommunications history.

Q88State the three telecommunications windows in silica fibre. Why are 1310 nm and 1550 nm preferred?
3 marksShow Answer ▾

The three fibre transmission windows are centred near 850 nm, 1310 nm, and 1550 nm, corresponding to local loss minima between OH absorption peaks and Rayleigh/IR absorption tails.

  • 1310 nm: Loss ≈0.35 dB/km\approx0.35\,\text{dB/km}≈0.35dB/km. Most importantly, this is the zero-dispersion wavelength of standard silica fibre - the material dispersion and waveguide dispersion cancel, giving near-zero total chromatic dispersion. Pulses can propagate with minimal spreading even from multiwavelength sources. Used in metro networks and LAN applications.
  • 1550 nm: Loss ≈0.2 dB/km\approx0.2\,\text{dB/km}≈0.2dB/km - the absolute minimum for silica, near the Rayleigh limit. Longer amplifier spacing (up to 80–100 km between EDFAs) enables transoceanic links. Dispersion is non-zero (+17 ps/nm/km) but manageable with DSP or DCF. The C-band (1530–1565 nm) supports 80+ WDM channels of 50 GHz spacing in the EDFA gain window.
  • 850 nm: Used for short-range multimode links (data centres, campus networks) because of low-cost VCSEL sources and Si photodiodes. Loss ≈3 dB/km\approx3\,\text{dB/km}≈3dB/km limits range to ∼300 m\sim300\,\text{m}∼300m.
Q89Explain modal dispersion. Why does it limit bandwidth in multimode fibres?
3 marksShow Answer ▾

In a multimode fibre, hundreds of guided modes propagate simultaneously, each at a different angle θ\thetaθ to the fibre axis. The axial ray (θ=0\theta=0θ=0) travels the shortest geometric path of length lll in time tax=nfl/ct_{\text{ax}} = n_f l/ctax​=nf​l/c. The highest-order guided ray travels at angle θc\theta_cθc​ to the axis, tracing a longer zig-zag path of total length nfl/ncn_f l/n_cnf​l/nc​ in time tmax=nf2l/(ncc)t_{\text{max}} = n_f^2 l/(n_c c)tmax​=nf2​l/(nc​c). The differential arrival time is: Δt=tmax−tax=nf(nf−nc)ncc⋅l\Delta t = t_{\text{max}} - t_{\text{ax}} = \frac{n_f(n_f-n_c)}{n_c c}\cdot lΔt=tmax​−tax​=nc​cnf​(nf​−nc​)​⋅l

A short input pulse spreads in time by Δt\Delta tΔt after propagation distance lll. If Δt\Delta tΔt exceeds the bit period T=1/BT = 1/BT=1/B, adjacent pulses overlap - inter-symbol interference (ISI) - and the bit stream is unreadable. The bandwidth-distance product is therefore B⋅l≲1/(2Δt/l)B\cdot l \lesssim 1/(2\Delta t/l)B⋅l≲1/(2Δt/l). For a typical step-index multimode fibre (nf=1.50n_f=1.50nf​=1.50, nc=1.48n_c=1.48nc​=1.48): Δt/l≈67 ns/km\Delta t/l \approx 67\,\text{ns/km}Δt/l≈67ns/km, giving B⋅l≈7 MHz⋅kmB\cdot l \approx 7\,\text{MHz·km}B⋅l≈7MHz⋅km - completely inadequate for modern communications. Graded-index multimode fibre (parabolic profile) reduces Δt/l\Delta t/lΔt/l to ∼1 ns/km\sim1\,\text{ns/km}∼1ns/km by equalising mode velocities.

Q90A multimode fibre has nf=1.50n_f=1.50nf​=1.50, nc=1.48n_c=1.48nc​=1.48. Calculate pulse spreading per km using Δt/l=nf(nf−nc)/(cnc)\Delta t/l = n_f(n_f-n_c)/(cn_c)Δt/l=nf​(nf​−nc​)/(cnc​).
3 marksShow Answer ▾
Δtl=nf(nf−nc)cnc=1.50�-(1.50−1.48)3�-108�-1.48=1.50�-0.024.44�-108=0.0304.44�-108=6.76�-10−11 s/m\frac{\Delta t}{l} = \frac{n_f(n_f-n_c)}{cn_c} = \frac{1.50\times(1.50-1.48)}{3\times10^8\times1.48} = \frac{1.50\times0.02}{4.44\times10^8} = \frac{0.030}{4.44\times10^8} = 6.76\times10^{-11}\,\text{s/m}lΔt​=cnc​nf​(nf​−nc​)​=3�-108�-1.481.50�-(1.50−1.48)​=4.44�-1081.50�-0.02​=4.44�-1080.030​=6.76�-10−11s/m =67.6 ns/km= \boxed{67.6\,\text{ns/km}}=67.6ns/km​

At 10 Gb/s, the bit period is 100 ps. The step-index multimode fibre would cause ISI after only 100 ps/67.6 ns/km≈1.5 m100\,\text{ps}/67.6\,\text{ns/km} \approx 1.5\,\text{m}100ps/67.6ns/km≈1.5m - making it completely useless for even the shortest 10G links. This stark number explains why graded-index MMF or single-mode fibre are mandatory for any data rate above ∼1 Gb/s\sim1\,\text{Gb/s}∼1Gb/s.

Q91What is material dispersion? Write the formula for pulse broadening and define DMD_MDM​.
3 marksShow Answer ▾

Material dispersion arises because the refractive index n(λ)n(\lambda)n(λ) of glass varies with wavelength (a consequence of the Lorentz oscillator model, Q24). Different wavelength components of a pulse have different group velocities vg=c/(n−λ dn/dλ)v_g = c/(n - \lambda\,dn/d\lambda)vg​=c/(n−λdn/dλ) and therefore arrive at the far end of a fibre at slightly different times.

The material dispersion coefficient is defined as: DM=−λcd2ndλ2[ps nm−1 km−1]D_M = -\frac{\lambda}{c}\frac{d^2n}{d\lambda^2} \quad [\text{ps\,nm}^{-1}\,\text{km}^{-1}]DM​=−cλ​dλ2d2n​[psnm−1km−1]

Pulse broadening due to material dispersion: Δtmat=DM⋅Δλ⋅l\Delta t_{\text{mat}} = D_M\cdot\Delta\lambda\cdot lΔtmat​=DM​⋅Δλ⋅l

where Δλ\Delta\lambdaΔλ is the spectral bandwidth of the source (nm) and lll is the fibre length (km). For standard silica: DM=0D_M = 0DM​=0 at λ≈1310 nm\lambda\approx1310\,\text{nm}λ≈1310nm (zero-dispersion wavelength) and DM≈+17 ps nm−1 km−1D_M \approx +17\,\text{ps\,nm}^{-1}\,\text{km}^{-1}DM​≈+17psnm−1km−1 at 1550 nm. A DFB laser with Δλ=0.01 nm\Delta\lambda = 0.01\,\text{nm}Δλ=0.01nm over 100 km gives Δt=17�-0.01�-100=17 ps\Delta t = 17\times0.01\times100 = 17\,\text{ps}Δt=17�-0.01�-100=17ps - small enough for 10 Gb/s (100 ps bit period) but critical at 100 Gb/s (10 ps).

Q92A single-mode fibre has DM=17 ps nm−1 km−1D_M = 17\,\text{ps\,nm}^{-1}\,\text{km}^{-1}DM​=17psnm−1km−1 at 1550 nm. A DFB laser has Δλ=0.1 nm\Delta\lambda = 0.1\,\text{nm}Δλ=0.1nm. Maximum fibre length before broadening exceeds one bit period at 10 Gb/s?
3 marksShow Answer ▾

Bit period at 10 Gb/s: T=1/R=1/1010=100 psT = 1/R = 1/10^{10} = 100\,\text{ps}T=1/R=1/1010=100ps.

Setting Δt=DM⋅Δλ⋅l=T\Delta t = D_M\cdot\Delta\lambda\cdot l = TΔt=DM​⋅Δλ⋅l=T: l=TDM⋅Δλ=100 ps17 ps nm−1 km−1�-0.1 nm=1001.7=58.8 kml = \frac{T}{D_M\cdot\Delta\lambda} = \frac{100\,\text{ps}}{17\,\text{ps\,nm}^{-1}\,\text{km}^{-1}\times0.1\,\text{nm}} = \frac{100}{1.7} = \boxed{58.8\,\text{km}}l=DM​⋅ΔλT​=17psnm−1km−1�-0.1nm100ps​=1.7100​=58.8km​

In practice, a tighter criterion of Δt<T/4≈25 ps\Delta t < T/4 \approx 25\,\text{ps}Δt<T/4≈25ps is used, giving l≈14.7 kml \approx 14.7\,\text{km}l≈14.7km - dispersion compensation modules (DCF with D≈−85 ps nm−1 km−1D\approx-85\,\text{ps\,nm}^{-1}\,\text{km}^{-1}D≈−85psnm−1km−1) are then inserted every ∼80 km\sim80\,\text{km}∼80km to compensate accumulated dispersion in long-haul links.

Q93Explain waveguide dispersion. Why is it significant near the material dispersion zero?
2 marksShow Answer ▾

Waveguide dispersion arises from the geometry of the waveguide itself: the propagation constant β(ω)\beta(\omega)β(ω) of a guided mode depends on frequency not only through n(ω)n(\omega)n(ω) but also through the normalised frequency V(ω)∝ωV(\omega) \propto \omegaV(ω)∝ω. As ω\omegaω changes, the fraction of the mode's power in the core vs. cladding changes - and since the core and cladding have slightly different refractive indices, the effective group velocity changes. This is an additional contribution to chromatic dispersion, independent of material properties.

Near the zero-material-dispersion wavelength (1310 nm for standard silica), DMD_MDM​ passes through zero but DWD_WDW​ (waveguide dispersion, typically −5-5−5 to −10 ps nm−1 km−1-10\,\text{ps\,nm}^{-1}\,\text{km}^{-1}−10psnm−1km−1) remains non-zero. The total dispersion D=DM+DWD = D_M + D_WD=DM​+DW​ therefore has its zero at a wavelength slightly shifted from the material dispersion zero. Dispersion-shifted fibre (DSF) exploits this by engineering the waveguide profile (typically using a triangular or W-profile) to make DWD_WDW​ sufficiently negative to shift the total zero from 1310 nm to 1550 nm - placing both minimum loss and zero dispersion at the same wavelength.

Q94A fibre has 3 dB/km at 0.8 μm, 0.3 dB/km at 1.3 μm, 0.2 dB/km at 1.55 μm. Compare how far a signal can travel to −10 dB at each wavelength.
3 marksShow Answer ▾

Distance =10 dB/loss rate= 10\,\text{dB} / \text{loss rate}=10dB/loss rate:

  • 0.8 μm: d=10/3=3.3 kmd = 10/3 = \boxed{3.3\,\text{km}}d=10/3=3.3km​
  • 1.3 μm: d=10/0.3=33.3 kmd = 10/0.3 = \boxed{33.3\,\text{km}}d=10/0.3=33.3km​
  • 1.55 μm: d=10/0.2=50 kmd = 10/0.2 = \boxed{50\,\text{km}}d=10/0.2=50km​

The 1.55 μm window provides 15�- greater reach than 0.8 μm. This huge advantage drove the transition from first-generation 850 nm systems (1970s) to second-generation 1310 nm (1980s) to third-generation 1550 nm with EDFAs (1990s–present). The factor of 10�- reduction in loss from 0.8 to 1.55 μm directly translates into 10�- fewer amplifiers/repeaters in long-haul systems - a massive cost and reliability advantage.

Q95Why must impurity concentrations (especially water/OH groups) be extremely low in communication fibres?
2 marksShow Answer ▾

OH bond stretching modes produce sharp absorption peaks at 0.95, 1.23, and 1.39 μm. The 1.39 μm peak falls between the 1310 nm and 1550 nm telecommunications windows, and its wings extend into both bands. Even a concentration of 1 part per billion (ppb) of OH in the glass adds ∼\sim∼1 dB/km of excess loss at 1383 nm. The target for premium fibre is OH concentration <0.1 ppb<0.1\,\text{ppb}<0.1ppb, achieved by using anhydrous (moisture-free) precursor gases (SiCl4_44​, GeCl4_44​) and carefully desiccated reaction atmospheres in MCVD. Transition metal impurities (Fe, Cr, Cu, Ni) absorb across the visible and near-IR - concentrations must be below 1 ppb to avoid measurable loss. Modern MCVD silica achieves purity levels unobtainable by any other glass-making process.

Q96The bandwidth of a 1 km fibre is limited to ∼\sim∼1000 GHz due to dispersion. At what distance would this limit reduce to 1 GHz? [Bandwidth ∝1/length\propto 1/\text{length}∝1/length]
2 marksShow Answer ▾

Since B⋅l=constB\cdot l = \text{const}B⋅l=const: B1l1=B2l2⇒l2=B1l1B2=1000 GHz�-1 km1 GHz=1000 kmB_1 l_1 = B_2 l_2 \Rightarrow l_2 = \frac{B_1 l_1}{B_2} = \frac{1000\,\text{GHz}\times1\,\text{km}}{1\,\text{GHz}} = \boxed{1000\,\text{km}}B1​l1​=B2​l2​⇒l2​=B2​B1​l1​​=1GHz1000GHz�-1km​=1000km​

After 1000 km, dispersion has accumulated enough to reduce the bandwidth to 1 GHz - barely sufficient for voice telephony. This is why dispersion-compensating fibre, coherent detection with digital signal processing, and dispersion-managed links were essential for transoceanic systems.

Topic 8

Fibre Production and Applications

Q97Explain why glass's viscosity-temperature behaviour is essential for making optical fibres.
3 marksShow Answer ▾

Unlike crystalline materials (which undergo a sharp solid-to-liquid transition at a single melting point), glass is an amorphous solid that softens gradually over a wide temperature range. This behaviour is characterised by a dramatically varying viscosity: from ∼1012 Pa⋅s\sim10^{12}\,\text{Pa·s}∼1012Pa⋅s (rigid solid) near the strain point, through ∼107 Pa⋅s\sim10^7\,\text{Pa·s}∼107Pa⋅s (honey-like) near the softening point, to ∼103 Pa⋅s\sim10^3\,\text{Pa·s}∼103Pa⋅s (drawing viscosity) at ∼2000°C\sim2000°\text{C}∼2000°C.

This gradual viscosity change is essential because:

  • Simultaneous drawing of core and cladding: Both glasses soften at similar temperatures; surface tension and viscous flow allow them to stretch without mixing (since diffusion is negligible at the drawing timescale).
  • Smooth cylindrical surface: Surface tension at the drawing viscosity creates an optically smooth <<<1 nm RMS surface - no polishing needed.
  • Precise diameter control: The viscous neck-down region is long enough for the feedback control loop (laser diameter gauge + capstan speed) to respond, maintaining 125±0.4 μm125\pm0.4\,\mu\text{m}125±0.4μm.
  • Preservation of index profile: The viscous state allows the preform's refractive index profile to be faithfully replicated in the drawn fibre, as the relative proportions of core/cladding are fixed by volume conservation.
Q98Describe the MCVD process for making a fibre preform.
4 marksShow Answer ▾

MCVD (Modified Chemical Vapour Deposition) is the industry-standard process for manufacturing low-loss telecommunications fibre preforms:

1Substrate: A pure silica glass tube (outer diameter ∼20 mm\sim20\,\text{mm}∼20mm) is mounted in a lathe and rotated at ∼60 rpm\sim60\,\text{rpm}∼60rpm.
2Gas flow: Precursor gases - SiCl4_44​ (silica) plus dopants such as GeCl4_44​ (GeO2_22​, raises nnn) or BCl3_33​/PF3_33​ (fluorine/boron, lowers nnn) - are carried by O2_22​ through the rotating tube.
3Reaction: An external oxy-hydrogen torch traverses the tube at ∼120 mm/min\sim120\,\text{mm/min}∼120mm/min, heating it to ∼1700°C\sim1700°\text{C}∼1700°C. The oxidation reaction: SiCl4+O2→SiO2+2Cl2\text{SiCl}_4 + \text{O}_2 \to \text{SiO}_2 + 2\text{Cl}_2SiCl4​+O2​→SiO2​+2Cl2​ deposits a thin layer of fine soot inside the tube, which vitrifies to a clear glassy layer immediately in the hot zone.
4Index profiling: The GeO2_22​ fraction in the gas mixture is varied layer by layer (typically 50–100 traversals) to build up the designed refractive index profile - graded-index, step-index, or more exotic profiles.
5Collapse: After all layers are deposited, the tube is heated to its softening point (∼2000°C\sim2000°\text{C}∼2000°C), and surface tension collapses it into a solid glass rod - the preform (∼10 mm\sim10\,\text{mm}∼10mm diameter, ∼1 m\sim1\,\text{m}∼1m long).

The central index dip (Q102) is a normal MCVD artefact. One preform typically yields ∼100 km\sim100\,\text{km}∼100km of fibre.

Q99What is a draw tower? What parameter controls fibre diameter, and to what precision can it be maintained?
3 marksShow Answer ▾

A draw tower is a vertical structure (typically 5–10 m tall) used to draw the preform into optical fibre. The preform is lowered into a graphite resistance furnace at the top, where the tip softens to ∼2000°C\sim2000°\text{C}∼2000°C. Gravity and a capstan (motorised winding drum at the base) pull the softened glass into a thin fibre. The fibre passes through an ultraviolet-cured acrylate coating die that applies a 250 μm diameter protective polymer jacket, then winds onto a drum at speeds of 10–100 m/min.

Diameter control: A laser micrometer measures the fibre diameter ∼0.5 m\sim0.5\,\text{m}∼0.5m below the furnace and sends a feedback signal to the capstan motor. Capstan (pulling) speed is the primary control variable: faster winding stretches the glass necked-down region more thinly, reducing diameter; slower winding gives a thicker fibre. Volume conservation: Apreform⋅vpreform=Afibre⋅vfibreA_{\text{preform}}\cdot v_{\text{preform}} = A_{\text{fibre}}\cdot v_{\text{fibre}}Apreform​⋅vpreform​=Afibre​⋅vfibre​. Maintained to: 125±0.4 μm\boxed{125\pm0.4\,\mu\text{m}}125±0.4μm​ - a relative precision of 0.3%.

Q100Compare electronic repeaters and EDFAs as signal regenerators. Why have EDFAs largely replaced electronic repeaters?
4 marksShow Answer ▾

Electronic repeaters operate by detecting each optical pulse with a photodiode, reshaping and retiming the electronic signal, then retransmitting it from a new laser. They are optoelectronic devices that regenerate the signal in 3R fashion (re-amplify, re-shape, re-time). Limitations: (1) Each repeater handles only one channel - WDM systems with 80 channels need 80 parallel repeaters per span, adding enormous complexity and unreliability. (2) Electronics are rate-limited - upgrading line rate requires replacing all repeaters. (3) Reliability in submarine cables is critical; more components = more failure points.

EDFAs (Erbium-Doped Fibre Amplifiers) amplify light directly in the optical domain. A silica fibre doped with Er3+^{3+}3+ ions (at ∼\sim∼1000 ppm) is pumped by a 980 nm or 1480 nm diode laser, creating population inversion between the 4I13/2^4\text{I}_{13/2}4I13/2​ and 4I15/2^4\text{I}_{15/2}4I15/2​ levels of Er3+^{3+}3+. Stimulated emission at 1550 nm amplifies the signal. Advantages: (1) All-optical - no conversion; simple, reliable (just a few metres of doped fibre + pump). (2) Broadband: EDFA gain bandwidth ∼35 nm\sim35\,\text{nm}∼35nm covers the entire C-band; one EDFA amplifies all 80 WDM channels simultaneously. (3) Transparent to bit-rate and modulation format - upgrade the terminal equipment, not the amplifiers. (4) Gain ∼20–40 dB\sim20\text{–}40\,\text{dB}∼20–40dB, noise figure ∼4–6 dB\sim4\text{–}6\,\text{dB}∼4–6dB. EDFAs enabled the WDM revolution of the 1990s and transoceanic links with amplifiers every 50–80 km and no electronic repeaters for >5000 km>5000\,\text{km}>5000km.

Q101The first transatlantic optical fibre cable (TAT-8) used 4 fibres. What was its capacity, and how did this compare to all previous copper cables?
2 marksShow Answer ▾

TAT-8 (1988) carried 40,000 simultaneous telephone calls on 4 optical fibres. This single cable exceeded the combined capacity of all 7 previous transatlantic copper cables together - a collective capacity representing decades of infrastructure investment. TAT-8 was a watershed demonstration of fibre's revolutionary bandwidth advantage: copper cables are limited by skin effect, bandwidth �- distance product, and impedance mismatches, while optical fibre's capacity scales with WDM channel count and bit rate. By 2000, a single fibre pair using WDM carried >1 Tb/s>1\,\text{Tb/s}>1Tb/s - 25,000�- TAT-8's capacity per fibre.

Q102Explain the "hole" in the centre of the refractive index profile of an MCVD fibre.
2 marksShow Answer ▾

During the final MCVD collapse step, the tube is heated to its softening point (∼2000°C\sim2000°\text{C}∼2000°C). At this temperature, GeO2_22​ (the dopant responsible for raising the core index) has a significantly higher vapour pressure than SiO2_22​. The innermost surface of the deposited core glass is directly exposed to the hot gas stream and partially evaporates - GeO2_22​ "boils off" before the tube closes. The result is a reduced dopant concentration at the very centre of the preform, giving a narrow central dip in the refractive index profile. This artefact shifts the fundamental mode profile slightly away from Gaussian but has negligible effect on attenuation. It can be minimised by over-pressurising the tube during collapse (to suppress evaporation) or by using an overclad (external deposition) process that avoids the problem entirely.

Q103Why are optical fibres impervious to EMI, and why does this make them useful in industrial environments?
2 marksShow Answer ▾

Optical fibres transmit photons, not electrons. Since glass is a dielectric, no free charges are present - the fibre cannot support electrical currents or voltages induced by external electromagnetic fields. Maxwell's equations for optical frequencies in pure silica are completely unaffected by external static or low-frequency EM fields, regardless of their amplitude. Lightning strikes, motor transients, railway traction currents, MRI magnets, and power-line hum have zero effect on the guided optical signal.

This immunity makes fibres ideal for: (1) Industrial process control near motors, welding equipment, or high-voltage lines. (2) Railway signalling where induction from traction currents would corrupt metallic cables. (3) Medical equipment near MRI or X-ray machines. (4) Military applications requiring emission security (no electromagnetic side-channel). (5) Hazardous environments (chemical plants, mines) where electrical cables pose sparking risks.

Q104A fibre is drawn from a 10 mm preform at 50 m/min to 125 μm diameter. Estimate the preform feed rate. [Volume conservation: Apreform⋅vpreform=Afibre⋅vfibreA_{\text{preform}}\cdot v_{\text{preform}} = A_{\text{fibre}}\cdot v_{\text{fibre}}Apreform​⋅vpreform​=Afibre​⋅vfibre​]
3 marksShow Answer ▾
vpreform=vfibre⋅AfibreApreform=vfibre⋅(dfdp)2=50 m/min�-(125�-10−610�-10−3)2v_{\text{preform}} = v_{\text{fibre}}\cdot\frac{A_{\text{fibre}}}{A_{\text{preform}}} = v_{\text{fibre}}\cdot\left(\frac{d_f}{d_p}\right)^2 = 50\,\text{m/min}\times\left(\frac{125\times10^{-6}}{10\times10^{-3}}\right)^2vpreform​=vfibre​⋅Apreform​Afibre​​=vfibre​⋅(dp​df​​)2=50m/min�-(10�-10−3125�-10−6​)2 =50�-(0.0125)2=50�-1.5625�-10−4=7.81�-10−3 m/min=7.8 mm/min= 50\times(0.0125)^2 = 50\times1.5625\times10^{-4} = 7.81\times10^{-3}\,\text{m/min} = \boxed{7.8\,\text{mm/min}}=50�-(0.0125)2=50�-1.5625�-10−4=7.81�-10−3m/min=7.8mm/min​

The preform descends at only 7.8 mm/min while the fibre is drawn at 50 m/min - a ratio of 6400:1 in speed, corresponding to a 80:1 ratio in linear dimension (and 6400:1 in cross-sectional area), consistent with (dp/df)2=(80)2=6400(d_p/d_f)^2 = (80)^2 = 6400(dp​/df​)2=(80)2=6400. A 1 m preform produces ∼1000�-50=50 km\sim1000\times50 = 50\,\text{km}∼1000�-50=50km of fibre (1000 mm / 7.8 mm/min �- 50 m/min).

Topic 9

Introduction to Quantum Optics

Q105What is the second-order correlation function g(2)(τ)g^{(2)}(\tau)g(2)(τ)? Describe the experimental apparatus used to measure it.
4 marksShow Answer ▾

The second-order correlation function (Glauber's g(2)g^{(2)}g(2)) quantifies the probability of detecting a second photon at time t+τt+\taut+τ given that a first was detected at time ttt, normalised to uncorrelated arrivals: g(2)(τ)=⟨I(t)I(t+τ)⟩⟨I(t)⟩2=⟨a^†(t)a^†(t+τ)a^(t+τ)a^(t)⟩⟨a^†a^⟩2g^{(2)}(\tau) = \frac{\langle I(t)I(t+\tau)\rangle}{\langle I(t)\rangle^2} = \frac{\langle \hat{a}^\dagger(t)\hat{a}^\dagger(t+\tau)\hat{a}(t+\tau)\hat{a}(t)\rangle}{\langle\hat{a}^\dagger\hat{a}\rangle^2}g(2)(τ)=⟨I(t)⟩2⟨I(t)I(t+τ)⟩​=⟨a^†a^⟩2⟨a^†(t)a^†(t+τ)a^(t+τ)a^(t)⟩​ The quantum form (using field operators a^†,a^\hat{a}^\dagger, \hat{a}a^†,a^) handles photon-number states correctly. For any classical field, g(2)(0)≥1g^{(2)}(0) \geq 1g(2)(0)≥1; values below 1 are a uniquely non-classical signature of single-photon states.

Hanbury Brown–Twiss (HBT) apparatus: A 50:50 beamsplitter directs the incident light onto two single-photon counting detectors (e.g. SNSPDs or avalanche photodiodes). A time-to-digital converter (TDC) records the time interval between a detection event at detector 1 (start) and detector 2 (stop). Accumulating these time differences over many photon pairs builds a histogram g(2)(τ)g^{(2)}(\tau)g(2)(τ) vs. τ\tauτ. The value at τ=0\tau=0τ=0 reveals the photon statistics: bunching (g(2)(0)>1g^{(2)}(0)>1g(2)(0)>1) for thermal light, Poissonian (g(2)(0)=1g^{(2)}(0)=1g(2)(0)=1) for laser light, or antibunching (g(2)(0)=0g^{(2)}(0)=0g(2)(0)=0) for single-photon sources.

Q106Explain the physical significance of g(2)(0)g^{(2)}(0)g(2)(0) for: (a) thermal light, (b) coherent (laser) light, (c) single-photon Fock state ∣1⟩|1\rangle∣1⟩.
4 marksShow Answer ▾

(a) Thermal light: g(2)(0)=2g^{(2)}(0) = 2g(2)(0)=2. Photons arrive in bunches - they tend to come in pairs rather than uniformly spaced. This photon bunching reflects the bosonic statistics of thermal photons: the probability of finding two photons simultaneously is twice the Poisson expectation. It is quantified by the super-Poissonian photon-number variance ⟨(Δn)2⟩=nˉ(1+nˉ)>nˉ\langle(\Delta n)^2\rangle = \bar{n}(1+\bar{n}) > \bar{n}⟨(Δn)2⟩=nˉ(1+nˉ)>nˉ. Physically: in a thermal field, modes are occupied according to the Bose-Einstein distribution; there is an enhanced probability of multiple photons occupying the same mode (bosons preferring populated modes - stimulated emission in reverse).

(b) Coherent (laser) light: g(2)(0)=1g^{(2)}(0) = 1g(2)(0)=1. Photon arrivals are completely uncorrelated - Poissonian statistics: ⟨(Δn)2⟩=nˉ\langle(\Delta n)^2\rangle = \bar{n}⟨(Δn)2⟩=nˉ. No bunching or antibunching. This is the classical limit: a coherent state ∣α⟩|\alpha\rangle∣α⟩ is the quantum state closest to a classical electromagnetic wave, with no photon correlations beyond the shot noise floor.

(c) Fock state ∣1⟩|1\rangle∣1⟩: g(2)(0)=0g^{(2)}(0) = 0g(2)(0)=0. A perfect single-photon source. Once a photon is detected at τ=0\tau=0τ=0, the source has emitted it - there is no second photon. No two-photon coincidences at zero delay are possible. g(2)(0)=0g^{(2)}(0) = 0g(2)(0)=0 is a uniquely non-classical result: no classical wave can produce it. It is the gold-standard experimental signature of a true single-photon emitter - required for photonic quantum computing, QKD, and linear optical quantum gates.

Q107What is a squeezed state of light? Distinguish between phase-squeezed and amplitude-squeezed states.
3 marksShow Answer ▾

A squeezed state of light has reduced quantum noise (uncertainty) in one field quadrature below the standard quantum limit (vacuum noise level), at the expense of increased noise in the conjugate quadrature, consistent with the Heisenberg uncertainty relation: ΔX1⋅ΔX2≥14\Delta X_1\cdot\Delta X_2 \geq \frac{1}{4}ΔX1​⋅ΔX2​≥41​ where X1=(a^+a^†)/2X_1 = (\hat{a}+\hat{a}^\dagger)/2X1​=(a^+a^†)/2 (amplitude quadrature) and X2=(a^−a^†)/2iX_2 = (\hat{a}-\hat{a}^\dagger)/2iX2​=(a^−a^†)/2i (phase quadrature). In a coherent (vacuum) state, ΔX1=ΔX2=1/2\Delta X_1 = \Delta X_2 = 1/2ΔX1​=ΔX2​=1/2. A squeezed state has, say, ΔX1<1/2\Delta X_1 < 1/2ΔX1​<1/2 and ΔX2>1/2\Delta X_2 > 1/2ΔX2​>1/2.

Phase-squeezed light: Reduced phase noise (ΔX2<1/2\Delta X_2 < 1/2ΔX2​<1/2), increased amplitude noise. The uncertainty ellipse in phase space is squeezed along the phase direction. Ideal for phase-sensitive measurements such as gravitational wave detection (LIGO, Q112) and optical coherence tomography.

Amplitude-squeezed light: Reduced intensity noise (ΔX1<1/2\Delta X_1 < 1/2ΔX1​<1/2), increased phase noise. Sub-Poissonian photon statistics (g(2)(0)<1g^{(2)}(0) < 1g(2)(0)<1). Ideal for precision spectroscopy and absorption measurements where shot noise limits sensitivity. Produced by degenerate optical parametric amplification or by driving diode lasers below threshold.

Q108List four types of single-photon sources with one key advantage and one key limitation each.
4 marksShow Answer ▾
  • Trapped ions / single atoms: Advantage: near-perfect photon indistinguishability and purity (g(2)(0)→0g^{(2)}(0) \to 0g(2)(0)→0); longest coherence times of any system. Limitation: requires ultra-high vacuum, laser cooling infrastructure, and complex ion trapping apparatus - not scalable to chip platforms.
  • NV centres in diamond: Advantage: room-temperature operation; naturally isolated emitter in a rigid solid-state host; compatible with sensing and quantum memory applications. Limitation: phonon sideband emission reduces zero-phonon-line (ZPL) fraction to ∼3%\sim3\%∼3%; NV placement is stochastic; collection efficiency limited by diamond's high refractive index.
  • Semiconductor quantum dots (QDs): Advantage: high brightness, GHz emission rates, compatible with photonic integrated circuits and cavity QED; g(2)(0)<0.01g^{(2)}(0) < 0.01g(2)(0)<0.01 demonstrated. Limitation: phonon dephasing degrades indistinguishability at T>4 KT > 4\,\text{K}T>4K; requiring cryogenic operation; deterministic positioning remains challenging.
  • Spontaneous parametric down-conversion (SPDC): Advantage: intrinsically produces entangled photon pairs; room temperature, wavelength-flexible, widely deployed in quantum optics labs. Limitation: probabilistic emission - Poisson statistics mean multi-pair events occur with probability ∝p2\propto p^2∝p2; increasing pump power to increase rate raises multi-photon contamination, degrading g(2)(0)g^{(2)}(0)g(2)(0).
Q109Explain the operating principle of a superconducting nanowire single-photon detector (SNSPD). Why must it operate at ~2 K?
3 marksShow Answer ▾

Operating principle: An SNSPD consists of a meandering ultrathin (∼\sim∼5 nm) superconducting nanowire (typically NbN or WSi) patterned on a substrate. A DC bias current slightly below the critical current IcI_cIc​ is applied. When a photon is absorbed, it breaks Cooper pairs in a small region of the wire, creating a resistive "hotspot." The supercurrent flowing around the hotspot locally exceeds IcI_cIc​, turning a segment of the wire normal (resistive). A measurable voltage pulse appears across the wire. After the pulse, the hotspot cools within picoseconds and the wire returns to the superconducting state - ready for the next photon. Timing jitter is typically <30 ps<30\,\text{ps}<30ps, system detection efficiency >95%>95\%>95% at 1550 nm, and dark count rates <1 Hz<1\,\text{Hz}<1Hz.

Why 2 K? The superconducting gap energy Δ≈3.5 kBTc\Delta \approx 3.5\,k_B T_cΔ≈3.5kB​Tc​ for NbN (Tc≈10 KT_c \approx 10\,\text{K}Tc​≈10K) is ∼3 meV\sim3\,\text{meV}∼3meV. At operating temperatures T∼2–4 KT \sim 2\text{–}4\,\text{K}T∼2–4K, thermal fluctuations kT∼0.2 meVkT \sim 0.2\,\text{meV}kT∼0.2meV are well below Δ\DeltaΔ, so thermal excitations do not spontaneously break Cooper pairs and generate dark counts. A single 1550 nm photon carries energy hc/λ≈0.8 eVhc/\lambda \approx 0.8\,\text{eV}hc/λ≈0.8eV - enormously larger than kTkTkT but only a factor of ∼100\sim100∼100 above the gap energy, ensuring reliable single-photon detection. At higher temperatures (T>Tc/2T > T_c/2T>Tc​/2), thermal fluctuations cause excessive dark counts and reduce the critical current below the bias, causing detection failure.

Q110Why is simulating quantum systems on a classical computer exponentially hard? What did Feynman propose?
3 marksShow Answer ▾

The exponential barrier: A quantum system of nnn two-level particles (qubits) exists in a superposition of all 2n2^n2n basis states simultaneously. The full quantum state requires 2n2^n2n complex amplitudes to specify. For n=50n = 50n=50, this is 250≈10152^{50} \approx 10^{15}250≈1015 amplitudes - exceeding the RAM of any classical computer. For n=300n = 300n=300, the number of amplitudes (2300≈10902^{300} \approx 10^{90}2300≈1090) exceeds the number of atoms in the observable universe. Not only storage, but the time to evolve the state (simulate the quantum dynamics) also scales as 2n2^n2n. Classical simulation of quantum chemistry, condensed matter, and materials is therefore intractable for systems beyond ∼50\sim50∼50 qubits.

Feynman's proposal (1982): In his landmark paper "Simulating Physics with Computers," Feynman observed that a controllable quantum system - one whose Hamiltonian can be engineered - naturally evolves according to quantum mechanics without the exponential overhead. Using quantum systems to simulate other quantum systems sidesteps the classical barrier: a 50-qubit quantum simulator contains just enough natural quantum information to efficiently track a 50-qubit system. This was the founding insight for both quantum simulation and quantum computing. As Feynman put it: "Nature isn't classical, dammit, and if you want to make a simulation of nature, you'd better make it quantum mechanical."

Q111What is the no-cloning theorem? State it and explain why it is crucial for QKD.
3 marksShow Answer ▾

No-cloning theorem (Wootters and Zurek, 1982): It is impossible to create an exact copy of an arbitrary unknown quantum state. Formally: there exists no unitary operation UUU such that U∣ψ⟩∣0⟩=∣ψ⟩∣ψ⟩U|\psi\rangle|0\rangle = |\psi\rangle|\psi\rangleU∣ψ⟩∣0⟩=∣ψ⟩∣ψ⟩ for all states ∣ψ⟩|\psi\rangle∣ψ⟩. Proof: assume such UUU exists. Apply it to the superposition ∣ϕ⟩=(∣ψ⟩+∣χ⟩)/2|\phi\rangle = (|\psi\rangle + |\chi\rangle)/\sqrt{2}∣ϕ⟩=(∣ψ⟩+∣χ⟩)/2​. Linearity of quantum mechanics gives U∣ϕ⟩∣0⟩=(∣ψ⟩∣ψ⟩+∣χ⟩∣χ⟩)/2U|\phi\rangle|0\rangle = (|\psi\rangle|\psi\rangle + |\chi\rangle|\chi\rangle)/\sqrt{2}U∣ϕ⟩∣0⟩=(∣ψ⟩∣ψ⟩+∣χ⟩∣χ⟩)/2​, which is entangled - not ∣ϕ⟩∣ϕ⟩=(∣ψ⟩∣ψ⟩+∣ψ⟩∣χ⟩+∣χ⟩∣ψ⟩+∣χ⟩∣χ⟩)/2|\phi\rangle|\phi\rangle = (|\psi\rangle|\psi\rangle + |\psi\rangle|\chi\rangle + |\chi\rangle|\psi\rangle + |\chi\rangle|\chi\rangle)/2∣ϕ⟩∣ϕ⟩=(∣ψ⟩∣ψ⟩+∣ψ⟩∣χ⟩+∣χ⟩∣ψ⟩+∣χ⟩∣χ⟩)/2. Contradiction. ∎

Importance for QKD (e.g. BB84): Alice encodes key bits in the polarization states of single photons. An eavesdropper (Eve) must intercept and re-transmit the photons to extract key information. But she cannot copy an unknown quantum state: she must measure the photon (collapsing its state) and retransmit her best guess. If she measures in the wrong basis (50% probability), she prepares the wrong state, introducing detectable errors in Alice and Bob's key (the QBER rises to ∼25%\sim25\%∼25% for full eavesdropping, far above the threshold of ∼11%\sim11\%∼11%). The no-cloning theorem is therefore a physical law that makes eavesdropping detectable - it guarantees information-theoretic security.

Q112Explain how LIGO uses squeezed light to improve its sensitivity to gravitational waves.
3 marksShow Answer ▾

LIGO is a power-recycled Michelson interferometer: passing gravitational waves differentially stretch and compress the two 4 km arm lengths by ΔL∼10−18 m\Delta L \sim10^{-18}\,\text{m}ΔL∼10−18m - smaller than a proton. The readout is a differential phase Δϕ=4πΔL/λ\Delta\phi = 4\pi\Delta L/\lambdaΔϕ=4πΔL/λ. The fundamental sensitivity limit at high frequencies is shot noise - quantum uncertainty in photon arrival times sets a phase resolution Δϕshot=1/N\Delta\phi_{\text{shot}} = 1/\sqrt{N}Δϕshot​=1/N​ where NNN is the photon count rate. Increasing laser power PPP (hence N∝PN \propto PN∝P) reduces shot noise but increases radiation pressure noise ∝P\propto \sqrt{P}∝P​ at low frequencies - a quantum trade-off set by the Heisenberg uncertainty principle applied to the mirror's position and momentum.

Squeezed light injection: Phase-squeezed vacuum states (with ΔX2<1/2\Delta X_2 < 1/2ΔX2​<1/2) are injected into the dark output port of the interferometer. These states replace the ordinary vacuum fluctuations (shot noise) entering from the same port, reducing the phase noise in the signal quadrature below the standard quantum limit. Since 2019, LIGO uses frequency-dependent squeezing (via a 300 m filter cavity) to inject phase squeezing at high frequencies (reducing shot noise) and amplitude squeezing at low frequencies (reducing radiation pressure noise) simultaneously. This has improved LIGO's strain sensitivity by ∼3 dB\sim3\,\text{dB}∼3dB - equivalent to doubling the detector's arm length - demonstrating quantum optics as a practical tool in the world's most sensitive scientific instrument.

Q113What is photon bunching in thermal light, and what physical effect causes it?
2 marksShow Answer ▾

Photon bunching: Thermal photons tend to arrive in clusters rather than uniformly spaced - g(2)(0)=2g^{(2)}(0) = 2g(2)(0)=2. This means the probability of detecting two photons simultaneously is twice the uncorrelated expectation.

Cause - Bose-Einstein statistics: Photons are bosons. In a thermal field, each mode is in a mixture of Fock states with occupation probability P(n)∝nˉn/(1+nˉ)n+1P(n) \propto \bar{n}^n/(1+\bar{n})^{n+1}P(n)∝nˉn/(1+nˉ)n+1 - the geometric distribution. This has super-Poissonian variance: ⟨(Δn)2⟩=nˉ(1+nˉ)\langle(\Delta n)^2\rangle = \bar{n}(1+\bar{n})⟨(Δn)2⟩=nˉ(1+nˉ). The +nˉ2+\bar{n}^2+nˉ2 excess arises because bosons have an enhanced tendency to occupy already-populated modes - the same stimulated emission enhancement that makes lasers possible but here manifesting as statistical bunching. Equivalently: thermal photons are produced by many independent, randomly phased oscillators; the intensity I=∣Etotal∣2I = |E_{\text{total}}|^2I=∣Etotal​∣2 of their superposition fluctuates dramatically (thermal statistics), and wherever III is large there are simultaneously many photons - bunching.

Q114Describe two current open challenges in photonic quantum technologies.
2 marksShow Answer ▾
  • Scalable on-chip integration: Combining high-efficiency single-photon sources (QDs at 4 K), low-loss waveguide routing (SiN, SiO2_22​), fast reconfigurable switches, and SNSPDs (2 K) on a single chip with many (>100>100>100) optical modes and acceptable yield remains unsolved. The thermal mismatch between components at different operating temperatures and the need for cryogenic packaging of detectors and sources simultaneously are fundamental engineering barriers.
  • Deterministic photon-photon entangling gates: Linear optics can implement two-qubit gates only non-deterministically (success probability p<1p < 1p<1 per gate). Scaling to fault-tolerant quantum computation requires either dramatically boosting ppp via ancilla photons and fast feedforward, or finding a physical two-photon nonlinear interaction strong enough to implement a deterministic CZ gate. No photonic medium currently provides the χ(3)\chi^{(3)}χ(3) nonlinearity needed for single-photon level switching; strong-coupling cavity QED approaches remain experimentally demanding.
Advanced Challenge Questions
H1 – H20

Advanced Challenge Questions

H1Derive the Einstein relation A/B=8πhν3/c3A/B = 8\pi h\nu^3/c^3A/B=8πhν3/c3 from Planck's blackbody energy density and Boltzmann's population ratio. Show every step.
8 marksShow Answer ▾
1Rate equations at equilibrium: At thermal equilibrium dN2/dt=0dN_2/dt = 0dN2​/dt=0: N1Bρ−N2A−N2Bρ=0N_1 B\rho - N_2 A - N_2 B\rho = 0N1​Bρ−N2​A−N2​Bρ=0
2Solve for ρ\rhoρ: Bρ(N1−N2)=N2A⇒ρ=AB⋅1N1/N2−1B\rho(N_1 - N_2) = N_2 A \Rightarrow \rho = \frac{A}{B}\cdot\frac{1}{N_1/N_2 - 1}Bρ(N1​−N2​)=N2​A⇒ρ=BA​⋅N1​/N2​−11​
3Boltzmann ratio: N2/N1=e−hν/kTN_2/N_1 = e^{-h\nu/kT}N2​/N1​=e−hν/kT, so N1/N2=e+hν/kTN_1/N_2 = e^{+h\nu/kT}N1​/N2​=e+hν/kT. Hence: ρ=AB⋅1ehν/kT−1\rho = \frac{A}{B}\cdot\frac{1}{e^{h\nu/kT}-1}ρ=BA​⋅ehν/kT−11​
4Match to Planck: ρ(ν)=8πhν3c3⋅1ehν/kT−1\rho(\nu) = \frac{8\pi h\nu^3}{c^3}\cdot\frac{1}{e^{h\nu/kT}-1}ρ(ν)=c38πhν3​⋅ehν/kT−11​. Comparing coefficients: AB=8πhν3c3■\boxed{\frac{A}{B} = \frac{8\pi h\nu^3}{c^3}}\quad\blacksquareBA​=c38πhν3​​■

Physical interpretation: A∝ν3A \propto \nu^3A∝ν3 means spontaneous emission grows enormously with frequency. For X-ray frequencies (ν∼1018 Hz\nu\sim10^{18}\,\text{Hz}ν∼1018Hz), A/BA/BA/B is ∼109�-\sim10^9\times∼109�- larger than at visible frequencies - making X-ray lasers extraordinarily difficult. The ν3\nu^3ν3 factor arises from the electromagnetic mode density Nν=8πν2/c3N_\nu = 8\pi\nu^2/c^3Nν​=8πν2/c3 times the photon energy hνh\nuhν: more modes and higher energy photons give more channels for spontaneous decay. Einstein derived this in 1917 as a remarkably elegant thermodynamic consistency argument - before the full machinery of quantum electrodynamics was available.

H2A step-index fibre has nf=1.480n_f=1.480nf​=1.480, nc=1.460n_c=1.460nc​=1.460. (a) NA. (b) VVV at 850 nm, a=3 μma=3\,\mu\text{m}a=3μm - single-mode? (c) Max aaa for SM at 850 nm. (d) Wavelength at which fibre becomes multimode.
8 marksShow Answer ▾

(a) NA=1.4802−1.4602=2.1904−2.1316=0.0588=0.2425\text{NA}=\sqrt{1.480^2-1.460^2}=\sqrt{2.1904-2.1316}=\sqrt{0.0588}=\boxed{0.2425}NA=1.4802−1.4602​=2.1904−2.1316​=0.0588​=0.2425​

(b) V=2π�-3�-10−6�-0.2425850�-10−9=4.571�-10−68.5�-10−7=5.38V=\frac{2\pi\times3\times10^{-6}\times0.2425}{850\times10^{-9}}=\frac{4.571\times10^{-6}}{8.5\times10^{-7}}=\boxed{5.38}V=850�-10−92π�-3�-10−6�-0.2425​=8.5�-10−74.571�-10−6​=5.38​. Since V=5.38>2.405V=5.38>2.405V=5.38>2.405, the fibre is multimode.

(c) a<2.405λ2π⋅NA=2.405�-850�-10−92π�-0.2425=2.044�-10−61.524=1.34 μma < \frac{2.405\lambda}{2\pi\cdot\text{NA}}=\frac{2.405\times850\times10^{-9}}{2\pi\times0.2425}=\frac{2.044\times10^{-6}}{1.524}=\boxed{1.34\,\mu\text{m}}a<2π⋅NA2.405λ​=2π�-0.24252.405�-850�-10−9​=1.5242.044�-10−6​=1.34μm​

(d) Single-mode when V=2πa⋅NA/λ<2.405V=2\pi a\cdot\text{NA}/\lambda < 2.405V=2πa⋅NA/λ<2.405: λ>2π�-3�-10−6�-0.24252.405=4.571�-10−62.405=1.90�-10−6 m=1900 nm\lambda > \frac{2\pi\times3\times10^{-6}\times0.2425}{2.405} = \frac{4.571\times10^{-6}}{2.405} = 1.90\times10^{-6}\,\text{m} = \boxed{1900\,\text{nm}}λ>2.4052π�-3�-10−6�-0.2425​=2.4054.571�-10−6​=1.90�-10−6m=1900nm​

Below 1900 nm, the a=3 μma=3\,\mu\text{m}a=3μm fibre supports multiple modes. This illustrates the trade-off: a larger core eases coupling but restricts single-mode operation to longer wavelengths.

H3A laser cavity: L=30 cmL=30\,\text{cm}L=30cm, R1=1.0R_1=1.0R1​=1.0, R2=0.95R_2=0.95R2​=0.95, intracavity loss Lrt=3%L_{\text{rt}}=3\%Lrt​=3%, gain medium lg=8 cml_g=8\,\text{cm}lg​=8cm. (a) Round-trip threshold condition. (b) Derive and calculate gthg_{\text{th}}gth​. (c) How does gthg_{\text{th}}gth​ change if R2=0.85R_2=0.85R2​=0.85?
7 marksShow Answer ▾

(a) Round-trip threshold: R1⋅R2⋅(1−Lrt)⋅e2gthlg=1R_1\cdot R_2\cdot(1-L_{\text{rt}})\cdot e^{2g_{\text{th}}l_g} = 1R1​⋅R2​⋅(1−Lrt​)⋅e2gth​lg​=1

(b) With R1=1.0R_1=1.0R1​=1.0, R2=0.95R_2=0.95R2​=0.95, Lrt=0.03L_{\text{rt}}=0.03Lrt​=0.03, lg=0.08 ml_g=0.08\,\text{m}lg​=0.08m: 0.95�-0.97�-e0.16 gth=1⇒e0.16 gth=10.9215=1.08520.95\times0.97\times e^{0.16\,g_{\text{th}}} = 1 \Rightarrow e^{0.16\,g_{\text{th}}} = \frac{1}{0.9215} = 1.08520.95�-0.97�-e0.16gth​=1⇒e0.16gth​=0.92151​=1.0852 gth=ln⁡(1.0852)0.16=0.08170.16=51.1 m−1g_{\text{th}} = \frac{\ln(1.0852)}{0.16} = \frac{0.0817}{0.16} = \boxed{51.1\,\text{m}^{-1}}gth​=0.16ln(1.0852)​=0.160.0817​=51.1m−1​

(c) With R2=0.85R_2=0.85R2​=0.85: 0.85�-0.97�-e0.16 gth=1⇒gth=ln⁡(1/0.8245)0.16=0.19320.16=120.8 m−10.85\times0.97\times e^{0.16\,g_{\text{th}}} = 1 \Rightarrow g_{\text{th}} = \frac{\ln(1/0.8245)}{0.16} = \frac{0.1932}{0.16} = \boxed{120.8\,\text{m}^{-1}}0.85�-0.97�-e0.16gth​=1⇒gth​=0.16ln(1/0.8245)​=0.160.1932​=120.8m−1​

Lower output coupler reflectivity (higher output coupling) more than doubles the threshold gain coefficient. This is the key design trade-off: higher output coupling extracts more power per pass but demands a higher-gain medium to sustain oscillation. In practice, the optimal R2R_2R2​ maximises output power - the Rigrod analysis gives the optimum coupling that balances threshold penalty against output extraction efficiency.

H4Unpolarized I0I_0I0​ on glass (n=1.65n=1.65n=1.65) at Brewster's angle. (a) θB\theta_BθB​ and θt\theta_tθt​. (b) Calculate R⊥R_\perpR⊥​. (c) Fraction of total incident intensity reflected. (d) Degree of polarization of reflected beam.
7 marksShow Answer ▾

(a) θB=arctan⁡(1.65)=58.8°\theta_B = \arctan(1.65) = 58.8°θB​=arctan(1.65)=58.8°; θt=90°−58.8°=31.2°\theta_t = 90°-58.8° = 31.2°θt​=90°−58.8°=31.2°

(b) R⊥=[sin⁡(θB−θt)sin⁡(θB+θt)]2=[sin⁡(27.6°)sin⁡(90°)]2=(0.4633)2=21.5%R_\perp = \left[\frac{\sin(\theta_B-\theta_t)}{\sin(\theta_B+\theta_t)}\right]^2 = \left[\frac{\sin(27.6°)}{\sin(90°)}\right]^2 = (0.4633)^2 = \boxed{21.5\%}R⊥​=[sin(θB​+θt​)sin(θB​−θt​)​]2=[sin(90°)sin(27.6°)​]2=(0.4633)2=21.5%​

(c) Unpolarized light = 50% ⊥\perp⊥ + 50% ∥\parallel∥. R∥=0R_\parallel = 0R∥​=0 at Brewster's angle. Reflected intensity: IreflI0=12R⊥+12�-0=12�-0.215=10.7%\frac{I_{\text{refl}}}{I_0} = \tfrac{1}{2}R_\perp + \tfrac{1}{2}\times0 = \tfrac{1}{2}\times0.215 = \boxed{10.7\%}I0​Irefl​​=21​R⊥​+21​�-0=21​�-0.215=10.7%​

(d) The reflected beam contains only ⊥\perp⊥ polarization (all ∥\parallel∥ transmitted). Degree of polarization: P=I⊥−I∥I⊥+I∥=I⊥−0I⊥+0=1.0=100% polarizedP = \frac{I_\perp - I_\parallel}{I_\perp + I_\parallel} = \frac{I_\perp - 0}{I_\perp + 0} = \boxed{1.0 = 100\%\text{ polarized}}P=I⊥​+I∥​I⊥​−I∥​​=I⊥​+0I⊥​−0​=1.0=100% polarized​

H5Derive Δt/l=nf(nf−nc)/(cnc)\Delta t/l = n_f(n_f-n_c)/(cn_c)Δt/l=nf​(nf​−nc​)/(cnc​) from geometry. For nf=1.50n_f=1.50nf​=1.50, nc=1.48n_c=1.48nc​=1.48: (a) Δt/l\Delta t/lΔt/l, (b) maximum bandwidth BBB, (c) max length for B=100 Mb/sB=100\,\text{Mb/s}B=100Mb/s.
7 marksShow Answer ▾
1Axial ray (fastest): path length lll, travel time tax=nfl/ct_{\text{ax}} = n_f l/ctax​=nf​l/c
2Marginal ray at critical angle to axis: hits wall at θc\theta_cθc​, travels zig-zag path of length Lmarg=l/sin⁡θc=l/(nc/nf)=nfl/ncL_{\text{marg}} = l/\sin\theta_c = l/(n_c/n_f) = n_f l/n_cLmarg​=l/sinθc​=l/(nc​/nf​)=nf​l/nc​. Travel time: tmarg=nf⋅(nfl/nc)/c=nf2l/(cnc)t_{\text{marg}} = n_f\cdot(n_f l/n_c)/c = n_f^2 l/(cn_c)tmarg​=nf​⋅(nf​l/nc​)/c=nf2​l/(cnc​)
3Δt=tmarg−tax=nf2lcnc−nflc=nflc(nfnc−1)=nf(nf−nc)cnc⋅l■\Delta t = t_{\text{marg}} - t_{\text{ax}} = \frac{n_f^2 l}{cn_c} - \frac{n_f l}{c} = \frac{n_f l}{c}\left(\frac{n_f}{n_c}-1\right) = \frac{n_f(n_f-n_c)}{cn_c}\cdot l\quad\blacksquareΔt=tmarg​−tax​=cnc​nf2​l​−cnf​l​=cnf​l​(nc​nf​​−1)=cnc​nf​(nf​−nc​)​⋅l■

(a) Δt/l=1.50�-0.023�-108�-1.48=0.0304.44�-108=67.6 ns/km\Delta t/l = \frac{1.50\times0.02}{3\times10^8\times1.48} = \frac{0.030}{4.44\times10^8} = \boxed{67.6\,\text{ns/km}}Δt/l=3�-108�-1.481.50�-0.02​=4.44�-1080.030​=67.6ns/km​

(b) B≈1/(2Δt/l)=1/(2�-67.6�-10−9)=7.4 MHz⋅kmB \approx 1/(2\Delta t/l) = 1/(2\times67.6\times10^{-9}) = \boxed{7.4\,\text{MHz·km}}B≈1/(2Δt/l)=1/(2�-67.6�-10−9)=7.4MHz⋅km​

(c) lmax=7.4 MHz⋅km/100 Mb/s=0.074 km=74 ml_{\text{max}} = 7.4\,\text{MHz·km}/100\,\text{Mb/s} = 0.074\,\text{km} = \boxed{74\,\text{m}}lmax​=7.4MHz⋅km/100Mb/s=0.074km=74m​. Step-index multimode fibre cannot support 100 Mb/s beyond 74 m - completely impractical for data communications.

H6DSF fibre, D=3.5 ps nm−1 km−1D=3.5\,\text{ps\,nm}^{-1}\,\text{km}^{-1}D=3.5psnm−1km−1 at 1550 nm. 40 Gb/s WDM, 40 channels, 100 GHz spacing. (a) Convert spacing to nm. (b) Walk-off after 80 km. (c) Max length for walk-off <<< half bit period. (d) Two engineering solutions.
8 marksShow Answer ▾

(a) Δλ=λ2Δf/c=(1550�-10−9)2�-1011/(3�-108)=0.80 nm\Delta\lambda = \lambda^2\Delta f/c = (1550\times10^{-9})^2\times10^{11}/(3\times10^8) = \boxed{0.80\,\text{nm}}Δλ=λ2Δf/c=(1550�-10−9)2�-1011/(3�-108)=0.80nm​. 40 channels span 39 spacings: Δλtotal=39�-0.80=31.2 nm\Delta\lambda_{\text{total}} = 39\times0.80 = 31.2\,\text{nm}Δλtotal​=39�-0.80=31.2nm

(b) Walk-off=D�-Δλtotal�-l=3.5�-31.2�-80=8736 ps=8.74 ns\text{Walk-off} = D\times\Delta\lambda_{\text{total}}\times l = 3.5\times31.2\times80 = \boxed{8736\,\text{ps} = 8.74\,\text{ns}}Walk-off=D�-Δλtotal​�-l=3.5�-31.2�-80=8736ps=8.74ns​

(c) Bit period at 40 Gb/s: T=25 psT = 25\,\text{ps}T=25ps. Half-period = 12.5 ps. lmax=12.53.5�-31.2=12.5109.2=0.114 km=114 ml_{\text{max}} = \frac{12.5}{3.5\times31.2} = \frac{12.5}{109.2} = \boxed{0.114\,\text{km} = 114\,\text{m}}lmax​=3.5�-31.212.5​=109.212.5​=0.114km=114m​

(d) Engineering solutions: (i) Dispersion compensation modules (DCMs) with large negative dispersion (DDCF≈−85 ps nm−1 km−1D_{\text{DCF}} \approx -85\,\text{ps\,nm}^{-1}\,\text{km}^{-1}DDCF​≈−85psnm−1km−1) inserted at each EDFA span to cancel accumulated chromatic dispersion. (ii) Coherent detection with digital back-propagation - the dispersion is deterministic and reversible; DSP in the receiver applies an inverse transfer function H(f)=e+iβ2ω2L/2H(f) = e^{+i\beta_2\omega^2 L/2}H(f)=e+iβ2​ω2L/2 to electronically compensate all accumulated dispersion in post-processing. (iii) Dispersion-managed transmission alternating positive and negative dispersion fibre sections to keep local dispersion low while maintaining zero average.

H7Show that in a two-level system the population difference saturates to ΔN=−NA/(A+2Bρ)\Delta N = -NA/(A+2B\rho)ΔN=−NA/(A+2Bρ). Hence show the gain saturates as k(ρ)=k0/(1+I/Isat)k(\rho) = k_0/(1+I/I_{\text{sat}})k(ρ)=k0​/(1+I/Isat​), identifying IsatI_{\text{sat}}Isat​.
7 marksShow Answer ▾
1Rate equation at steady state: N1Bρ=N2(A+Bρ)N_1 B\rho = N_2(A+B\rho)N1​Bρ=N2​(A+Bρ). With N1=N−N2N_1 = N-N_2N1​=N−N2​: N2=NBρA+2Bρ,N1=N(A+Bρ)A+2BρN_2 = \frac{NB\rho}{A+2B\rho},\quad N_1 = \frac{N(A+B\rho)}{A+2B\rho}N2​=A+2BρNBρ​,N1​=A+2BρN(A+Bρ)​
2ΔN=N2−N1=N(Bρ−A−Bρ)A+2Bρ=−NAA+2Bρ■\Delta N = N_2 - N_1 = \frac{N(B\rho - A - B\rho)}{A+2B\rho} = \frac{-NA}{A+2B\rho}\quad\blacksquareΔN=N2​−N1​=A+2BρN(Bρ−A−Bρ)​=A+2Bρ−NA​■
3Gain coefficient: k=(Bhν/c)ΔN=−BNhν/c⋅AA+2Bρk = (Bh\nu/c)\Delta N = \frac{-BNh\nu/c\cdot A}{A+2B\rho}k=(Bhν/c)ΔN=A+2Bρ−BNhν/c⋅A​. At low intensity (Bρ≪AB\rho\ll ABρ≪A): k0=−BNhν/ck_0 = -BNh\nu/ck0​=−BNhν/c (absorption ≡−α0\equiv -\alpha_0≡−α0​).
4k(ρ)=k0⋅AA+2Bρ=k01+2Bρ/A=k01+I/Isatk(\rho) = k_0\cdot\frac{A}{A+2B\rho} = \frac{k_0}{1+2B\rho/A} = \frac{k_0}{1+I/I_{\text{sat}}}k(ρ)=k0​⋅A+2BρA​=1+2Bρ/Ak0​​=1+I/Isat​k0​​
5Saturation intensity: Isat=Ac/(2B)I_{\text{sat}} = Ac/(2B)Isat​=Ac/(2B). At I=IsatI = I_{\text{sat}}I=Isat​, absorption is halved; at I≫IsatI \gg I_{\text{sat}}I≫Isat​, k→0k\to0k→0 (full transparency).
H8He-Ne laser: ΔνD=1.4 GHz\Delta\nu_D=1.4\,\text{GHz}ΔνD​=1.4GHz, L=40 cmL=40\,\text{cm}L=40cm, λ=632.8 nm\lambda=632.8\,\text{nm}λ=632.8nm. (a) Mode spacing and number of modes. (b) Why multimode here but single-mode Nd:YAG? (c) Coherence length of single mode (δν≈1 MHz\delta\nu\approx1\,\text{MHz}δν≈1MHz).
6 marksShow Answer ▾

(a) Δνmode=c/2L=3�-108/0.8=375 MHz\Delta\nu_{\text{mode}} = c/2L = 3\times10^8/0.8 = 375\,\text{MHz}Δνmode​=c/2L=3�-108/0.8=375MHz. Number of modes ≈1.4 GHz/375 MHz≈3–4 modes\approx 1.4\,\text{GHz}/375\,\text{MHz} \approx \boxed{3\text{–}4\,\text{modes}}≈1.4GHz/375MHz≈3–4modes​

(b) He-Ne - inhomogeneous (Doppler) broadening: Each velocity class vzv_zvz​ of Ne atoms is resonant at ν0(1±vz/c)\nu_0(1\pm v_z/c)ν0​(1±vz​/c) - a different frequency. Lasing at mode frequency ν1\nu_1ν1​ depletes only the velocity class near ν1\nu_1ν1​ (spectral hole burning). Other velocity classes have untouched inversions and can independently support additional modes. Multiple modes coexist. Nd:YAG - homogeneous (phonon) broadening: All Nd3+^{3+}3+ ions share the same broadened lineshape due to phonon interactions. Lasing at one mode depletes the inversion of all ions globally. No remaining gain for other frequencies. Single mode results (in principle).

(c) lc=c/δν=3�-108/106=300 ml_c = c/\delta\nu = 3\times10^8/10^6 = \boxed{300\,\text{m}}lc​=c/δν=3�-108/106=300m​. A 300 m coherence length is sufficient for precision interferometry including holography and laser ranging.

H9Pulse: λ0=1550 nm\lambda_0=1550\,\text{nm}λ0​=1550nm, Δλ=5 nm\Delta\lambda=5\,\text{nm}Δλ=5nm, through 200 km of SMF-28 (D=17 ps nm−1 km−1D=17\,\text{ps\,nm}^{-1}\,\text{km}^{-1}D=17psnm−1km−1). (a) Dispersion broadening. (b) Transform-limited initial duration. (c) Total broadened duration. (d) Maximum data rate.
7 marksShow Answer ▾
