(a) Δtdisp=D�-Δλ�-l=17�-5�-200=17,000 ps=17 ns\Delta t_{\text{disp}} = D\times\Delta\lambda\times l = 17\times5\times200 = \boxed{17{,}000\,\text{ps} = 17\,\text{ns}}Δtdisp​=D�-Δλ�-l=17�-5�-200=17,000ps=17ns​

(b) Δν=cΔλ/λ2=3�-108�-5�-10−9/(1550�-10−9)2=624 GHz\Delta\nu = c\Delta\lambda/\lambda^2 = 3\times10^8\times5\times10^{-9}/(1550\times10^{-9})^2 = 624\,\text{GHz}Δν=cΔλ/λ2=3�-108�-5�-10−9/(1550�-10−9)2=624GHz. Transform limit: Δt0=0.44/Δν=0.44/6.24�-1011=0.70 ps\Delta t_0 = 0.44/\Delta\nu = 0.44/6.24\times10^{11} = \boxed{0.70\,\text{ps}}Δt0​=0.44/Δν=0.44/6.24�-1011=0.70ps​

(c) Δttotal=(0.70 ps)2+(17 000 ps)2≈17 000 ps=17 ns\Delta t_{\text{total}} = \sqrt{(0.70\,\text{ps})^2+(17\,000\,\text{ps})^2} \approx \boxed{17\,000\,\text{ps} = 17\,\text{ns}}Δttotal​=(0.70ps)2+(17000ps)2​≈17000ps=17ns​ (completely dominated by dispersion)

(d) Bmax≈1/(2Δttotal)=1/(2�-17�-10−9)≈29 Mb/sB_{\text{max}} \approx 1/(2\Delta t_{\text{total}}) = 1/(2\times17\times10^{-9}) \approx \boxed{29\,\text{Mb/s}}Bmax​≈1/(2Δttotal​)=1/(2�-17�-10−9)≈29Mb/s​

A 5 nm source on 200 km of uncompensated SMF limits capacity to just 29 Mb/s - barely adequate for video streaming. This vividly demonstrates why narrow-linewidth (Δλ<0.1 nm\Delta\lambda < 0.1\,\text{nm}Δλ<0.1nm) DFB lasers and dispersion compensation are both mandatory for 10+ Gb/s long-haul systems.

H10State the plane-wave solution to Maxwell's equations, derive the relationship between phase velocity and nnn. Explain using the oscillator model why n>1n>1n>1 for visible light in glass (ω≪ω0\omega\ll\omega_0ω≪ω0​) and n<1n<1n<1 for X-rays (ω≫ω0\omega\gg\omega_0ω≫ω0​).
7 marksShow Answer ▾

Plane wave solution: E=E0 ei(kx−ωt)\mathbf{E} = \mathbf{E}_0\,e^{i(kx-\omega t)}E=E0​ei(kx−ωt) with k=nω/ck = n\omega/ck=nω/c. Phase velocity: vph=ω/k=c/nv_{\text{ph}} = \omega/k = c/nvph​=ω/k=c/n, hence n=c/vph\boxed{n = c/v_{\text{ph}}}n=c/vph​​.

Below resonance (ω≪ω0\omega \ll \omega_0ω≪ω0​, visible light in glass): The driving frequency is far below the natural frequency of the bound electron oscillator. The electron displacement is in phase with the driving field (positive susceptibility). The re-radiated field from the driven dipoles constructively reinforces a phase lag on the forward wave - the wave front is retarded, meaning vph<cv_{\text{ph}} < cvph​<c, hence n>1n > 1n>1. The susceptibility χ∝(ω02−ω2)−1>0\chi \propto (\omega_0^2-\omega^2)^{-1} > 0χ∝(ω02​−ω2)−1>0, giving n=1+χ>1n = \sqrt{1+\chi} > 1n=1+χ​>1. Normal dispersion: nnn increases toward ω0\omega_0ω0​ as the denominator decreases.

Above resonance (ω≫ω0\omega \gg \omega_0ω≫ω0​, X-rays): The driving frequency is so high that the bound electrons respond as if nearly free. The oscillator response flips phase (180°) above resonance - the driven displacement lags the field. The re-radiated field now advances the wave front rather than retarding it: vph>cv_{\text{ph}} > cvph​>c, hence n<1n < 1n<1. Explicitly: n≈1−Ne22mϵ0ω2=1−δ<1n \approx 1 - \frac{N e^2}{2m\epsilon_0\omega^2} = 1 - \delta < 1n≈1−2mϵ0​ω2Ne2​=1−δ<1 for ω≫ω0\omega \gg \omega_0ω≫ω0​, where δ∼10−5–10−6\delta \sim 10^{-5}\text{–}10^{-6}δ∼10−5–10−6 for X-rays. Note: vph>cv_{\text{ph}} > cvph​>c does not violate causality - the group velocity (at which information propagates) remains ≤c\leq c≤c.

H11In BB84 QKD: (a) Probability Bob gets the correct bit measuring in the wrong basis. (b) Show a full-intercept eavesdropper introduces 25% QBER. (c) If measured QBER = 11%, should Alice and Bob continue or abort? (d) Why can't Eve copy photons to avoid detection?
8 marksShow Answer ▾

(a) If Alice sends ∣H⟩|H\rangle∣H⟩ (rectilinear) and Bob measures in the diagonal basis, he gets ∣+⟩|{+}\rangle∣+⟩ or ∣−⟩|{-}\rangle∣−⟩ with equal probability 1/21/21/2. His assignment of bit value is random - P(correct bit)=1/2\boxed{P(\text{correct bit}) = 1/2}P(correct bit)=1/2​. These events are discarded during the sifting step.

(b) Eve intercepts every photon and measures in a randomly chosen basis. She chooses the correct basis 50% of the time (no error introduced) and wrong basis 50% of the time. When Eve chooses the wrong basis, she prepares a wrong state with probability 1/2 and sends it to Bob. Bob then gets the wrong answer with probability 1/2. QBER contribution: 50%�-50%=25%50\%\times50\% = 25\%50%�-50%=25%. Combined: QBER=25%\boxed{\text{QBER} = 25\%}QBER=25%​.

(c) QBER = 11\% is at the BB84 security threshold - near the maximum tolerable under intercept-resend attacks (∼11.0%\sim11.0\%∼11.0%). Given this borderline value, Alice and Bob should abort: the presence of eavesdropping or high channel noise cannot be excluded, and privacy amplification cannot reduce the leaked information to acceptable levels.

(d) The no-cloning theorem (Q111) forbids copying an unknown quantum state. Eve cannot copy each photon and measure the copy while forwarding the original. She must measure the photon to extract any information - which collapses the state. If she measures in the wrong basis, she prepares the wrong state, introducing the 25% QBER. Any physical interaction that could extract information inevitably disturbs the quantum state.

H12Derive Beer's law for high intensity (including stimulated emission), show I=I0ekxI = I_0 e^{kx}I=I0​ekx for a population inversion, and explain why gain is limited by saturation.
6 marksShow Answer ▾

Including both stimulated absorption (∝N1\propto N_1∝N1​) and stimulated emission (∝N2\propto N_2∝N2​) in the intensity evolution equation for a thin slice: dIdx=(N2−N1)Bhνc I=k I,k=Bhνc(N2−N1)\frac{dI}{dx} = (N_2 - N_1)\frac{Bh\nu}{c}\,I = k\,I, \quad k = \frac{Bh\nu}{c}(N_2-N_1)dxdI​=(N2​−N1​)cBhν​I=kI,k=cBhν​(N2​−N1​)

When N2>N1N_2 > N_1N2​>N1​: k>0k > 0k>0, integrating: I(x)=I0 ekx\boxed{I(x) = I_0\,e^{kx}}I(x)=I0​ekx​ - exponential amplification. The beam doubles in intensity over a gain length 1/k1/k1/k.

Saturation: From H7, as III increases, stimulated emission rapidly depletes N2N_2N2​ and repopulates N1N_1N1​, reducing ΔN=N2−N1\Delta N = N_2 - N_1ΔN=N2​−N1​ and hence kkk. At high intensity: k(I)=k0/(1+I/Isat)→0k(I) = k_0/(1+I/I_{\text{sat}}) \to 0k(I)=k0​/(1+I/Isat​)→0. The gain is self-limiting: an amplifier cannot deliver arbitrarily large output from a finite excited population. This is why optical amplifiers have output power saturation - pushing more signal into an EDFA eventually depletes the Er3+^{3+}3+ inversion and reduces gain.

H13Calculate the solar constant using: (a) Wien's law for T⊙=5778 KT_{\odot}=5778\,\text{K}T⊙​=5778K. (b) Stefan's law for solar surface flux. (c) Total luminosity from R⊙=6.96�-108 mR_{\odot}=6.96\times10^8\,\text{m}R⊙​=6.96�-108m. (d) Solar constant at d=1.496�-1011 md=1.496\times10^{11}\,\text{m}d=1.496�-1011m.
6 marksShow Answer ▾

(a) λmax⁡=2.898�-10−3/5778=501 nm\lambda_{\max} = 2.898\times10^{-3}/5778 = \boxed{501\,\text{nm}}λmax​=2.898�-10−3/5778=501nm​ - peak in green, consistent with our eyes having evolved to match sunlight.

(b) P/A=σT4=5.67�-10−8�-(5778)4=5.67�-10−8�-1.115�-1015=6.32�-107 W m−2P/A = \sigma T^4 = 5.67\times10^{-8}\times(5778)^4 = 5.67\times10^{-8}\times1.115\times10^{15} = 6.32\times10^7\,\text{W\,m}^{-2}P/A=σT4=5.67�-10−8�-(5778)4=5.67�-10−8�-1.115�-1015=6.32�-107Wm−2

(c) L⊙=4πR⊙2�-(P/A)=4π�-(6.96�-108)2�-6.32�-107=4π�-4.84�-1017�-6.32�-107=3.84�-1026 WL_\odot = 4\pi R_\odot^2\times(P/A) = 4\pi\times(6.96\times10^8)^2\times6.32\times10^7 = 4\pi\times4.84\times10^{17}\times6.32\times10^7 = \boxed{3.84\times10^{26}\,\text{W}}L⊙​=4πR⊙2​�-(P/A)=4π�-(6.96�-108)2�-6.32�-107=4π�-4.84�-1017�-6.32�-107=3.84�-1026W​

(d) Solar constant =L⊙/(4πd2)=3.84�-1026/(4π�-(1.496�-1011)2)=3.84�-1026/2.81�-1023=1366 W m−2= L_\odot/(4\pi d^2) = 3.84\times10^{26}/(4\pi\times(1.496\times10^{11})^2) = 3.84\times10^{26}/2.81\times10^{23} = \boxed{1366\,\text{W\,m}^{-2}}=L⊙​/(4πd2)=3.84�-1026/(4π�-(1.496�-1011)2)=3.84�-1026/2.81�-1023=1366Wm−2​ ✓ (matches measured value of 1361 W m−2^{-2}−2)

H14SPDC crystal pumped at λp=405 nm\lambda_p=405\,\text{nm}λp​=405nm, degenerate pairs. (a) Signal/idler wavelength. (b) Pair separation on screen 1.5 m away at ±3°\pm3°±3°. (c) Actual pair rate if heralding efficiency η=0.85\eta=0.85η=0.85 and 10610^6106 detected/s. (d) Why does higher pump power reduce purity?
6 marksShow Answer ▾

(a) Energy conservation: νs=νi=νp/2⇒λs=2λp=810 nm\nu_s = \nu_i = \nu_p/2 \Rightarrow \lambda_s = 2\lambda_p = \boxed{810\,\text{nm}}νs​=νi​=νp​/2⇒λs​=2λp​=810nm​

(b) Separation =2dtan⁡θ=2�-1.5�-tan⁡(3°)=3�-0.05241=0.157 m=15.7 cm= 2d\tan\theta = 2\times1.5\times\tan(3°) = 3\times0.05241 = \boxed{0.157\,\text{m} = 15.7\,\text{cm}}=2dtanθ=2�-1.5�-tan(3°)=3�-0.05241=0.157m=15.7cm​

(c) Actual pair rate =106/η=106/0.85=1.18�-106 pairs/s= 10^6/\eta = 10^6/0.85 = \boxed{1.18\times10^6\,\text{pairs/s}}=106/η=106/0.85=1.18�-106pairs/s​

(d) SPDC is a spontaneous process: each pump photon converts to a pair with small probability p≪1p \ll 1p≪1. The probability of generating two pairs simultaneously scales as p2p^2p2. At high pump power, ppp increases - multi-pair events become non-negligible relative to single-pair events. These multi-pair contributions create "2-photon" emissions that degrade the g(2)(0)g^{(2)}(0)g(2)(0) of the heralded single-photon source away from zero, reducing single-photon purity. The mean photon number per pulse μ=p\mu = pμ=p and purity scales as 1−μ1 - \mu1−μ for small μ\muμ - a fundamental trade-off between brightness and purity in probabilistic sources.

H15FP semiconductor laser: L=250 μmL=250\,\mu\text{m}L=250μm, n=3.5n=3.5n=3.5, R=0.31R=0.31R=0.31 (cleaved facets), αi=20 cm−1\alpha_i=20\,\text{cm}^{-1}αi​=20cm−1. (a) Optical round-trip path. (b) gthg_{\text{th}}gth​. (c) Δν\Delta\nuΔν and Δλ\Delta\lambdaΔλ. (d) Modes in 5 nm gain bandwidth at 850 nm.
7 marksShow Answer ▾

(a) 2nL=2�-3.5�-250�-10−6=1.75�-10−3 m=1.75 mm2nL = 2\times3.5\times250\times10^{-6} = \boxed{1.75\times10^{-3}\,\text{m} = 1.75\,\text{mm}}2nL=2�-3.5�-250�-10−6=1.75�-10−3m=1.75mm​

(b) Round-trip: R2e2(gth−αi)L=1R^2 e^{2(g_{\text{th}}-\alpha_i)L} = 1R2e2(gth​−αi​)L=1: e2(gth−2000)�-250�-10−6=1/R2=1/0.0961=10.40e^{2(g_{\text{th}}-2000)\times250\times10^{-6}} = 1/R^2 = 1/0.0961 = 10.40e2(gth​−2000)�-250�-10−6=1/R2=1/0.0961=10.40 2(gth−2000)�-2.5�-10−4=ln⁡(10.40)=2.3422(g_{\text{th}}-2000)\times2.5\times10^{-4} = \ln(10.40) = 2.3422(gth​−2000)�-2.5�-10−4=ln(10.40)=2.342 gth=2000+2.342/(5�-10−4)=2000+4684=6684 m−1=66.8 cm−1g_{\text{th}} = 2000 + 2.342/(5\times10^{-4}) = 2000 + 4684 = \boxed{6684\,\text{m}^{-1} = 66.8\,\text{cm}^{-1}}gth​=2000+2.342/(5�-10−4)=2000+4684=6684m−1=66.8cm−1​

(c) Δν=c/(2nL)=3�-108/1.75�-10−3=171 GHz\Delta\nu = c/(2nL) = 3\times10^8/1.75\times10^{-3} = \boxed{171\,\text{GHz}}Δν=c/(2nL)=3�-108/1.75�-10−3=171GHz​; Δλ=λ2/(2nL)=(850�-10−9)2/1.75�-10−3=0.41 nm\Delta\lambda = \lambda^2/(2nL) = (850\times10^{-9})^2/1.75\times10^{-3} = \boxed{0.41\,\text{nm}}Δλ=λ2/(2nL)=(850�-10−9)2/1.75�-10−3=0.41nm​

(d) Modes in 5 nm: 5/0.41≈12 modes5/0.41 \approx \boxed{12\,\text{modes}}5/0.41≈12modes​. A Fabry-Perot semiconductor laser is inherently multimode - a DFB (distributed feedback) grating is needed for single-mode operation.

H16Derive Snell's law using Fermat's principle. Set up the optical path length from A to B via a planar interface, minimise with respect to the crossing point, and show n1sin⁡θ1=n2sin⁡θ2n_1\sin\theta_1 = n_2\sin\theta_2n1​sinθ1​=n2​sinθ2​.
6 marksShow Answer ▾
1Geometry: A at height aaa in medium n1n_1n1​; B at depth bbb in medium n2n_2n2​; horizontal separation ddd. Ray crosses interface at xxx from below A. Path lengths: l1=a2+x2l_1 = \sqrt{a^2+x^2}l1​=a2+x2​, l2=b2+(d−x)2l_2 = \sqrt{b^2+(d-x)^2}l2​=b2+(d−x)2​
2Optical path length: L(x)=n1a2+x2+n2b2+(d−x)2\mathcal{L}(x) = n_1\sqrt{a^2+x^2} + n_2\sqrt{b^2+(d-x)^2}L(x)=n1​a2+x2​+n2​b2+(d−x)2​
3dLdx=n1xa2+x2−n2(d−x)b2+(d−x)2=0\frac{d\mathcal{L}}{dx} = \frac{n_1 x}{\sqrt{a^2+x^2}} - \frac{n_2(d-x)}{\sqrt{b^2+(d-x)^2}} = 0dxdL​=a2+x2​n1​x​−b2+(d−x)2​n2​(d−x)​=0
4Recognise: x/a2+x2=sin⁡θ1x/\sqrt{a^2+x^2} = \sin\theta_1x/a2+x2​=sinθ1​ and (d−x)/b2+(d−x)2=sin⁡θ2(d-x)/\sqrt{b^2+(d-x)^2} = \sin\theta_2(d−x)/b2+(d−x)2​=sinθ2​. Therefore: n1sin⁡θ1=n2sin⁡θ2■\boxed{n_1\sin\theta_1 = n_2\sin\theta_2}\quad\blacksquaren1​sinθ1​=n2​sinθ2​​■

This derivation - finding Snell's law from a stationarity principle - prefigures quantum electrodynamics (Feynman's path integral): the "ray" is the stationary-phase path of the quantum amplitude. Feynman shows in QED that partial reflections from glass can be understood entirely by summing complex amplitudes over all possible photon paths, with the observed ray emerging from constructive interference near the classical path.

H1780-channel C-band WDM, 50 GHz spacing, 100 Gb/s/channel, loss 0.2 dB/km, EDFAs every 80 km. (a) Total capacity. (b) Loss per span and EDFA gain. (c) OSNR after 10 spans. (d) Link viable at 15 dB required OSNR?
8 marksShow Answer ▾

(a) 80�-100 Gb/s=8 Tb/s80\times100\,\text{Gb/s} = \boxed{8\,\text{Tb/s}}80�-100Gb/s=8Tb/s​

(b) Loss =0.2�-80=16 dB= 0.2\times80 = \boxed{16\,\text{dB}}=0.2�-80=16dB​ per span. EDFA gain = 16 dB to compensate.

(c) Pin=0 dBm=1 mWP_{\text{in}} = 0\,\text{dBm} = 1\,\text{mW}Pin​=0dBm=1mW. ν=c/λ≈1.935�-1014 Hz\nu = c/\lambda \approx 1.935\times10^{14}\,\text{Hz}ν=c/λ≈1.935�-1014Hz. NF = 5 dB → 100.5=3.16210^{0.5} = 3.162100.5=3.162. S/N=10−310�-1.283�-10−19�-12.5�-109�-3.162=10−35.08�-10−8=1.97�-104S/N = \frac{10^{-3}}{10\times1.283\times10^{-19}\times12.5\times10^9\times3.162} = \frac{10^{-3}}{5.08\times10^{-8}} = 1.97\times10^4S/N=10�-1.283�-10−19�-12.5�-109�-3.16210−3​=5.08�-10−810−3​=1.97�-104 OSNR=10log⁡10(1.97�-104)=42.9 dB\text{OSNR} = 10\log_{10}(1.97\times10^4) = \boxed{42.9\,\text{dB}}OSNR=10log10​(1.97�-104)=42.9dB​

(d) 42.9 dB≫15 dB42.9\,\text{dB} \gg 15\,\text{dB}42.9dB≫15dB required. Link is viable with 27.9 dB of margin - in practice this margin is consumed by nonlinear impairments (self-phase modulation, XPM, FWM), PMD, and connector losses, which a full system simulation would account for.

H18Nd:YAG at 1064 nm, τ=230 μs\tau=230\,\mu\text{s}τ=230μs, lower level lifetime 30 ns. (a) A21A_{21}A21​. (b) Einstein BBB coefficient. (c) ρ\rhoρ where stimulated = spontaneous. (d) Peak power from N2=1018N_2=10^{18}N2​=1018 ions released in 10 ns.
7 marksShow Answer ▾

(a) A21=1/τ=1/(230�-10−6)=4.35�-103 s−1A_{21} = 1/\tau = 1/(230\times10^{-6}) = \boxed{4.35\times10^3\,\text{s}^{-1}}A21​=1/τ=1/(230�-10−6)=4.35�-103s−1​

(b) ν=c/λ=2.82�-1014 Hz\nu = c/\lambda = 2.82\times10^{14}\,\text{Hz}ν=c/λ=2.82�-1014Hz. A/B=8πhν3/c3=8π�-6.63�-10−34�-(2.82�-1014)3/(3�-108)3=1.384�-10−14A/B = 8\pi h\nu^3/c^3 = 8\pi\times6.63\times10^{-34}\times(2.82\times10^{14})^3/(3\times10^8)^3 = 1.384\times10^{-14}A/B=8πhν3/c3=8π�-6.63�-10−34�-(2.82�-1014)3/(3�-108)3=1.384�-10−14. B=A/(A/B)=4.35�-103/1.384�-10−14=3.14�-1017 m3 J−1 s−2B = A/(A/B) = 4.35\times10^3/1.384\times10^{-14} = \boxed{3.14\times10^{17}\,\text{m}^3\,\text{J}^{-1}\,\text{s}^{-2}}B=A/(A/B)=4.35�-103/1.384�-10−14=3.14�-1017m3J−1s−2​

(c) Stimulated = spontaneous when Bρ=AB\rho = ABρ=A: ρeq=A/B=1.38�-10−14 J m−3 Hz−1\rho_{\text{eq}} = A/B = \boxed{1.38\times10^{-14}\,\text{J\,m}^{-3}\,\text{Hz}^{-1}}ρeq​=A/B=1.38�-10−14Jm−3Hz−1​

(d) Energy per photon: Eγ=hc/λ=1.87�-10−19 JE_\gamma = hc/\lambda = 1.87\times10^{-19}\,\text{J}Eγ​=hc/λ=1.87�-10−19J. Stored energy: U=N2Eγ=1018�-1.87�-10−19=0.187 JU = N_2 E_\gamma = 10^{18}\times1.87\times10^{-19} = 0.187\,\text{J}U=N2​Eγ​=1018�-1.87�-10−19=0.187J. Peak power: P=U/Δt=0.187/10−8=18.7 MWP = U/\Delta t = 0.187/10^{-8} = \boxed{18.7\,\text{MW}}P=U/Δt=0.187/10−8=18.7MW​

18.7 MW from a crystal the size of a pencil eraser. Q-switched Nd:YAG pulses at these peak powers drive laser machining, laser-induced breakdown spectroscopy (LIBS), range-finding, and inertial confinement fusion targets.

H19Three polarizers at 0°, 45°, 90°. Unpolarized I0I_0I0​ incident. (a) Final transmitted intensity. (b) Remove the middle polarizer - final intensity. (c) Resolve the paradox.
5 marksShow Answer ▾

(a) After P1 (0°): I1=I0/2I_1 = I_0/2I1​=I0​/2. After P2 (45° from P1): I2=(I0/2)cos⁡2(45°)=I0/4I_2 = (I_0/2)\cos^2(45°) = I_0/4I2​=(I0​/2)cos2(45°)=I0​/4. After P3 (45° from P2): I3=(I0/4)cos⁡2(45°)=I0/8I_3 = (I_0/4)\cos^2(45°) = \boxed{I_0/8}I3​=(I0​/4)cos2(45°)=I0​/8​.

(b) Without the middle polarizer: after P1, light is polarized at 0°. P3 is at 90° - crossed polarizers. I=(I0/2)cos⁡2(90°)=0I = (I_0/2)\cos^2(90°) = \boxed{0}I=(I0​/2)cos2(90°)=0​.

(c) Resolution of the paradox: An intermediate polarizer does not merely "block" or "pass" light - it actively re-prepares the polarization state. After P1, the photons are in the state ∣0°⟩|0°\rangle∣0°⟩. P2 at 45° projects each photon onto ∣45°⟩|45°\rangle∣45°⟩ - measuring in a new basis and collapsing the quantum state. After P2, the photons are genuinely in state ∣45°⟩|45°\rangle∣45°⟩, not ∣0°⟩|0°\rangle∣0°⟩. P3 at 90° then sees photons arriving in state ∣45°⟩|45°\rangle∣45°⟩ and transmits cos⁡2(45°)=50%\cos^2(45°) = 50\%cos2(45°)=50% of them. Without P2, the photons arrive at P3 in state ∣0°⟩|0°\rangle∣0°⟩, and P3 (at 90°) transmits zero. Adding P2 injects new quantum information into the polarization state, enabling light to pass through what would otherwise be a perfect block - a striking demonstration that quantum measurement is not passive observation but physical state preparation.

H20KLM scheme for photonic quantum computing. (a) Why can't linear optics give deterministic two-qubit gates? (b) If single gate succeeds with p=0.25p=0.25p=0.25 and 10 sequential gates are needed, overall success probability? (c) Mean wait time per 1 ns attempt? (d) Two approaches to overcome non-determinism.
7 marksShow Answer ▾

(a) Photons interact via the electromagnetic field, but photon-photon scattering cross-sections at optical frequencies are negligibly small (σ∼10−66 m2\sigma \sim 10^{-66}\,\text{m}^2σ∼10−66m2 via virtual electron-positron pairs). Linear optical elements - beamsplitters, phase shifters, mirrors - implement only unitary transformations on single-photon modes, equivalent to single-qubit rotations. A two-qubit gate (CNOT, CZ) requires a conditional phase shift on one photon depending on the state of another - this demands a nonlinear interaction between distinct photons. In the absence of deterministic photon-photon nonlinearity, two-qubit gates must be implemented using ancilla photons and post-selection (measurement of the ancilla), succeeding only with probability p<1p < 1p<1.

(b) P(all 10 succeed)=(0.25)10=(1/4)10=4−10=1/1,048,576≈9.5�-10−7P(\text{all 10 succeed}) = (0.25)^{10} = (1/4)^{10} = 4^{-10} = 1/1{,}048{,}576 \approx \boxed{9.5\times10^{-7}}P(all 10 succeed)=(0.25)10=(1/4)10=4−10=1/1,048,576≈9.5�-10−7​

(c) Mean attempts needed =1/P≈1.05�-106= 1/P \approx 1.05\times10^6=1/P≈1.05�-106. Time per attempt = 1 ns. Mean wait=1.05�-106�-10−9 s=1.05 ms\text{Mean wait} = 1.05\times10^6\times10^{-9}\,\text{s} = \boxed{1.05\,\text{ms}}Mean wait=1.05�-106�-10−9s=1.05ms​

(d) Two leading approaches: (i) Measurement-based (cluster-state) quantum computing: Prepare a large entangled resource state ("cluster state") offline using non-deterministic gates and many attempts. Once prepared, computation proceeds via adaptive single-qubit measurements - all two-qubit entanglement is already embedded in the resource state. Gate non-determinism becomes a resource-preparation problem, not a real-time bottleneck. (ii) Temporal multiplexing with fast switching: Run many parallel copies of the gate attempt simultaneously in loop-based architectures; a high-speed optical switch routes the successful attempt forward. With mmm parallel attempts per clock cycle, the effective success probability approaches 1−(1−p)m1-(1-p)^m1−(1−p)m - boosted arbitrarily close to 1 with enough parallelism. Both approaches are actively pursued at companies including PsiQuantum and Xanadu.
